## SB 1.18.15 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 05-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna-katha, Qualified Speaker, Qualified Audience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ECsl2cFFhYs?t=51
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Subject matter of Krishna is not exactly relishable if the speaker is not a pure devotee and the audience is not exactly eager to hear about Krishna Katha."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of both a qualified speaker (a pure devotee of Krishna) and a qualified audience (eager to hear about Krishna) for a truly beneficial Krishna-katha discourse.

In this session, we will be discussing the 15th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, "O Suta Goswami, you are a learned and pure devotee of the Lord, because the personality of Godhead is your chief object of service. Therefore, please describe to us the pastimes of the Lord, which are above all material conception, for we are anxious to receive such messages." The Sages of Naimisharanya are speaking to Suta Goswami. Suta Goswami has narrated how Pariksit Maharaj received the age of Kali, he punished and rewarded both, and now the events leading to Pariksit Maharaj meeting Sukadeva Goswami will be described, but before that, the discussion is centered around the topics for discussion. What kind of topics? Not merely some history, but specifically topics connected with the supreme personality of Godhead, Krishna. So, Krishna-katha is the important topic for discussion. Shri La Prabhupada says in the purport, the speaker on the transcendental activities of the Lord should have only one object of worship and service, Lord Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead. So, it's very important from whom we hear, if at all we engage in hearing about Krishna Katha, then from whom do we hear? Who is the speaker? It's very important. So, Shri La Prabhupada is telling the speaker on the transcendental activities of the Lord, what kind of person should he be? He should be a person who has only one object of worship and service. That only object is Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead. That means, such a person should be a pure devotee of Krishna. That is the intended meaning here. And the audience for such topics should be anxious to hear about him. Him refers to Krishna. The speaker and the hearers. The speaker should be a pure devotee of Krishna. The hearers should be eager to hear about Krishna only. So, only when such a combination is possible, namely, a qualified speaker and a qualified audience, it is then and there very much congenial to continue discourses on the transcendence. Subject matter of Krishna is not exactly relishable if the speaker is not a pure devotee and the audience is not exactly eager to hear about Krishna Katha. Both the speaker and the hearers should be qualified that is being said here now.

---
### Professional Speakers, Material Motivation, Pure Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ECsl2cFFhYs?t=317
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Professional speakers have got a material intention, material benefit in their mind because of which they engage in speaking, so for them it is a means of earning money."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts pure devotees with professional speakers who are motivated by material gain, and highlights the importance of the audience's eagerness to hear about Krishna, as exemplified by the sages of Naimisharanya.

There are other kinds of speakers other than a pure devotee speaker, what are they called? They are called professional speakers. So, Srila Prabhupada writes about these professional speakers because this is elaborately explained in Chaitanya Charitamrita. So what does Prabhupada say here? Professional speakers and a materially absorbed audience cannot derive real benefit from such discourses. So who are these professional speakers? Professional speakers have got a material intention, material benefit in their mind because of which they engage in speaking, so for them it is a means of earning money, speaking on Bhagavatam is yet another means of earning money. There are so many ways people earn money in this world, but speaking Bhagavatam also has become in recent times a way of earning money, so such a speaker is called a professional speaker and the audience if they are materially engrossed then their purpose of hearing Bhagavatam is not at all the purpose for which one should actually hear Bhagavatam. Just like the sage Naimisharanya, their purpose of hearing Bhagavatam is exclusively hearing about Krishna, they are very eager to hear about Krishna, this is noted by Suta Goswami right in the beginning of the Bhagavatam itself in the first canto, Suta Goswami said I am noticing that all of you are very eager to hear about Krishna, therefore let me recite to you the Srimad Bhagavatam which I heard from Sukadeva Goswami.

---
### Bhagavata Saptaha, Imitation, Daily Hearing
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ECsl2cFFhYs?t=465
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They don't consider the fact that Bhagavatam says daily you must hear Bhagavatam. If you are serious about spiritual benefit. If you are serious about spiritual advancement by hearing Bhagavatam. You must hear Bhagavatam every day."
**Summary:** This segment criticizes the commercialization of Bhagavata Saptaha by professional speakers who imitate Sukadeva Goswami without understanding the specific context of Pariksit Maharaj's limited time, and emphasizes the importance of daily hearing of Bhagavatam for genuine spiritual progress.

How did Suta Goswami decide to speak on Bhagavatam? It is because the audience, the hearers, the sage Naimisharanya, they were very eager to hear about Krishna, so Suta Goswami said, he is very pleased to get an opportunity to speak in front of such an audience, so the materially absorbed audience, they are not exactly eager to hear about Krishna, so if either the speaker or the hearers or the audience, they are not eager to hear about Krishna, or they are not purely devotees or they are not persons on a pure devotional path, then there cannot be real spiritual benefit from such a discourse. Professional speakers make a show of Bhagavat Saptaha for the sake of family maintenance, this is exactly what is meant by professional speakers, they are trying to imitate Shukadeva Goswami. Shukadeva Goswami spoke Bhagavatam for one week. Saptaha. One week. Because Pariksit Maharaj had only one week's time before quitting his body. There is a very very specific reason why Shukadeva Goswami spoke for only one week. Because Pariksit Maharaj who was asking questions, inquiring from Shukadeva Goswami, he had only one week. That will be explained in this chapter as we go further. Therefore, Shukadeva Goswami spoke only for one week. It is not that Shukadeva Goswami, without a reason, he simply decided let me speak one week. No. So, the professional speakers whose main intention is money, they make a show of Bhagavata Saptaha. They try to imitate Shukadeva Goswami. They don't consider the fact that Bhagavatam says daily you must hear Bhagavatam. If you are serious about spiritual benefit. If you are serious about spiritual advancement by hearing Bhagavatam. You must hear Bhagavatam every day. It's called Seva. It's called Seva. It's a service. It's a devotional service to hear Bhagavatam. And Nityam means every day. Constantly. Always. At least for us who have got so many other engagements, we should at least hear every day for little time. Not just one week. No. That was only for Pariksit Maharaj, because he had only one week's time.

---
### Material Benefit, Contamination, Three Gunas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ECsl2cFFhYs?t=679
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything in this material world is contaminated by the three gunas. So, even the Bhagavatam discourses could be contaminated by the gunas."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how both professional speakers and their audiences often seek material benefits (Dharma, Artha, Kama, Moksha) from Bhagavata Saptaha, leading to contamination of the discourse by the three gunas, despite the inherent purity of the Bhagavatam itself.

So, these professional speakers make a show of Bhagavata Saptaha for the sake of family maintenance. The real intention is money. And the materially disposed audience hears such discourses of Bhagavata Saptaha for some material benefit. The audience also. Many times. They are really interested in material benefit by hearing Bhagavatam. And what is that material benefit? Religiosity, wealth, gratification of the senses or liberation. Dharma, Artha, Kama, Moksha. This is their aim. To attain one or more of these four things. Such a Bhagavatam discourse or such Bhagavatam discourses are not purified from the contamination of the material qualities. Always remember. Everything in this material world is contaminated by the three gunas. So, even the Bhagavatam discourses could be contaminated by the gunas. Bhagavatam by itself is pure, but it can get contaminated by such intent of earning money or getting material benefit. It gets polluted.

---
### Transcendental Pleasure, Material Motives, Spiritual Transformation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ECsl2cFFhYs?t=777
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only if there is no material motive, motive for material gain, and there is no money intention, intention of earning money by the speaker, then only the transcendent unlimited transcendental pleasure can be realized by or relished by both the audience and the speaker."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the contaminated discourses with the pure, transcendental exchanges between the saints of Naimisharanya and Suta Goswami, emphasizing that only when both speaker and audience are free from material motives can unlimited transcendental pleasure be relished and genuine spiritual transformation occur.

Here Prabhupada is warning us. But the discourses between the saints of Naimisharanya and Sri Suta Goswami are on the transcendental level, above all material contamination. They are pure. There is no motive for material gain. This is the important point. Neither the sages are interested in any material gain, nor Suta Goswami is interested in any material benefit. So, therefore the speaker and audience are both pure. In such discourses, transcendental pleasure, unlimited transcendental pleasure is relished both by the audience and by the speaker. And therefore they can continue the topics for many thousands of years. Why is Prabhupada saying this? Because if you remember, the beginning of the Bhagavatam it is said, the sages of Naimisharanya assembled in that holy place, performed one thousand year sacrifice. So, for one thousand years, what sacrifice are they doing? They are performing the sacrifice of hearing and chanting the glories of the Supreme Lord. So only if there is no material motive, motive for material gain, and there is no money intention, intention of earning money by the speaker, then only the transcendent unlimited transcendental pleasure can be realized by or relished by both the audience and the speaker. And then it can continue. There is no time limitation. Now Bhagavata Saptas are held for only seven days, and after finishing the show, both the audience and the speaker become engaged in material activities as usual. They go back to their usual material activities. There is no change of heart. There is no transformation. So, it is explained here, both the nowadays, both the speaker and the hearers are not exactly pure or qualified. So, there can't be that spiritual transformation in such a condition. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### liberation, devotion, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That should be the only intent. No other intent should be there."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 16th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Maharaj Parikshit's attainment of the Lord's lotus feet through knowledge imparted by Sukadeva Goswami, emphasizing the pure intention behind seeking shelter at the Lord's feet.

In this session we will be discussing from the 16th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Savaimahabhagavata Parikshit Yena apavarga aakhyam adabhra buddhihi Jnanena vayasaki shabditena Bhaje khagendra dhwaja padamoolam. The meaning of this verse is, O Suta Goswami, please describe those topics of the Lord by which Maharaj Parikshit, whose intelligence was fixed, on liberation attained the lotus feet of the Lord who is the shelter of Garuda, the king of birds. Those topics were vibrated by the son of Vyasa, Srila Sukadeva. So the sages of Naimisharanya are specifically requesting Suta Goswami to now speak about those topics, which were discussed by Parikshit Maharaj and Sukadeva Goswami with the intention of attaining shelter at the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord Krishna. That should be the only intent. No other intent should be there.

---
### impersonalism, devotional service, brahmajyoti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=146
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maharaj Pariksit's goal was the lotus feet of the Lord. Not some impersonal Brahman effulgence."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the controversy between impersonalists and devotees, highlighting that devotees worship the transcendental form of the Lord, not idols or imaginary forms, while impersonalists meditate on the Brahmajyoti. Maharaj Parikshit's goal was the lotus feet of the Lord, not impersonal Brahman effulgence.

So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says, there is some controversy amongst the students on the path of liberation. Such transcendental students are known as impersonalists and devotees of the Lord. Two categories of transcendentalists are generally there, among those who desire a transcendental benefit. Two kinds of transcendentalists, the impersonalists and the devotees. The devotee of the Lord worships the transcendental form of the Lord. Devotees always worship the transcendental form of the Lord. They don't engage in any idol worship. They don't worship any material form. They don't worship any imaginary form. They don't worship idols. Idol means imaginary form. Whereas the impersonalist meditates upon the glaring effulgence of the bodily rays of the Lord known as the Brahmajyoti. Always the impersonalist, their goal is the impersonal Brahmajyoti, which is the effulgence of the Supreme Lord Krishna, His bodily effulgence. So their object of meditation is the impersonal effulgence, Brahmajyoti. Here in this verse it is said that Maharaj Pariksit attained the lotus feet of the Lord by the instructions in knowledge delivered by the son of Vyasadeva, Srila Sukhdev Goswami. So Maharaj Pariksit's goal was the lotus feet of the Lord. Not some impersonal Brahman effulgence. Not some Brahmajyoti. Not some spiritual sky. No. His aim was very clear. Lotus feet of the Supreme Lord.

---
### sukadeva goswami, transformation, attraction to krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=278
**Verse References:** SB 2.1.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sukhdev Goswami in the beginning was an impersonalist... But later on, he was attracted by the transcendental pastimes of the Lord and thus became a devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Sukadeva Goswami was initially an impersonalist, realized in Brahman, but later became attracted to the transcendental pastimes of the Lord and became a devotee. This highlights the transformative power of Krishna's pastimes and the superiority of devotional service.

Sukhdev Goswami was also an impersonalist in the beginning as he himself is admitted in the Bhagavatam, Second canto, first chapter, ninth verse. But later on, he was attracted by the transcendental pastimes of the Lord and thus became a devotee. This is a very very important point. Sukhdev Goswami in the beginning was an impersonalist. That means he had realization of the impersonal aspect of the absolute truth. Brahman realized. He was a Brahman realized person. And he was a liberated soul by birth. That's a fact. But he did not know about the personality of Godhead. He did not know about the Bhagavan feature. He only knew the Brahman feature. And he himself has admitted in the beginning of the Bhagavatam that he was an impersonalist. Later on, he was attracted by the transcendental pastimes of the Lord and thus became a devotee. There is a verse called the Atmarama verse. That verse describes all varieties of Atmaramas. They are attracted by the Supreme Lord Krishna and they become devotees. And they do render pure devotional service to Krishna, irrespective of what their background might have been. So similarly, Sukhdev Goswami, even though he was a liberated soul, even though he was absorbed in thought of Brahman, still, he got attracted to the transcendental pastimes of the Lord when he heard them from his father Vyasadeva. So he heard the pastimes and he became very much attracted.

---
### devotee classification, mahabhagavata, madhyama adhikari, prakrita bhakta
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=414
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Mahabhagavata or the first class devotee sees everything in relation with the Lord and the Lord present in everyone's relation."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the three classes of devotees: Prakrita (third class), Madhyama (second class), and Mahabhagavata (first class). The Mahabhagavata sees everything in relation to the Lord, while the Madhyama Adhikari distinguishes between the Lord, devotees, neophytes, and non-devotees.

So, Srila Prabhupada says further, such devotees with perfect knowledge are called Mahabhagavatas or first class devotees. Like Sukhdev Goswami, Pariksit Maharaj, they are all Mahabhagavatas, first class devotees. So Srila Prabhupada further says, there are three classes of devotees and they are called Prakrita, Madhyama and Mahabhagavata. Three classes of devotees. The Prakrita or third class devotees are temple worshippers without specific knowledge of the Lord and the Lord's devotees. The lowest level of devotees is called third class devotees, Prakrita Bhakta. And what is their characteristic? They worship the Lord in the temple but they do not have specific knowledge of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. They do not have specific knowledge of the Supreme Lord's pure devotees. Then, the second class devotee or Madhyama knows well the Lord, the Lord's devotees, the neophytes, and the non-devotees also. So four distinct categories of personalities are distinguished by those in the intermediate stage, Madhyama Adhikari or second class devotee. The four stages are four stages in which different personalities are situated. The topmost is the Lord himself, the Supreme Personality, then the Lord's devotees, established devotees, and the neophytes, those who are just beginning devotional service. And finally, the non-devotees. They are neither interested nor they engage in any devotional service. So such non-devotees are the fourth class of people in this world in the view of the Madhyama Adhikari, the second class devotee. And the second class devotee accordingly deals with different categories of persons differently.

---
### mahabhagavata, universal vision, equality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=559
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Mahabhagavata therefore does not make any distinction particularly between a devotee and non-devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the qualities of a Mahabhagavata, emphasizing their ability to see everything in relation to the Lord and the Lord's presence in every relationship. They do not distinguish between devotees and non-devotees, recognizing everyone's engagement in service to the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

But the Mahabhagavata or the first class devotee sees everything in relation with the Lord and the Lord present in everyone's relation. This is a special qualification of a Mahabhagavata devotee. What is that? A Mahabhagavata devotee always sees everything in relation with the Lord. Everything actually is connected with the Lord. Nothing is independent of the Lord. So Mahabhagavata is able to see that. And not only everything is connected with the Lord, but the Lord is present in everyone's relation. There is no relationship in which Lord is absent. So Lord is connected with everybody. Everything is connected with the Lord. Both ways. The Mahabhagavata therefore does not make any distinction particularly between a devotee and non-devotee. This is a very peculiar qualification of a Mahabhagavata. He does not make a distinction between devotees and non-devotees. Why? Because his vision is everyone is engaged in their service to the supreme personality of God. That's the way a Mahabhagavata sees everyone engaged. So Mahabhagavata does not make a distinction between a devotee and a non-devotee.

---
### pariksit maharaj, initiation, kindness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=668
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pariksit's qualifications are being described here. What category of person is Pariksit? He was equally kind even to the personality of Kali and what to speak of others."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the Mahabhagavata's vision with that of the Madhyama Adhikari, highlighting that Maharaj Parikshit was a Mahabhagavata due to his initiation by Sukadeva Goswami. Parikshit's kindness extended even to Kali Purusha, demonstrating his advanced spiritual state.

Whereas we heard earlier a Madhyama Adhikari, intermediate devotee. He makes distinction between devotees and non-devotees. Maharaj Pariksit was such a Mahabhagavata devotee because he was initiated by a Mahabhagavata devotee, Shukadeva Swami. So how did Pariksit Maharaj become a Mahabhagavata? Because he was initiated by Shukadeva Swami. So he also became a Mahabhagavata. So Pariksit's qualifications are being described here. What category of person is Pariksit? He was equally kind even to the personality of Kali and what to speak of others. Such a kind-hearted soul, Pariksit Maharaj. He was kind to Kali Purusha because Kali Purusha surrendered. And what to speak of others who are not as fallen as Kali Purusha. So a Mahabhagavata devotee, Mahabhagavata devotee he is equally kind to everybody. That is one of his qualifications or characteristics.

---
### impersonalism vs devotion, transcendental hierarchy, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=764
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But a devotee has never become an impersonalist... this very fact proves that on the transcendental steps the step occupied by a devotee is higher than the step occupied by an impersonalist."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the superiority of devotional service over impersonalism, stating that while impersonalists can become devotees, devotees never regress to impersonalism. This indicates that devotional service is a higher transcendental platform. The speaker also mentions that those stuck on the impersonal step undergo more suffering.

So Srila Prabhupada says further there are many instances in the transcendental histories of the world of an impersonalist who has later become a devotee. Many instances. Shukadeva Goswami, the Kumaras. But a devotee has never become an impersonalist. Somebody who has taken to devotional service and perfected his devotional service he becomes situated on the transcendental platform eternally he remains on the transcendental platform. So Prabhupada says this very fact proves that on the transcendental steps the step occupied by a devotee is higher than the step occupied by an impersonalist. That means that impersonal realization is an intermediate position of a progressive transcendentalist. It is also stated in the Bhagavad Gita in the twelfth chapter of Bhagavad Gita in the fifth verse purport that person stuck on the impersonal step undergo more sufferings than achievement of reality.

---
### impersonalism, difficulty, perfection, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zhHx0b62YFM?t=839
**Verse References:** BG 12.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore knowledge imparted by Sukhadeva Goswami unto Maharaj Pariksit helped him attain the service of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes the Bhagavad Gita to illustrate the difficulties faced by those attached to the impersonal aspect of the Absolute Truth. He concludes by reiterating that the knowledge imparted by Sukadeva Goswami helped Maharaj Parikshit attain the transcendental service of the Lord, which is the perfect stage of liberation.

Even the verse says Those who are attached to is avyakta, impersonal aspect of the absolute truth. For them there is so much of difficulty on the path of impersonal realization that they have to undergo. And in the end they don't get the realization. They don't get that realization which they are entitled to. Therefore knowledge imparted by Sukhadeva Goswami unto Maharaj Pariksit helped him attain the service of the Lord. This is the kind of knowledge that was imparted by Sukhadeva Goswami. He spoke the Srimad Bhagavatam. And this stage of perfection is called apavarga or the perfect stage of liberation. So in this stage of liberation there is actual freedom from material bonding because of which they are factually liberated. So simple knowledge of liberation is material knowledge. Whereas when somebody attains the transcendental service of the Lord that is called the perfect stage of liberation. So Sukhadeva Goswami he gave perfect knowledge to Pariksit Maharaj. And the result was that Pariksit Maharaj attained the transcendental service of the Supreme Lord. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## SB 1.18.17-18 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 08-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, prayer

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

Om Agyanati Mirandhasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chaksharunmeelitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvijesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jai Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya.

---
### Srimad Bhagavatam, Bhakti Yoga, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=37
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is mainly full of the narrations of the activities of the supreme unlimited and therefore, it is the science of Bhakti Yoga or the devotional service of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, SB 1.18.17-18, explaining that they describe the purifying and supreme nature of the topics discussed between Pariksit Maharaj and Sukadeva Goswami, which are full of Bhakti Yoga and dear to pure devotees. He emphasizes that Srimad Bhagavatam is the science of Bhakti Yoga.

In this session we will be discussing from the 17th and 18th verses of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavata. To begin with, I will recite the 17th verse. Tanna parampuñña asamrutartham ākhyānam atyadbhuta yoganiṣṭham ākhyāhi ananta ācharitopa pannam pārīkṣitam bhāgavata ābirāmam. The meaning of this verse is that verses. Thus, please narrate to us the narrations of the unlimited, for they are purifying and supreme. They were spoken to Maharaj Pariksit and they are very dear to the pure devotees being full of Bhakti Yoga. So, the sages of Naimisharanya are requesting Suta Goswami to speak about those topics discussed between Pariksit Maharaj and Sukadeva Goswami. And what is the nature of those topics? They are purifying and supreme. They are very dear to pure devotees, these topics, because they are full of Bhakti Yoga. They are pure devotees. These topics are full of Bhakti Yoga. So, Prabhupada explains in the purport, what are spoken to Maharaj Pariksit and what is very dear to the pure devotees is Srimad Bhagavatam. Srimad Bhagavatam is mainly full of the narrations of the activities of the supreme unlimited and therefore, it is the science of Bhakti Yoga or the devotional service of the Lord. Thus, it is para or supreme.

---
### devotional service, knowledge, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=163
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even by conversing with such great souls, one can without delay cleanse oneself of all disqualifications resulting from lower births."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Srimad Bhagavatam is enriched with knowledge, religion, and especially devotional service. He then introduces the eighteenth verse, where Suta Goswami reveals that even someone born in a mixed caste can be elevated by serving and associating with advanced souls, cleansing themselves of disqualifications from lower births.

Because, although it is enriched with all kinds of knowledge and religion, it is specifically enriched with the devotional service of the Lord. That is a special characteristic of Bhagavatam. It contains transcendental knowledge. It contains topics of renunciation. It contains what is real religion. But most importantly, it contains devotional service of the supreme Lord. Bhakti Yoga. It is enriched with Bhakti Yoga. The eighteenth verse. Sūtavvāca ahovayam janma-bhṛto adhyahāsma vṛddhānu-vṛtyāpi viloma-jātāha dauṣkulyam ādhyam vidhunoti-shīgram mahattamānām abhidhāna-yogaḥ. The meaning is, Sri Sathya Goswami said, he is responding to whatever the sage Suna Mishra has said now, O God, although we are born in a mixed caste, we are still promoted in birthright simply by serving and following the great who are advanced in knowledge. Even by conversing with such great souls, one can without delay cleanse oneself of all disqualifications resulting from lower births.

---
### association, purification, Caitanya Mahaprabhu
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=261
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Higher association means all disqualifications are washed away."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the secret revealed by Suta Goswami: that association with higher devotees washes away disqualifications. He explains that Suta Goswami was born in a mixed caste but was purified by associating with Sukadeva Goswami and the sages of Naimisharanya. He then connects this principle to Caitanya Mahaprabhu, who elevated many low-born individuals through transcendental association, establishing them as acharyas.

So, Suta Goswami is revealing some secret here. A very, very important secret. What is he telling? That he was born in a mixed caste, viloma-jātāha born in a mixed caste. So, Srila Prabhupāda explains what is that born in a mixed caste. In the purport, Srila Prabhupāda says, Suta Goswami did not take his birth in a brāhmaṇa family. He was born in a family of mixed caste or an uncultured low family. So, Suta means somebody who is born in an uncultured low family is called a Suta. But because of higher association, like Sri Śukadeva Goswami and the great sages of Naimishāraṇya, certainly the disqualification of inferior birth was washed off. There is a power of association. Higher association means all disqualifications are washed away. And it actually happened to Suta Goswami. So, Srila Prabhupāda explains the principle that Lord Śrī Caitanya Mahāprabhu followed this principle in pursuance of the Vedic youth sages. And by his transcendental association, he elevated many low-born or those disqualified by birth or action to the status of devotional service and established them in the position of ācāryas or authorities.

---
### Caitanya Mahaprabhu, guru, qualification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=384
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If he is conversant with the science of Kṛṣṇa, then he can be accepted as ācārya or guru, or a spiritual master."
**Summary:** The speaker highlights that Caitanya Mahaprabhu strictly followed the principle of the Bhagavatam, similar to how Suta Goswami became qualified through association. He quotes Caitanya Mahaprabhu stating that anyone, regardless of their varna or ashrama, can be accepted as an acharya or guru if they are conversant with the science of Krishna.

Caitanya Mahāprabhu strictly followed this principle of the Bhagavatam. Just like Suta Goswami became qualified, and he became cleansed of all disqualifications simply by associating with pure devotees. Same thing, Caitanya Mahāprabhu himself gave association and he elevated those who were disqualified by birth or action to the status of devotional service. And when they become situated on this platform of pure devotional service, they become authorities. They become, they attain the position of ācāryas, those who can teach others by their own example. They are perfect in knowledge and realization. So, Caitanya Mahāprabhu clearly stated, any man, whatever he may be, whether a brāhmaṇa or a śūdra, by birth, or a householder or a mendicant in the order of society, if he is conversant with the science of Kṛṣṇa, he can be accepted as an ācārya or guru, a spiritual master. The exact verse is, kibha vipra kibha nyāsi śūdra kenanāya yehi kṛṣṇa tattvaveta sei guru hoya. This is Caitanya Mahāprabhu's statement. This means, irrespective of the position, either in the varṇa or the āśrama, varṇa positions, the highest is brāhmaṇa, the lowest is śūdra. So, Caitanya Mahāprabhu says it doesn't matter what the varṇa position is of a person. Neither the āśrama position, whether one is householder or mendicant in the order of society, that means whether one is grāṣṭa or sannyāsi, it doesn't matter. If he is conversant with the science of Kṛṣṇa, yehi kṛṣṇa tattvaveta, kṛṣṇa tattva, in truth he knows what is Kṛṣṇa, then he can be accepted as ācārya or guru, or a spiritual master. So, Caitanya Mahāprabhu followed this very rigidly.

---
### knowledge, qualification, Suta Goswami
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=533
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Suta Goswami learned the science of Kṛṣṇa from great rishis and authorities like Śukadeva and Vyāsadeva."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Suta Goswami learned the science of Krishna from Sukadeva Goswami and Vyasadeva, becoming so qualified that even high-class brahmanas eagerly sought to hear from him. He emphasizes that Suta Goswami's knowledge and realization of Krishna elevated him to the position of acharya. Prabhupada notes that Suta Goswami had the double advantage of hearing and preaching.

This principle, he followed this principle. Now, it is described here, Suta Goswami learned the science of Kṛṣṇa from great rishis and authorities like Śukadeva and Vyāsadeva. And he was so qualified, that even the sages of Naimisharanya eagerly wanted to hear from him the science of Kṛṣṇa in the form of Srimad Bhagavatam. So, Suta Goswami, by birth he was a low-born, but he learned the science of Kṛṣṇa from Śukadeva Goswami, from Vyāsadeva, and in this way he became qualified. And how much qualified did he become? That even the sages of Naimisharanya, who were high-class brāhmaṇas, atappumbir dvijaśreṣṭhā, Suta Goswami himself addresses the sages of Naimisharanya, you are dvijaśreṣṭhā, you are high-class brāhmaṇas. Even such high-class brāhmaṇas, they are eager to hear from Suta Goswami about Kṛṣṇa, because Suta Goswami knows and has realized the science of Kṛṣṇa. That's why he is in that exalted position of being the ācārya, to impart knowledge about Kṛṣṇa. So, Prabhupāda says, Suta Goswami had the double association of great souls by hearing and preaching. He heard them when they made inquiries, and he answered their questions by which he was preaching to them also. Transcendental science, or the science of Kṛṣṇa, has to be learned from the authorities, from somebody who is following these principles taught by Chaitanya Mahāprabhu.

---
### preaching, qualification, caste system
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=665
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By learning one acquires some qualification, and by preaching one acquires a higher qualification."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that preaching the science of Krishna consciousness is essential for higher qualification. He highlights that Suta Goswami was freed from all disqualifications due to his learning and preaching. He clarifies that Sukadeva Goswami did not refuse to teach Suta Goswami, nor did the sages of Naimisharanya refuse to hear from him, because of his birth. Their only consideration was Suta Goswami's knowledge of Krishna.

So, learning is not enough. One has to preach the science of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, and then one becomes still more qualified. By learning one acquires some qualification, and by preaching one acquires a higher qualification. So, Suta Goswami had both the advantages, and thus undoubtedly he was completely freed from all disqualifications, low birth and mental agonies. So, Suta Goswami cannot be considered as low born. Those disadvantages are all cleansed away. This verse definitely proves that Śrīla Śukadeva Goswami did not refuse to teach Suta Goswami about the transcendental science, nor did the sages of Naimishāna refuse to hear lessons from Suta Goswami, because of his inferior birth. See, both are there. Suta Goswami though low born, Śukadeva Goswami spoke Bhāgavatam, and Suta Goswami heard, and became enlightened, qualified. And the sages of Naimishāna, even though they are high class brāhmaṇas, they did not refuse to hear from Suta Goswami, because their only consideration was this Suta Goswami actually has heard about Kṛṣṇa and knows Kṛṣṇa. Therefore, let us hear from Him. That was their only consideration. So, Prabhupāda says one important point. This means that thousands of years ago, there is no bar to learning or preaching the transcendental science because of inferior birth.

---
### caste system, qualification, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UT9iNBB49kc?t=794
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Birth is not important, but the qualification of the person after taking birth, whatever be the family, if they acquire the qualifications by proper hearing, then they are washed off all the contamination of the lower birth."
**Summary:** The speaker refutes the claim that sudras were denied Vedic knowledge in the past, stating that there was never a bar to learning or preaching due to low birth. He explains that the rigidity of the caste system became prominent only in the last century due to the increase of unqualified people in higher castes. He concludes that qualification through proper hearing and association with pure devotees is more important than birth, leading to elevation and purification.

Many times these ignorant people, they accuse that in the past, so-called śūdras were denied the knowledge of the Vedas. They were denied transcendental knowledge. No, that's never true. That's never true. As Prabhupāda says, there was never any bar for learning or preaching the transcendental science because of low birth. The rigidity of the so-called caste system in Hindu society became prominent within only one hundred years or so, when the number of dvija bandhus or disqualified men in the families of higher caste increased. Prabhupāda is clearly pointing out in the last hundred years or so, that is, if you count from now, when Prabhupāda is writing the purport, he says last hundred years this has become so, vitiated caste system, Hindu caste system. But that was not the case before. So, how did this happen in the last hundred years or hundred and fifty years? Because the number of disqualified people taking birth in higher caste increased. And then, such disqualified people, they don't have qualification, they claim, I am a brāhmaṇa by birth. But without qualification, what is the use of such birth? It is useless. It is no good. So, therefore, Prabhupāda explains that birth is not important, but the qualification of the person after taking birth, whatever be the family, if they acquire the qualifications by proper hearing, then they are washed off all the contamination of the lower birth. So, this is the conclusion that more important than birth is the association through which one actually becomes elevated, association of pure devotees, association of great ācāryas, one becomes elevated, cleansed of all contamination. And one becomes qualified to speak such transcendental subject matter to others who may not be conversant with it. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Kṛṣṇa.

---

### chanting, purification, devotee
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MXzFvTU1ncI?t=49
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It completely purifies all contamination and elevates them directly to the transcendental platform to become pure devotees."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 19th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on the potency of chanting the holy name of the Lord to purify and elevate individuals.

In this session we are discussing from the 19th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. Kutap Unar Grunato Namatasya Mahatam Aikanta Parayanasya Yo Ananta Shaktir Bhagavanananto Mahat Gunatvadhyam Anantam Ahuhu. The meaning of this verse is, and what to speak of those who are under the direction of the great devotees chanting the holy name of the unlimited who has unlimited potency. The personality of Godhead, unlimited in potency and transcendental by attributes, is called the Ananta or unlimited. So here Suta Goswami is describing the potency of chanting the holy name of the unlimited Lord. What is that potency? It completely purifies all contamination and elevates them completely purifies all contamination and elevates them directly to the transcendental platform to become pure devotees.

---
### caste, karma, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MXzFvTU1ncI?t=116
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam asserts that one who chants the holy name of the Lord and the direction of a pure devotee can at once get free from the disadvantages due to a lower caste birth."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Prabhupada's purport, addressing arguments against lower-caste individuals becoming Brahmanas, and emphasizing that chanting the holy name under the guidance of a pure devotee can overcome disadvantages due to birth.

So in the purport, Prabhupada says, the Dvija Bandhu or the less intelligent uncultured men born of higher caste put forward many arguments against the lower caste men becoming Brahmanas in this life. They argue that birth in a family of Shudras or less than Shudras is made possible by one's previous sinful acts and that one therefore has to complete the terms of disadvantages due to lower birth. What does it mean? If somebody is taking birth in a Shudra family, that is because of their past sinful activities or past life, karma. So there is a basis for somebody to take birth in a Shudra family and somebody else to take birth in a Brahmana family. Yes, agreed. There is a definite basis for such birth to happen. It is not accidental, it is not blind, no. The argument by those born in Brahmin families, they say that if somebody is taken such a birth, they have to complete the life in that body that they have acquired. And only in the next birth, if they take birth in a Brahmana family, then they may be accepted as a Brahmana. Not before that, but here something else is told. So Prabhupada says to answer these false logicians, Srimad Bhagavatam asserts that one who chants the holy name of the Lord and the direction of a pure devotee can at once get free from the disadvantages due to a lower caste birth. Even though some, we may admit that somebody born in a low class family due to their past misdeeds or past karma, still, when they chant the holy name of the Supreme Lord under the direction of a pure devotee, they become free from all such material contamination due to the low birth. It completely purifies them.

## SB 1.18.20 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 10-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BFiVtLo-C4s?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy

**Summary:** The lecture begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers and mantras, invoking blessings and paying respects to the spiritual master and the Supreme Lord.

Om Agyana Timiraandhasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chaksharunmilitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamil Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Paschatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare.

---
### Godhead, unlimitedness, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BFiVtLo-C4s?t=41
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is now ascertained that he, the personality of Godhead, is unlimited and there is none equal to him. Consequently, no one can speak of him adequately."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces the 20th verse of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining its context and the meaning of the verse, which emphasizes the unlimited nature of the Supreme Lord and the unique position of Lakshmi Devi.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 20th verse of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. This chapter describes how Maharaj Pariksit was cursed by a brahmana boy, because of which it led to Pariksit Maharaj meeting Shukadev Goswami and Shri Madbhagavatam being spoken. So the 20th verse: Etavatalam Nanu Suchitena Gunair Asamya Natishamaya Nishaya Nasya Hitvetaraan Prarthayato Vibhuter Yasyangri Renum Jushate Nabhip Soho. The meaning of this verse is: It is now ascertained that he, the personality of Godhead, is unlimited and there is none equal to him. Consequently, no one can speak of him adequately. Great demigods cannot obtain the favor of the goddess of fortune even by prayers. But this very goddess renders service unto the Lord although he is unwilling to have such service. So this verse is a very important verse spoken by Suta Goswami.

---
### Krishna, Lakshmi, devotee service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BFiVtLo-C4s?t=134
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So he accepts the service of Lakshmi Devi, he accepts the service of his innumerable pure devotees simply for blessing them, for giving them his blessings, for showing his mercy upon them."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the unlimited qualities of Krishna, contrasting the efforts of demigods to gain Lakshmi Devi's favor with her voluntary service to the Lord. He emphasizes that Krishna is complete and doesn't need service, but accepts it to bless his devotees.

What is the position of the personality of Godhead? Sri Krishna. In the previous verse, there was a description about the personality of Godhead who is unlimited in potency and transcendental by all the attributes. He is called Ananta, the unlimited personality, supreme personality. So that unlimited, now he is justifying. Suta Goswami is justifying because nobody is equal to him and nobody else is, nobody else can be unlimited. Only one personality in the entire cosmic manifestation, that is the supreme personality of Godhead, Krishna alone is unlimited. No one can speak of him adequately. Anybody amount of description cannot exhaust the different transcendental qualities of the Supreme Lord. So he has unlimited qualities. He has unlimited forms. He has unlimited opulences. Everything about him is unlimited. One specific point here in this verse is that when compared to the goddess of fortune Lakshmi Devi and what is the position of the goddess of fortune Lakshmi Devi, great demigods they seek her favors but they are unable to obtain it even by offering so many prayers, but that goddess of fortune Lakshmi Devi she herself renders service to the unlimited Supreme Lord Anantha, the personality of godhead Krishna. But Krishna's position is not that he is in need of Lakshmi Devi service. He is full and himself is complete and he is not in need of Lakshmi Devi service. He is not in need even then it is his kindness that even though he doesn't require the service, it is for benefiting the servitor that the lord agrees to accept that service. So he accepts the service of Lakshmi Devi, he accepts the service of his innumerable devotees, pure devotees simply for blessing them, for giving them his blessings, for showing his mercy upon them and one should not wrongly think it is some kind of necessity that Krishna has to be served and he is dependent on the service of his devotees, no never.

---
### Shruti, Smriti, Vedic literature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BFiVtLo-C4s?t=353
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Shruti is the original Vedas which have been received in oral translation."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the difference between Shruti and Smriti literature, defining Shruti as the original Vedas received through oral tradition and Smriti as compilations and explanations of the Vedic message by saintly persons.

So in the purport Srila Prabhupada explains this point by clearly referencing the Shruti literature. Now what is the difference between Shruti literature and Smriti literature? Two kinds of Vedic literatures, Shruti and Smriti. Shruti is the original Vedas which have been received in oral translation of the Shruti literature which have been received in oral translation of the Shruti literature which have been received in oral translation of the Shruti literature which have been received in oral translation of the Shruti literature two kinds of Vedic literature one or the other. Shruti is within Brahma's heart. So at that time there was nobody other than the Supreme Lord and Brahma. Brahma is the first created being. So then from Brahma it is coming down in parampara from Brahma to his sons and disciples, from them to their disciples and successors. That is the oral tradition. It's called Shruti. It is received by hearing. It is received by hearing from the predecessor. So it is coming down in human society and since the beginning of creation, that's called Shruti literature, the original Vedas. What we say the original Vedas, you would have heard of these terms Rig Veda, Yajur Veda, Sama Veda, Atharva Veda. So these four Vedas are basically the original Vedic knowledge and these Vedas have many subdivisions also, many varieties of details are there of this original Vedas, Samhitas, Brahmanas, Aranyakas, Upanishads like that. It there is it's very vast body of knowledge. Then there are other kind of Vedic literature called Shruti literature. What is Shruti literature? Shruti literature is the compilation of the same vedic message by the saintly persons, by the great devotees, by witches sages or by for explaining the Vedic message to a particular audience, to explain in an easily understandable way because the Vedic message in the original Vedas is very difficult to understand for most people, for common people. So it has been simplified and presented in different ways to disseminate the same knowledge, the same message without any change, without any interpretation, the original message as it is explained in simple way. So what is this Smriti literature? The examples of Smriti literature are all other literature other than the Vedas.

---
### Puranas, Itihasas, Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BFiVtLo-C4s?t=579
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavatam is in the category of Smriti literature, that is one of the Puranas, but it is not a creation of some person which is independent of the Vedic message; it is exactly the same message of the vedas."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Smriti literature, giving examples such as the Puranas, Itihasas, Sutras, Upavedas, and Vedangas. He clarifies that Srimad Bhagavatam falls under Smriti but contains the same message as the original Vedas, presented in a more accessible way.

Like the Puranas, the Itihasas, the Sutras, the Upaveda's, the Vedangas and the various commentaries, the various explanations given by realized Acharyas etc. So Bhagavatam is in the category of Smriti literature, that is one of the Puranas, but it is not a creation of some person which is independent of the Vedic message. It is exactly the same message of the vedas, the original Vedas which is there in the Bhagavatam. But the way it is written and it is not something which gets stolen in some way there is no reason for that. The speciality of bhagavatam is it is directly and easily understandable the Vedas sometimes speak in a very very indirect way whereas the Puranas are directly describing the Vedic message.

---
### Shruti, Parameshwara, Krishna

**Verse References:** —

So Srila Prabhupada in the opening of the purport itself he says the personality of Godhead or the Parameshwara Parabrahman according to the Shrutis has nothing to do. So in the Shrutis it is said natasya karyam karanam cha vidyate natat samascha abhyadikas cha dhrushyate parashya shaktir vividhaiva shuyate svabhaviki jnanam karyam karanam cha vidyate natasya karyam karanam cha vidyate natasya karyam karanam cha vidyate natasya karyam karanam cha vidyate natasya karyam karanam cha vidyate natasya karyam karanam cha vidyate natasya.

---
### self-sufficiency, completeness, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BFiVtLo-C4s?t=792
**Verse References:** BG 3.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Thus the supreme personality of God is full in himself and he has nothing to accept from anyone else."
**Summary:** The speaker reinforces the concept of Krishna's self-sufficiency, drawing from the Bhagavad Gita and explaining that unlike everyone else who has duties and needs, Krishna is complete and doesn't require anything from anyone, including the demigods.

See Bhagavad Gita also the same message. Bhagavad Gita is also part of Mahabharata, which is Smriti literature, Itihasa. But even that Bhagavad Gita, what is spoken by Krishna and documented or recorded by Vyasa Deva also contains the same Esoteric message that the absolute supreme absolute truth the Supreme Personality of God has nothing to do. What does it mean he has nothing to do? So that is explained by Prabhupada further. He has no equal. That means everybody else has got duties. Everybody else has got something to do by their very constitution. Prabhupada further says nor does anyone excel him. Nobody can excel him. He is no equal. He has no superior. Everybody is subordinate to him. His position is unique in that he has unlimited potencies and is every action is carried out systematically in his natural perfect ways. parashya-shaktir-vividhayi-vashuyate-svabhavikī, natural jñāna-balak-kriyāca automatically, systematically, very nicely carried out by his different energies. Thus the supreme personality of God is full in himself and he has nothing to accept from anyone else including the great demigods like Brahma. So nobody need to offer him service for the sake of Krishna being in need of something, no. In this world we accept service from others because we are in need. Everybody is in need. Nobody is complete by themselves, nobody is complete, but the supreme lord is full in himself and he is completely full. So he is not in need, we are in need, so that difference always eternally exists. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## SB 1.18.21 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 11-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna as Supreme Lord, Vedic Literature, Impersonal vs. Personal
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eVpj0Zlc1Ec?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The absolute truth is impersonal, localized, and personal, and the person is the ultimate source of even the localized feature and the impersonal feature."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam and explains that Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead, contrasting this with the impersonal understanding of the absolute truth found in some Vedic literature.

Om Agyana Timiraandhasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chaksharonmilitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhigaura Bhakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will discuss from the 21st verse of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam: Athapiyatpada Nakhaava Srishtam Jagadvirincho Pahruta Arganambhaha Sesham Punati Anyatamau Mukundarth Konamaloke Bhagavadpadarthaha. The meaning of this verse is who can be worthy of the name of the Supreme Lord but the personality of God as Sri Krishna. Brahmaji collected the water emanating from the nails of his feet in order to award it to Lord Shiva as a worshipful welcome. This very water, the Ganges, is purifying the whole universe including Lord Shiva. This yet another word spoken by Suta Goswami to assert that Krishna is the supreme personality recorded because in the Shruti literature, in the original Vedas, there is so much description of the supreme absolute truth in impersonal feature, especially the Upanishads, that people think according to the Shrutis the absolute truth is impersonal only. But that is completely wrong. The absolute truth is impersonal, localized, and personal, and the person is the ultimate source of even the localized feature and the impersonal feature.

---
### Brahma's Role, Creation, Krishna's Supremacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eVpj0Zlc1Ec?t=115
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Among the many, many, many personalities in this entire creation, in the cosmic manifestation, who can be called as a supreme Lord? It is only Krishna, Shri Krishna, the personality of God is Shri Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Brahma's role in secondary creation, emphasizing that even Brahma, the topmost personality in the universe, collects water from Krishna's feet, further establishing Krishna's supreme position.

This is clearly described in many places in the Shruti and the Smriti, so therefore who among the many, many, many personalities in this entire creation, in the cosmic manifestation, can be called as a supreme Lord? It is only Krishna, Shri Krishna, the personality of God is Shri Krishna. Only in the Shruti can be called as the Supreme Lord, nobody else can be called like that. Why so? That is being substantiated by Suta Goswami by saying one specific feature which illustrates this point very, very clearly. Now consider Brahma. He is the top most personality in this entire created universe. He is the first created being and he does the secondary creation after the initial work of creating the different elements and making all arrangements for creation and creation and creation and creation and creation and creation and creation, the living entities to take birth in this material world. The Supreme Lord has entrusted the secondary creation to Brahma. So Brahma is so powerful, he creates all the other creatures in the entire universe in millions of planets, each planet having millions of living beings. All of them are given their body at the beginning of creation by Brahma. Of course, Brahma is assisted by his many assistants, but nevertheless it begins with Brahma among the created beings. So but above Brahma is the Supreme Lord himself and Brahma has been given the power by the Supreme Lord.

---
### Ganga's Origin, Purification, Vamana Deva
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eVpj0Zlc1Ec?t=297
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It consists of the water that emanated from the nails of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the origin of the Ganges River from the nails of Krishna's lotus feet and its flow through the universe, also mentioning the incarnation of Vamana Deva and the causal ocean.

That Brahma, what is he doing? He is collecting the water emanating from the nails of Krishna's lotus feet and why is he doing that? In all of our need to Lord Shiva as a worshipful welcome. There is a description in the fifth canto bother in other places in the scriptures that the origin of the river Ganga flows through all the planets in the whole universe, one river that flows in all the planets from the topmost Brahmaloka up to Pathada. Every planet, this river flows. What is this river actually consisting of? It consists of the water that emanated from the nails of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord Krishna. In his incarnations, Vamana Deva, the Supreme Lord Krishna, he extended his foot to pierce the covering of the universe and because each universe is like a shell floating in the Garbhodaka ocean, I am sorry, floating in the Karanodaka ocean, causal ocean. So that water of the causal ocean, which is completely spiritual water, that leaked from the hole that was formed in this universal covering. And while that water came through the hole, how was that hole made? When Vamana Deva extended his foot to pierce the coverings of the universe. And the water came, it washed the feet of the Supreme Lord. And that water is very respectfully received by Brahma. It's very precious. Why? Because it was spiritual, but it contains the dust of the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord.

---
### Ganga's Potency, Lord Shiva, Purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eVpj0Zlc1Ec?t=451
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Shiva considers that that water of the river Ganges is purifying him."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the water from Krishna's feet is precious and potent, received by Brahma and awarded to Lord Shiva, who considers it purifying even for himself.

And that is very, very, very, very precious for all of us. That has got all the potencies of the Supreme Lord. So Brahma received it and he awarded it to Lord Shiva. Shiva received this water of the Ganga river on this earth planet. And that's another description given in the fifth canto of the Bhagavatam. What was the need for Shiva to receive that water and how Shiva received it? That's a detailed account given in the fifth canto. Some other time I may explain that. But Shiva considers that that water of the river Ganges is purifying him. Shiva is a pure personality, but even he considers the sacred water of the Ganga river as to be very, very potent and powerfully purifying. He says it purifies even me. What to speak of others? So therefore it is said this water of the Ganga is purifying the whole universe including Lord Shiva.

---
### Ganga's Purifying Power, Krishna's Supremacy, One God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eVpj0Zlc1Ec?t=532
