**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Ganga can purify any sinner completely, simply if he takes bath once in the river Ganga."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the purifying power of the Ganges, originating from Krishna's feet, and clarifies that despite the existence of many devatas, Krishna is the one Supreme Lord.

So that is the actual position of the Supreme Lord in comparison with such great personalities as Brahma, Shiva and Mother Ganga herself. Ganga flows through the whole universe, all the planets, because wherever any living being has got any sinful reactions, simply by taking a bath in the Ganga, their sinful reactions are completely nullified, are destroyed. So who can have that potency? How did Mother Ganga, the river Ganga get that potency? Because Ganga is emanating from the nails of the lotus feet of Krishna. Therefore Ganga has become very, very powerful and got the purifying effect. Ganga can purify any sinner completely, simply if he takes bath once in the river Ganga. So how powerful the water of the river Ganga is, you can understand by this description. So in the purport Shila Prabhupada says, the conception of many gods in the Vedic literature by the ignorant is completely wrong. People think, no? In the Vedic tradition or in the Hindu religion, they say there are many gods. Shiva, Brahma, Vishnu, Ganesha, Kartikeya, Durga and Lakshmi and this thing. So many, 33 crore devatas are there. 33 crore devatas are there. So they think that actually God is not one, but God is one. God is not one, but God is many in all these forms of the devatas. Actually, the scriptures clearly describe the Lord is one without a second. Ekameva Advitiya. Means he is only one, Supreme God. And Advitiya, there is no one who is a second god equal to him or like him. Nobody is there. Nobody is there.

---
### Krishna's Expansions, Mystic Powers, Krishna's Supremacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/eVpj0Zlc1Ec?t=690
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is always the supreme, always the greatest, always the most powerful."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that although the Lord is one, He expands Himself into many forms like Krishna, Balarama, and others, and that Krishna possesses unlimited mystic powers compared to the limited powers of yogis, solidifying Krishna's position as the Supreme Lord.

Now, even though the Lord is one without a second, he is only one personality, one person. Still, because he is an unlimited person, he expands himself in many ways. What is the meaning of expanding? He is present, let us say, as Krishna. He is also present as Balarama. He is also present as Ramachandra. He is also present as Vamanadeva. He is also present as Varaha. He is also present as Narasimha. He is also present as Narayana. He is also present as Sankarsana. Now, all these forms of personality is one person. He is one person. Now, this is difficult to understand for limited beings like us. What is our experience? One person means one form. That's all. That's our experience. We don't know of any one person, one soul, multiple bodies or multiple forms. We don't know. Actually, in the Vedic literature it is described those who acquire this mystic power, yogic power, yoga siddhi. One of the siddhis is, one of the powers they acquire is when they practice Ashtanga Yoga and advance, then they can expand themselves in nine forms. A yogi who has got yogic power by properly practicing the Ashtanga Yoga system can acquire different kinds of powers. Eight major siddhis, eight minor siddhis and four auxiliary siddhis. Totally 20 siddhis. But Krishna also has mystic powers. He has unlimited mystic powers. See the difference? And Krishna's mystic powers are such that Krishna also expands. Yogi can also expand. But yogi can expand maximum nine forms and Krishna expands into unlimited, he expands into unlimited forms. So, Krishna is always the supreme, always the greatest, always the most powerful and everyone else has got a very, very, very, very tiny fraction of the qualities of Krishna, of the powers of Krishna, of the beauty of Krishna, of the wealth of Krishna, of the every aspect of Krishna. Everyone else has got a very, very tiny portion of it and that tiny portion of whatever they possess is awarded by Krishna. So, never consider Krishna to be like another devata, like another god. No. Krishna is the only, one and only supreme lord. He is unlimited. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## SB 1.18.22 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 12-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, verse analysis, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Self-control means the person who is attached to the Supreme Lord, Sri Krishna, can all of a sudden give up the world of material attachment, including the gross body and subtle mind, and go away to attain the highest perfection of the renounced order of life, by which non-violence and renunciation are consequential."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with invocations and then introduces and translates Srimad Bhagavatam 1.18.22, focusing on the concept of self-control and detachment from material attachments through devotion to Krishna.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananj Nishalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatharine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Ganadhara Shri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udeerayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 22nd verse of the 18th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, the first canto of the Srimad Bhagavatam. The 22nd verse is as follows: Yatra Anuraktaha Sahasaiva Dheera Vyapohya Deha Dishu Sangam Udham Vrajantitat Paramahamsyam Antyam Yasmin Ahimsa Upashamaha Swadharmaha. The meaning of this verse is self-control. Self-control means the person who is attached to the Supreme Lord, Sri Krishna, can all of a sudden give up the world of material attachment, including the gross body and subtle mind, and go away to attain the highest perfection of the renounced order of life, by which non-violence and renunciation are consequential. Ahimsa and Upashama.

---
### Krishna's qualities, renunciation, attachment to Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=116
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even if somebody has got a lot of yogic power, even if somebody has done a lot of tapasya, even if somebody has got a lot of knowledge, has got so many other good qualities, if they don't have attachment for Krishna, it is not possible to nullify all material attachments, to go away from this material existence to Krishna, to attain lotus feet of Krishna. Not possible."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the glorification of Krishna and the unique qualities that enable great renunciants like Maharaj Parikshit to detach from material existence. He emphasizes that attachment to Krishna is essential for nullifying material attachments, even surpassing the power of yoga, austerity, or knowledge.

So, further, the glorification of Krishna, Krishna's unique position and unique qualities of Krishna, that if we see some wonderful qualities in great renunciants, like Maharaj Parikshit, for example, this verse has got a reference to Maharaj Parikshit and such great personalities. Those who are self-controlled and they have reached the perfection of renunciation, they are called Paramahamsas. They will be explained by Prabhupada in the purport. I am just mentioning it with respect to the translation itself. So such Paramahamsas, they are able to completely give up all of a sudden their gross body and subtle mind, and go away to achieve the perfection of existence. And how is it possible for them to give up such attachments, material attachments, which are very deep-rooted and very complex, and very difficult to give up? It is because of their attachment to Krishna. So even if somebody has got a lot of yogic power, even if somebody has done a lot of tapasya, even if somebody has got a lot of knowledge, has got so many other good qualities, if they don't have attachment for Krishna, it is not possible to nullify all material attachments, to go away from this material existence to Krishna, to attain lotus feet of Krishna. Not possible.

---
### self-control, sense enjoyment, minimum requirements
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=251
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Self-controlled means not indulging in sense enjoyment more than is necessary. The minimum requirements of the senses are to be fulfilled. Bare minimum."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Prabhupada's explanation of self-control, defining it as minimizing sense enjoyment to only the necessary requirements for maintaining the material body. He clarifies that self-control doesn't mean completely giving up all necessities, as that would make existence impossible.

So Prabhupada explains this in the purport, in a very nice way. He says, only the self-controlled can gradually be attached to the Supreme Personality. So even among different categories of transcendentalists, if any transcendentalist, a spiritual aspirant, is not self-controlled, then he cannot get attachment, cannot develop attachment for Krishna. Now what does self-controlled mean? Prabhupada says, self-controlled means not indulging in sense enjoyment more than is necessary. The minimum requirements of the senses are to be fulfilled. Bare minimum. And such people are called self-controlled. Self-controlled does not mean I completely give up all the necessities of the body or the senses. No. Then there is no possibility of existing even. Existence itself becomes impossible. So, as long as the material body is there, the minimum requirements of the body has to be provided, even by a transcendentalist, even by a spiritualist. So, such personalities who have perfected their life by practicing, giving the requirements of the senses, providing the requirement of the senses to the minimum extent possible, in a matter of eating or sleeping or defending, they provide the body with the minimum requirements. And such people, they never indulge in sense enjoyment more than minimum required.

---
### sense enjoyment, philosophical speculation, subtle enjoyment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=398
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dry philosophical speculation is a subtle sense enjoyment of the mind."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts self-control with unrestricted sense enjoyment and introduces the concept of 'dry philosophical speculation' as a subtle form of sense enjoyment that can hinder spiritual progress. Speculation involves guessing, imagining, calculating, and deducing about the absolute truth, God, the cause of material existence, the ultimate goal of life, etc.

So, those who are not self-controlled, Prabhupada says, are given over to sense enjoyment without restriction. Unrestricted sense enjoyment. Or as much as they feel like enjoying, they enjoy. But that is not the way of progressing in spiritual life to attain perfection. And Prabhupada makes a very important point here. Dry philosophical speculation is a subtle sense enjoyment of the mind. So, some people gross senses they have controlled. They are only providing the minimum required. But as far as philosophical speculation, what is philosophical speculation? Speculation means keep on guessing, keep on imagining, keep on calculating, keep on deducing. So, that sort of speculation, which is in order to understand what is the absolute truth, in order to know God, or in order to theorize what is the cause of this material existence, or what is the ultimate goal of life, or who is God. Such philosophical speculation is also a subtle sense enjoyment of the mind.

---
### sense enjoyment, material bondage, path of darkness, path of light
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=497
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sense enjoyment, whether gross or subtle, leads one to the path of darkness. That means one remains in ignorance."
**Summary:** The speaker distinguishes between gross and subtle sense enjoyments, explaining how both can lead to material bondage and ignorance. He emphasizes that those who remain in ignorance continue to cycle through birth and death, suffering in material existence. He concludes by stating that self-control is necessary to progress towards liberation, urging listeners to move from darkness to light.

There are two kinds of enjoyments any person can have in this world. One type of enjoyment is the gross enjoyment of the external senses: the eyes, the ears, the nose, the tongue, and the sense of touch. Then there is one more sense within. It's called the mind. Now satisfying the mind, demands of the mind. Just like somebody likes to read a nice story, fiction, novel. Now what kind of pleasure does it give the person? It's a subtle pleasure of the mind. Or the pleasure of the mind. Pleasure of the intelligence. When somebody uses their intelligence for solving some problem in physics or mathematics, that gives them great joy. And what is that kind of joy or pleasure? It's a pleasure, internal pleasure, subtle pleasure. So this sort of subtle sense enjoyment is also a cause for material bondage. So Prabhupada says, sense enjoyment, whether gross or subtle, leads one to the path of darkness. That means one remains in ignorance. Who am I? What is this world? What is the goal of life? They remain in ignorance. And as a result of remaining ignorance, they keep taking repeated birth and death, birth and death, birth and death, birth and death, and consequently they suffer in material existence, the fundamental miseries, janma-mṛtyu-jarā-vyādhī-duḥkha-dośānudarshana. Those who are self-controlled can make progress on the path of liberation from the conditional life of material existence. So, to make progress beyond this material world, to get out of material existence, is possible for those who practice self-control. The Vedas therefore enjoin that one should not go on the path of darkness, but should make a progress and march towards the path of light or liberation. Tamaso ma jyotir gama. Tamas means the darkness of this material existence. And jyoti means the light, path of light or liberation, out of this material world, out of material existence. Don't remain in darkness, progress towards the light.

---
### self-control, sense engagement, bhakti, transcendental service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=670
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The real method of self-control is to become attached to the Supreme Lord by engaging one's pure senses in his transcendental service."
**Summary:** The speaker refutes the idea of artificially stopping the senses as a means of self-control. Instead, he advocates for engaging purified senses in the transcendental service of the Supreme Lord, Krishna, through bhakti. This involves freeing oneself from material designations and engaging the senses in loving service.

Self-control is actually a journey achieved not by artificially stopping the senses from material enjoyment. Some people, some philosophers, some transcendentalists, they say if you want to control the senses, stop all sense activity. That means if you are seeing with the eyes, stop seeing. If you are hearing with the ears, stop hearing. Like that. Negate all sense activities. Now, this is not the way of self-control. It may seem like with great amount of austerity, if somebody stops the sense activities, they may expect that they will become completely self-controlled. But the real method of self-control is to become attached to the Supreme Lord by engaging one's pure senses in his transcendental service. sarvopādhi-vinirmuktaṁ tat-paratvena-nirmalam hṛṣīkeṇa hṛṣīkeṣa-sevanam bhaktir ucyate. Bhakti means serving the master of the senses, the Supreme Lord, through one's purified senses by becoming free from all material designations. Our senses become designated due to different material activities. But to free oneself from material designations and thereby purify the senses, and engage the purified senses in transcendental loving service of the Supreme Lord Krishna, that is actually the way of becoming self-controlled. Senses have to be given proper engagement, not simply forcibly stop them. Don't do that. Don't forcibly stop the senses.

---
### sense purification, bhakti yoga, detachment, going back to Godhead
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/OQRMpDaJJoM?t=803
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those who are attached to bhakti yoga, they can easily give up all material attachments, any time, all of a sudden if they are called."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that senses become purified through bhakti, such as chanting Hare Krishna, which cleanses the mind of material contamination. Purified senses are always engaged in the transcendental service of the Lord, a perfectional stage called bhakti yoga. Those attached to bhakti yoga can easily detach from material attachments and go back to Godhead, even at the time of death.

So Prabhupād says, when the senses become purified, and that is the process of bhakti, sādhanā bhakti, senses become purified. When you chant Hare Kṛṣṇa, ceto darpana mārjanam, your senses, your mind becomes purified. Mārjanam, become cleansed of material dirt, material contamination that goes away as you keep on chanting, as you keep on performing bhakti, activities of devotional service. So these purified senses are always engaged in the transcendental service of the Lord. The only engagement for the purified senses is serving Kṛṣṇa, doing devotional service to Kṛṣṇa. This perfectional stage of sense enjoyment is called bhakti yoga. So those who are attached to bhakti yoga, they can easily give up all material attachments, any time, all of a sudden if they are called. Like what happens? It seems like suddenly death comes. So it's never sudden, but we don't know exactly when we are going to die. We know we are going to die, but we don't know when. So whenever that happens, it is like all of a sudden death comes. But a devotee who has practiced devotional service and is factually situated in practice of bhakti, such a devotee can suddenly give up all material attachments and progress and go back to Godhead. That is what is explained here. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Kṛṣṇa.

---

### knowledge, humility, capacity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ukEyxoXQRY?t=50
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.23
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Learned devotees always describe the Lord as much as their realization allows."
**Summary:** Suta Goswami addresses the sages, stating he will describe Vishnu's pastimes to the best of his ability, comparing it to birds flying as high as they can, and acknowledging the Lord's unlimited qualities.

In this session we are discussing from three verses: the 23rd, 24th and 25th verses of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. So first we will discuss the 23rd verse. The meaning of this verse is that O Rishis, now Suta Goswami is addressing the sages who are as powerfully pure as the sun. He is glorifying the assembled sages, that you are all very very powerfully pure as the sun. To you I am speaking. So I shall try to describe to you the transcendental past tense of Vishnu as far as my knowledge is concerned. And he gives an example. As the birds fly in the sky as far as their capacity allows, so do the learned beings. So do the learned devotees describe the Lord as far as their realization allows. Now this has got a connection with one of the previous verses where it was described the supreme personality of Godhead Krishna has got unlimited qualities, has got unlimited attributes. Therefore it's called Ananta. So nobody can completely describe the Lord. Nobody can fully describe the Lord. So the Lord is the Lord.

---
### analogy, devotion, capacity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ukEyxoXQRY?t=173
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Garuda can fly from one planet to another planet."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of birds flying at different heights, with Garuda flying the highest, to illustrate how devotees describe the Lord according to their realization and capacity.

So does it mean because we can't describe the Lord fully, so we don't describe him at all? No. Learned devotees always describe the Lord as much as their realization allows. As much as they know they describe. Just like the birds, they fly in the sky. Not all birds can fly as high as another bird. Some birds can fly at low heights. Some birds can fly even higher. And Garuda, what is the capacity of Garuda to fly? Garuda can fly from one planet to another planet. And there are other birds also that can fly. But Garuda can fly piercing the coverings of the universe, can go outside this material universe, into the spiritual sky. And even in the spiritual sky, Garuda can fly through the spiritual sky and go to the spiritual planet of Vaikuntha Loka because Garuda is the carrier of the Supreme Lord Vishnu. And Vishnu travels. He travels within this universe from one planet to another planet. The descriptions are there in the Bhagavatam. Sometimes he travels from the material world to the spiritual world, sometimes he travels in the spiritual world to the material world like that. So Garuda because he's the carrier of the Lord and the Lord needs to go wherever, so Garuda is able to fly the maximum, he can fly everywhere. So like that the example is given.

---
### hunting, renunciation, circumstance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ukEyxoXQRY?t=277
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.24, SB 1.18.25
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Once upon a time Maharaj Pariksit while engaged in hunting in the forest with bow and arrows became extremely fatigued hungry and thirsty while following the stags while searching for a reservoir of water he entered the hermitage of the well-known Shamika Rishi and saw the sage sitting silently with closed eyes."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the next two verses, describing Pariksit Maharaj's hunting expedition, his fatigue, and his arrival at Shamika Rishi's hermitage, setting the stage for the Srimad Bhagavatam's narration.

Just like birds can actually fly as high as their capacity permits them, similarly the learned devotees also described the Lord as much as their realization allows. So then the next two verses are actually giving us the prelude to the Bhagavatam. What happens by which Shukadeva, sorry, Pariksit Maharaj met Shukadeva. So on what happened so that he is bringing a description here. 24th and 25th verses together they are given ekadha dhanurudhyamya vicharan vrgyamvane vrgaan anugata shantaha shuditas trishito vrsham janashayam achakshanah praviveshatam ashramam dadarshamunim asinam shantam milita no chanam beautiful verses. The meaning of these two verses is once upon a time Maharaj Pariksit while engaged in hunting in the forest with bow and arrows became a hunter and a hunter and a hunter and a hunter and a hunter and a hunter and a hunter became extremely fatigued hungry and thirsty while following the stags while searching for a reservoir of water he entered the hermitage of the well-known Shamika Rishi and saw the sage sitting silently with closed eyes.

---
### divine arrangement, devotion, purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ukEyxoXQRY?t=377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord is so kind to his pure devotees that in proper time he calls such devotees up to him and thus creates an auspicious circumstance for the devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Prabhupada's purport, highlighting that the circumstances leading to the Bhagavatam's narration were divinely arranged to bring Pariksit Maharaj back to Godhead.

So Prabhupada says in the purport that the circumstances under which Srimad Bhagavatam was suffering was the circumstances under which Srimad Bhagavatam was suffering was the circumstances under which Srimad Bhagavatam was spoken for the benefit of all concerned is now being explained beginning with the words once upon a time this happened sometime in history. Therefore Suta Goswami he also is talking of the past because he heard the Bhagavatam from Shukadeva Goswami. When Shukadeva Goswami spoke to Pariksit Maharaj. So that's what Goswami said. Once upon a time in the past, what happened? Maharaj Pariksit was engaged in hunting in the forest. Hunting was permitted for Kshatriyas to keep up their skills for fighting, for protecting the weaker sections, for challenging the enemies, for controlling the miscreants, etc. So while he went hunting, he became extremely fatigued, hungry and thirsty while hunting. And he started searching for water, reservoir of water, and he couldn't find. And while doing so, because he was very thirsty and fatigued and hungry, he entered the hermitage of the well-known Shamika Rishi. A great saint. I will be described later on who is the Shamika Rishi in more detail. For now, he entered the hermitage of the well-known Shamika Rishi and saw the sage sitting with closed eyes. Shamika Rishi was sitting in meditation. His eyes were closed. And in such a situation, Pariksit Maharaj entered that ashram of the Shamika Rishi. Now in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says something very, very significant. He says how this is the prelude. Prabhupada says, the Supreme Lord is so kind to his pure devotees that in proper time he calls such devotees up to him and thus creates an auspicious circumstance for the devotee. Devotees come to the material world for the purpose of the Lord. The pure devotees who belong to the spiritual world. They come to this material world.

---
### detachment, divine will, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ukEyxoXQRY?t=523
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By the desire of the Lord even such a devotee pure devotee like Parikshit Maharaj can become apparently fatigued and thirsty just to create a situation favorable for its renunciation of worldly activities."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on how the Lord orchestrates situations to bring devotees back to Him, even causing discomfort to pure devotees like Pariksit Maharaj to facilitate their renunciation of worldly attachments.

But after they finish, the purpose for which they have come to this world they generally are going back to godhead because that's where they belong. So they come from Krishna's personal abode and they go back just like the Lord comes from his personal abode and he goes back same way pure devotees also the associates of the Lord also do the same thing. So when the Lord feels that his pure devotee who has gone to the material world from his abode has to come back, if he wants the devotee to come back earlier than a normal time frame then he creates some awkward situation so that the devotee will leave everything and go to the Lord. So a similar thing happened to Pariksit Maharaj. So because the Lord wanted Pariksit to come back to him, so therefore the Lord arranged that Pariksit Maharaj become extremely fatigued hungry and thirsty. So Prabhupada says was a pure devotee and there is no reason for him to become extremely fatigued hungry and thirsty. Why? Because a devotee of the Lord never becomes perturbed by such bodily demands. A devotee is fully self-controlled as his personal bodily demands are concerned, he is completely controlled. So there is no need for him to feel extremely inconvenienced by thirst hunger and fatigue. But by the desire of the Lord even such a devotee pure devotee like Parikshit Maharaj can become apparently fatigued and thirsty just to create a situation favorable for its renunciation of worldly activities. Crispy is ruling as the Emperor of the world and we have heard about his.

---
### detachment, divine intervention, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ukEyxoXQRY?t=665
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The supreme law never forgets his pure devotee even though that pure devotee may be engaged in so-called worldly affairs sometimes he creates an awkward situation and the devotee becomes obliged to renounce all worldly affairs."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain how Krishna creates situations to encourage detachment and renunciation in devotees, even when they are deeply involved in worldly duties, ensuring they return to Him.

Encountering the personality of Kali and how he dealt with Kali in a very just manner he punished and rewarded Kali but he was ruling perfectly but when Krishna wants his devotee back he wants a devotee first of all to renounce all worldly connections so you know to create a favorable situation for renouncing of Pariksit Maharaj what did Krishna do Krishna made him feel extreme inconvenience due to thirst fatigue and hunger so Srila Prabhupada says one has to give up all attachment for worldly relations before one is able to go back to Godhead so this is one of the qualifications anybody wants to go back to Godhead has to give up all the attachments for worldly relationships and when a devotee is too much absorbed in worldly affairs the Lord creates situation to cause indifference devotees Pariksit Maharaj he was busy doing his duty of ruling the world and in the course of doing his duty he was engaged in so many different material activities in the kingdom but the Lord wants him to give up all those material attachments so what the Lord do he created a situation the supreme law never forgets his pure devotee even though that pure devotee may be engaged in so-called worldly affairs sometimes he creates an awkward situation and the devotee becomes obliged to renounce all worldly affairs it happened to Pariksit.

Similarly, had Pariksit Maharaj not been fatigued, hungry and thirsty, then Bhagavatam would not have been spoken by Shukadev Goswami. So therefore, in order that Shukadev Goswami speak Bhagavatam for our benefit through Pariksit Maharaj, Pariksit Maharaj apparently became extremely hungry, thirsty and fatigued. ### consciousness, spiritual life, sense control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mI2xBQkS-uE?t=118
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this is a very, very technical description of the state of consciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker reads the translation of the 26th verse, emphasizing the restraint of the muni's senses, breath, mind, and intelligence from material action, and highlights the technical description of the state of consciousness, explaining that those interested in spiritual life must control their senses and elevate them to a spiritual platform.

The 26th verse. The meaning of this verse is the muni sense organs, breath, mind and intelligence were all restrained from material action. They were all restrained from physical activity and he was situated in a trance apart from the three, wakefulness, dream and unconsciousness, having achieved a transcendental position qualitatively equal with the supreme absolute. So, this is a very, very technical description of the state of consciousness. In the passage of the books, there is an analysis of Samika Rishi. He was externally, he appeared to be just sitting and doing some meditation. That's all. The way King Pariksit saw him in his ashram, in Samika Muni's ashram, he saw Samika Rishi sitting in meditation. But what was the real state of consciousness that is very precisely described in this verse? Okay. So, just before I go to the purport, let me just re-read the translation with sufficient pause for each of the different portions of this wonderful translation. The muni sense organs, indriya, prathiruddha indriya. Prathiruddha means it was restrained. From what? Restrained from what? From material engagement, from material contact, from material activities. It is just like our eyes or ears. Generally, what are they engaged in? In seeing material objects, in hearing different kinds of material sounds. So, they are engaged materially. That's a normal condition of any person in this world. But, those who are interested in spiritual life, in spiritual experience, they want to stop this material engagement and completely engage spiritually. So, the spiritual engagement is possible provided, first of all, we practice controlling, restraining these senses and gradually elevate these senses to the spiritual position, spiritual platform.

### dream state, unconscious state, subtle world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mI2xBQkS-uE?t=478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dream state is where we enter a subtle world."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the dream state (swapna avastha) and the unconscious state, explaining that in the dream state, we become unaware of our external surroundings and enter a subtle world, while in the unconscious state, there is no awareness of either the dream world or the external world.

Then there is the dream state. Swapna avastha. When we go to sleep at night, then you are aware, every night we experience this, but we become unaware of our external surroundings. Our external surroundings we become unaware. We don't know what is happening outside. So that is the state when we go to sleep and we are fast asleep. So then in that sleeping state, there are two different states of consciousness. One is called a dream state and other is called an unconscious state. Dream state is called in Sanskrit, swapna avastha. Swapna means dream. So in English it is translated by Prabhupada, swapna avastha is translated as dream state. So wakeful state, dream state and a third state, which is the other state in the sleeping condition, unconscious state. Sometimes it is called also as a deep sleep state. That means there is no awareness of a dream, but there is no awareness externally also. This dream state is where we enter a subtle world. V means the spirit soul inside this body is experiencing a certain state of awareness. State of awareness. So wakeful state is the state where basically the mind is interacting through the five senses with everything outside. And the dream state is where the external interaction is stopped because we go to sleep. Normally it is with closed eyes. And then not only we have closed our eyes, but we have also lost our touch or awareness of the external world. And basically the five senses. These five senses are the gateway for our interaction with the outside world. Is it not? So that is cut off when you go to sleep. And then in the sleeping something more happens. Not just merely closing the eyes or shutting the ears or shutting the breathing. No. Shutting the sense of smelling. Not breathing. Sense of smelling. Breathing goes on even when you are asleep. The sense of smelling is shut off kind of. So these five senses are shut off when we sleep. When we are in the dreaming state, we are aware or conscious of a dream world. We enter a dream world. That dream world is a subtle world. Subtle world means there are no gross objects.

### dream experience, memory, subtle experience
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mI2xBQkS-uE?t=679
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

**Summary:** The speaker continues describing the dream world as a subtle world perceived through the mind, and draws an analogy between remembering dreams and recalling events from the wakeful state, noting that not all experiences are easily remembered, but subtle experiences are present in the dream state.

There are no objects for the five senses. It is just the mind which is active and is through the mind. Through the mind we perceive a subtle world. And in that subtle world so many things happen. Just like after waking up from sleep, sometimes we remember while I was sleeping I experienced a dream or I witnessed a dream. I had a dream. Most people can relate with this. Of course you should remember, you should understand that always we don't remember the dream that we experience when we wake up. We don't always remember. Sometimes we remember. But it's a fact that every night when we go to sleep then we get into a dream state and we also experience we are unconscious with some time. Sometimes we are dreaming and sometimes we are unconscious. Both are there. For everyone. But we don't always remember the dream. Sometimes we remember. Just like to give you an example if I ask you ten days back in your wakeful state can you tell me at this time exactly what happened? Now it is 7.40 pm so exactly ten days back 7.40 pm what were you doing? Immediately you will not be able to recall. Even if you try to recall by going back ten days what was the day of the week? I should have been doing this. I must have been in this place. You try to build up the facts and then you try to still recollect anything significant happened you may try to extrapolate or build it up but you can't exactly remember that this particular thing happened unless it was something very very very significant, something very unusual, something very very different from the normal happening in our day to day life. So like that all dreams we don't remember. Similarly all just like all incidents in the wakeful state we don't remember. Similarly all dreams we don't remember. So dreaming is definitely there every time we go to sleep but all dreams we don't remember. Extraordinary dreams supposing one particular night you had a very bad dream or a very good dream, good and bad in relative terms of course dream is a dream that's all grossly nothing is happening neither anything in your external world is getting disturbed but subtle experience is there that is there in the dream state so that is dream state.

**Key Quote:** "This way you transcend the matter to perceive the senses the senses you transcend to subtle mind and mind to the intelligence and intelligence to ultimately realize the soul."
**Summary:** Using the examples of a desert mirage and the perceived size of the sun, the speaker explains how to transcend matter and the senses to realize the soul, concluding with the Muni's state of trance.

the perception of matter is transient is still subtler states of the senses just like normally when we see something we just aware of that object we are seeing we're not conscious that my eyes I am seeing the object and through my sense of understanding or knowing I am perceiving that object to be something so we are not aware of that when we become aware of the whole process of seeing just like if somebody is looking at a distance in a desert when they are walking they see at a distance there is a body of water that may not be a body of water because when they approach that there is no body of water it appears like at a distance of some 50 feet there is a body of water but when they cross 50 feet while walking there is no body of water it looked like there was a body of water that's called a mirage it's a optical vision optical illusion so that optical illusion when they become aware of that then they become aware of the defectiveness or the limitation of this sense of seeing like the Sun when it is rising you look in the horizon if you need to see the Sun it will appear a disc this big that's all that's all it will appear this big a disc but is the Sun that big no it's an optical illusion so this way you transcend the matter to perceive the senses the senses you transcend to subtle mind and mind to the intelligence and intelligence to ultimately realize the soul so this is the process and the Muni was sitting in that particular state of trance so I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna

In this session we will be discussing from two words, the 27th and the 28th verses of the 18th chapter of the first canto. So the 27th verse to begin this session. Vipra Kirna Jatachannam Rauravena Jinenacha Vishushyat Talur Udakam Tathabhutam Ayachata. The meaning of this verse is, the sage in meditation was covered by the skin of a stanchion and long compressed hair was scattered all over him. The king whose palate was dry from thirst asked him for water. So you just try to picturize what's happening. Pariksit Maharaj has gone hunting and he became very tired, thirsty and hungry and he started searching for water and he came across this muni's ashram. Shastra of the sage in meditation, Shamika Rishi's ashram.

### description, devotion, divine arrangement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fjRm5JlVs4k?t=115
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the scene, describing the sage's appearance and King Parikshit's thirst, highlighting the unexpected nature of the king's request for water from a sage in deep meditation, and emphasizing the divine arrangement behind the events.

He entered inside and saw the sage is sitting in meditation and the way king Pariksit saw the muni is being described here further, that the sage, that is Shamika Rishi, who was sitting in meditation was covered by skin of a stanchion. These rishis they don't wear regular clothes because they are in the forest. So the sage was sitting in meditation, so they cover themselves with some skin of an animal or a bark of a tree, something easily available and that need not be changed, that need not be washed, just because they sit for meditation for very long time. So they have no time to maintain all those things and long compressed hair was scattered all over him. There's no time to trim the hair or shave etc so the compressed hair is everywhere over his body and this king Parikshit he was very very thirsty so thirsty that his palate or his tongue was completely dry and he entered and he asked for water because he was very very thirsty so Srila Prabhupada says in the purport the king being thirsty asked the sage for water something very natural and very simple that such a great devotee and King asked for water from a sage absorbed in trance was certainly providential something unexpected something not expected from a devotee.

Parikshit is a devotee and he is aware that he is entering the ashram of a Rishi and the Rishi is sitting in meditation. This is an incident arranged by the Supreme Lord for Bhagavatam to be spoken by Shukadev Goswami on enquiry of Parikshit Maharaj. ### anger, neglect, etiquette
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fjRm5JlVs4k?t=324
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.28
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 28th verse, explaining that King Parikshit felt neglected due to the lack of basic hospitality and became angry, as he was not offered a seat, water, or kind words.

28th verse further description, Alabdha Trna Bhumi Adihi, Asam Prapt Argya Soondrata, Avagyatam Ivaatmanam Manya Manas Chukopah, the meaning of this 28th verse. The king not received by any formal welcome by means of being offered a seat, a place, water and sweet addresses, considered himself neglected and so thinking he became angry. This is describing King Parikshit. What was his reaction? He thought I have come to his Muni's ashram and the Muni is not angry. He is not angry. He is responding. He is indifferent to my having come here. He is not offering a seat or place to sit down. No welcome and no water and no sweet talk. So he considered that I have been neglected. Very basic etiquette is not being followed. I have been neglected and he became angry. So Prabhupada explains in the purport.

### Vedic culture, hospitality, etiquette
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fjRm5JlVs4k?t=409
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The law of reception in the courts of the Vedic principles state that even if an enemy is received at home he must be received with all respects."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the Vedic principles of hospitality, stating that even enemies should be treated with respect when they come to one's home, and provides the example of Jarasandha's reception of Krishna, Arjuna, and Bhima.

So the background for this verse being this this description by Suta Goswami. The law of reception in the courts of the Vedic principles state that even if an enemy is received at home he must be received with all respects. This is the Vedic culture. Whoever it is, when they come to your house, enemy or friend, a stranger or a known person, you have to receive that person with all respect. This is very important. After you get to the be all respects the visitor should not be given a chance to understand that he has come into the house of an enemy if he happens to be an enemy and proper gives an example here when Lord Krishna accompanied by Arjuna and Bhima approached Jarasandha in Magadha the respectable enemies were given a royal reception by King Jarasandha enemy means enemy camp Jarasandha belongs to the camp of the Asuras headed by Kamsa and Krishna Arjuna Bhima the Pandavas they belong to the camp of devotees and Supreme Lord obviously so when Krishna Arjuna and Bhima approached Jarasandha they were in disguise so even though they were in disguise when Jarasandha tried to inquire who are you because he could not recognize their external features they disguised themselves so then when they reveal their identity still Jarasandha understood these are my enemies but still he received them with a royal reception this is very cultural and it is also told in the Mahabharata the guest enemy namely Bhima was to fight with Jarasandha that the purpose for which Krishna Bhima and Arjuna had gone and yet they were given a grand reception so at night they used to sit down together as friends and guests and in the daytime they used to fight risking life and death that was a law of reception in their fighting and one of them will be defeated and killed still that is only due to the daytime the fight is going on Bhima versus Jarasandha at night the fight is not concluded at the end of day one day two day three four for so many days so when the fight has not been decided they at the night they will be like Bhima Arjuna and Krishna are guests of Jarasandha.

he still offers a good reception simply by offering a straw mat straw mat means some straws which are available anywhere they just woven together and made into a sitting mat so straw mat some water to drink water is available everywhere you know poor man is plenty of water earlier there is to live always by a water source either a river or a nice pond or lake or a nice well and plenty of water always there is water so water even a poorest of poor man can offer a straw mat he can offer to the guest he can offer sweet words welcome to my humble abode please come sit down accept some water expect a royal reception by the Rishi because he knew that saints and Rishis are not material rich persons they are living in an ashram they are not material rich persons they are living in an ashram they are living in an ashram they are not material rich persons they are living in an ashram some hut and where can they offer royal reception even the king because King deserves a royal reception but how can they offer a royal reception so Pariksit did not expect a royal reception but he never expected that a seed of straw a glass of water and some sweet words would be denied to him he was not an ordinary guest nor was he an enemy of the Rishi and therefore the cold reception with the Rishi was not an ordinary guest nor was he an enemy of the Rishi and therefore the cold reception with the Rishi was not an ordinary guest nor was he an enemy of the Rishi and astonished the King greatly why am I astonished the King greatly why am I being denied even some basic reception I being denied That's what thinking of Pariksit Maharaj is so going by the etiquette that is expected the king was right to get angry with the Rishi when he needed a glass of water very badly so Papa says in the elecmosis to become angry in such a grave situation was not unnatural for the king but this special circumstance here what is that because the King himself was not less than a great saint he was saintly king rajarshi Pariksit was not ordinary king his becoming angry and taking action were astonishing very the muni is sitting in trance he is engaged in spiritual activities he is cut off from external sense perception he is absorbed within completely and you expect that such a meditating sage he's going to offer you some reception he's going to give some water he's conscious that you have come to his ashram no so why is King Pariksit becoming angry that is a question that should be raised so proper says it must be accepted that this situation was ordained by the supreme will of Lord Krishna the king was a great devotee of the Lord and the saint that is Samika Rishi was also as good as the king in saintly conduct but by the will of the Lord the circumstances.

Are so created that they became ways to the kings becoming unattached to family connection and governmental activities and thus becoming a surrendered soul unto the lotus feet of Lord Krishna the events led to that and that we described in the next few verses the merciful Lord sometimes creates such awkward position for his pure devotees in order to drag the pure devotee towards himself from the mire of material existence mire means that entangling situations in this material world the Lord wants to pull out every one of us but the pure devotees is particularly conscious come out quickly out of this material existence and come back to me outwardly the situations appear to be frustrating to the devotees but the devotees of the Lord are always under the protection of the Lord and in any condition frustration of success the Lord is a supreme guide for the devotees devotees understand if there is some frustrating situation they know it is the arrangement of the Lord Lord has got a higher plan otherwise the devotees are never put in frustrating situation in inconvenience they execute the devotional service peacefully but sometimes in spite of their endeavor to execute devotional service they put in a situation where is difficulty even for doing devotional service and that is the Lord has got a higher plan devotees therefore accept all conditions of frustration as blessings from the Lord and that's what happened in the case of Parikshit Maharaj I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 29th and the 30th verses of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers and an introduction to the verses that will be discussed: SB 1.18.29-30.

Om Agyana Timiraandhasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chakshurunmilitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 29th and the 30th verses of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam.

---
### Anger, Devotee Behavior, Unprecedented Event
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o537ZG7tTb0?t=45
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The king's anger and envy directed toward the Brahmana sage were unprecedented, being that circumstances had made him hungry and thirsty."
**Summary:** The speaker explains verse 29, highlighting the unprecedented nature of Maharaj Parikshit's anger towards a Brahmana sage, emphasizing that such behavior from a devotee king was extremely rare.

The 29th verse: Abhutapurvahasahasa Kshutrbhyam Ardhitatmanah Brahmanam Prati Abhut Brahman Matsaro Manyurevacha. The meaning of this 29th verse: O Brahmanas, this is Suta Goswami addressing the sages of Naimisharanya while describing the incidents leading to Parikshit Maharaj becoming angry at the Shamika Rishi. So the king's anger and envy directed toward the Brahmana sage were unprecedented, being that circumstances had made him hungry and thirsty. Unprecedented means such a thing has never happened in the past: a devotee king approaching a Brahmana sage who is absorbed in meditation, expecting that I will get some reception, I will get some water, I will get some relief from my thirst, when he was extremely thirsty and hungry, not getting anything, he became frustrated, he became angry, so this is unprecedented. The Parpochala Prabhupada says for a king like Maharaj Parikshit to become angry and enraged, envious, especially at a sage and Brahmana, was undoubtedly unprecedented. Never has such a thing happened in the past. It is very, very, very rare. It is as they say, rarest of rare situations.

---
### Vedic Culture, Protection, Social Order
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o537ZG7tTb0?t=161
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is again the Vedic culture that a king protects his citizens very nicely, and among the citizens, he gives special care and protection to five categories of the citizens."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the Vedic culture where the king protects citizens, with special care given to Brahmanas, sages, children, women, and old men, explaining the reasons for their special protection and the king's role in ensuring their well-being.

Extraordinary situation. The king knew well that Brahmanas, sages, children, women, and old men are always beyond the jurisdiction of punishment. This is again the Vedic culture that a king protects his citizens very nicely, and among the citizens, he gives special care and protection to five categories of the citizens. Who are they? First is Brahmanas. Brahmanas. And that Brahmanas includes the cows also. Brahmanical culture, Govabrahmanahitayacha. Krishna is described as Govabrahmanahitayacha. He is always very much concerned about the well being of Brahmanas and cows. Brahmanahiya means those who are devotees. Second is sages. Sages are saintly persons, those who are dedicated for spiritual life, Sanyasis, or Rishis, or Trans-Elders of any kind, Yogis etc. So they are also given special protection by the king. They are dedicated completely for spiritual life. A Brahmana is dedicated for giving the service in the society to the other classes of people, the Kshatriyas, Vaishyas and Shudras. They always depend on the Brahmanas for getting spiritual guidance, for any spiritual duties, for any rituals to be performed, for any spiritual knowledge, etc. The third category of protection, special protection for citizens is the children. The children are small, so they have to be given extra protection, and women. Women. Women are naturally the weaker section of the society, the men are stronger, so women require special protection. Generally they are protected by their husbands or father or grown up sons, but in case somebody is all alone, then the king must be protected, then the king must give special protection for such women who don't have a husband or they are a widow or they are somehow an orphan or they are not able to get any protection from any of the elders, then the king has to give special protection for such women and old men, very aged, and they don't have a guardian, they don't have grown up son or they don't have a guardian who can protect them, so king has to give special protection for such categories of people and not only he gives protection, but he also understands if they make a mistake, it is pardonable because they are in a special category, so they are to be given a concession that they make some small mistake, you just don't apply the law for such categories of personalities.

---
### King's Authority, Divine Will, Justification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o537ZG7tTb0?t=398
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The only justification for Maharaj Pariksit's behaviour is that it was ordained by the lord, the lord arranged this."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the concept that a king is generally considered to be above reproach, and even if Shamika Rishi made a mistake, he shouldn't be punished. He concludes that Parikshit's anger was due to the will of the Lord, setting the stage for future events.

Similarly, the king even though he commits a great mistake is never to be considered a wrong doer. So on one side is Shamika Rishi, who is a saintly person, he should never be punished even if he has made a mistake, he has not done a mistake, even if he has done a mistake, he is not punishable by the king. Other side is the king, the king can do no wrong, the king can do no wrong. See even Shamika Rishi should not have become, not Shamika Rishi, in this case, the Shamika Rishi's son should not have become angry at the king because king being the protector and the well wisher of all the citizens, if he makes a minor mistake, that should be excused by everyone else. So king is never considered a wrong doer. In this case, Maharaj Pariksit became angry and envious at the sage due to his thirst and hunger by the will of the lord. So here the will of the lord, the lord is arranging the situation, extraordinary situation for Bhagavatam we spoke. So the king was right to punish his subject for coldly receiving him or neglecting him, but because the culprit was a sage and a brahmana it was unprecedented. Normal person if he does not receive the king properly, in order to teach that person a lesson that you should receive people properly, he may give him some punishment but not in the case of this brahmana who is sitting in sage sitting in meditation. As the lord is never envious of anyone so also the lord's devotee is never envious of anyone. So Maharaj Pariksit is a devotee, he can't be envious at the sage, not out of envy he became angry. The only justification for Maharaj Pariksit's behaviour is that it was ordained by the lord, the lord arranged this, Pariksit's unusual behaviour and the brahmana's son Karsing that we will hear later what happened after this.

---
### Insult, Retaliation, Divine Arrangement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o537ZG7tTb0?t=536
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.30
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The king thus treated the sage tit for tat: you didn't receive me properly, so I also want to teach you a lesson that you are neglecting basic etiquette."
**Summary:** The speaker explains verse 30, describing how Parikshit Maharaj, feeling insulted, placed a dead snake on the sage's shoulder as a form of retaliation, but emphasizes that this action was also part of the Lord's plan.

The 30th verse: Satu Brahma Risher Amse Gatasum Uragam Rusha Minir Dacchan Dhanush Kotya Nidhaya Puram Agatah. The meaning of this 30th verse is while leaving, the king was not received, he was very thirsty, he didn't get water, he became angry what did he do? While leaving that place, being so insulted, he was feeling insulted, he picked up a lifeless snake, a dead snake how did he pick up? With his bow, with his bow because he was he had come hunting he was bow and arrows bow in one hand, so that bow he picked up a dead snake and angrily placed it on the shoulder of the sage and returned to his palace. This is the 30th verse. So Prabhupada says that in the purport, the king thus treated the sage tit for tat: you didn't receive me properly, so I also want to teach you a lesson that you are neglecting basic etiquette. So he took one dead snake and put it on the shoulder of the sage and said what is this, he is garlanding the snake at the sage. It is a way of I was insulted, so I also insult you, that tit for tat. Although the king was never accustomed to such silly actions, why should he take so seriously the sage not receiving him under the circumstances? I have not been given water and I am very thirsty, do not think like that, but a will of the lord the king while going away found a dead snake in front of him. Again the dead snake, it is not that it is so common to find dead snakes everywhere no. So one dead snake was there, happened to be there nearby and while going away he thought the sage who had coldly received him might be coldly rewarded by being offered a garland of a dead snake. Basically I was insulted, so I also insult him, take one dead snake which just happened to be lying there and put it on his shoulder and just go away. In the ordinary course of dealing this was not very unnatural.

---
### Unprecedented Event, Divine Will, Bhagavatam's Purpose
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/o537ZG7tTb0?t=696
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is something that Krishna has arranged with a higher plan, so that we have to always understand by the descriptions being given here by Suta Goswami and it is going to lead to that."
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates the unprecedented nature of the event, emphasizing that it was orchestrated by the Lord for a higher purpose, and that the details will be further explained in the next video.

That means it is expected only if somebody is not received they become angry and they react, but in the case of Maharaj Pariksha dealing with a brahmana sage, this was certainly unprecedented. Never such a thing would have happened in the interaction between a devotee king and a sage who is a brahmana who is situated in trance who is very, very advanced yogi who is absorbed in meditation, cut off from external awareness. It is not expected such a thing would happen, so it so happened by the will of the lord. This being stressed throughout by Prabhupada, it is something that Krishna has arranged with a higher plan, so that we have to always understand by the descriptions being given here by Suta Goswami and it is going to lead to that and the details should be further described in the next video in a few verses. So I will stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

## SB 1.18.3 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 26-03-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse

### death, detachment, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=53
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.3
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He realized that he has to prepare for death whenever death might come; death cannot be totally avoided when the material body is there."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam and explains how Parikshit Maharaj, upon hearing the curse, decided to prepare for death by surrendering to Shukadeva Goswami.

In this session, we will be discussing from the 3rd verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Utsarijya Sarvata Sangam Vijnata Ajita Samsthitihi Vaya Saker Jahu Shishyo Gangayam Swam Kalevaram. The meaning of this verse is, furthermore, after leaving all his associates, the king surrendered himself as a disciple to the son of Vyasa, Shukadeva Goswami, and thus he was able to understand the actual position of the personality of Godhead. So, R. S. Goswami is narrating the events leading to Maharaj Pariksit being cursed by a Braamana boy, so before describing the actual incident, it is being described here that Pariksit Maharaj, in his life, is charming in a lot of ways. Maharaj he heard the curse and he decided to prepare for quitting the body.

For death by the bite of the snake bird, even though he could have contracted the curse, because he realized that he has to prepare for death whenever death might come. Death cannot be totally avoided when the material body is there. The body cannot be kept forever, this material body, so death means we'll quit this material body and spirit soul. So that Parikshit Maharaj knew very well, so he said, let me prepare for quitting this body by becoming Krishna conscious. Therefore, he surrendered himself as a disciple to Shukadeva Goswami.

### Ajita, Krishna, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=170
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.3
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The personality of God, Shri Krishna, is known as Ajita or unconquerable."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the significance of the word 'Ajita' as a name of Krishna, referring to the Kurma Avatara and emphasizing that the Lord's actual position is unconquerable and cannot be known by ordinary people.

And thus he was able to understand the actual position of the personality of God. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, the word Ajita is significant here. The personality of God, Shri Krishna, is known as Ajita or unconquerable, and so in every respect this word Ajita is a name of the Supreme Lord. Elsewhere in the Bhagavatam, it is described that the Supreme Lord appeared as Ajita when the churning of the milk ocean was going on, it was about to begin, and in order to favor the Devatas who wanted to produce nectar by churning the milk ocean, he assumed the form of a tortoise, Kurma Rupa or Kurma Avatara. The tortoise incarnation, it was Lord Narayana who appeared first as Ajita. He became the Kurma Avatara and he supported the Mandara mountain on his back because the Mandara mountain was used as a churning rod by the Devatas and the Asooras to churn the milk ocean and produce nectar. So that same name is used here to describe the Supreme Lord. Why? Why is he called Ajita? He is unconquerable in every respect. No one can know his actual position. Why is he called Achitha? No one can know his actual position ever. Why is Ajita used?

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that Parikshit Maharaj could understand the Lord's position because it cannot be understood by people in general, and that the Supreme Lord is unconquerable by knowledge alone.

Because Parikshit Maharaj could understand the actual position of the Lord, whose position cannot be understood by people in general, ordinarily. So, the Supreme Lord is unconquerable. He is unconquerable by knowledge also. So, that means if somebody tries to undergo cultivation of knowledge, accumulating knowledge, getting knowledge more and more and more and more of the absolute truth or about God, simply by acquiring knowledge, any amount of knowledge about God, you cannot understand the actual position of God, not possible.

### Goloka Vrindavana, spiritual master, confidential knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=341
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By the grace of a spiritual master like Shukadev Goswami, unto whom the king gave himself up as a most humble disciple, one is able to understand the actual position of the Lord, his eternal abode, Goloka Vrindavana."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that while scholars may interpret Goloka Vrindavana differently, one can understand the actual position of the Lord and His abode through the grace of a spiritual master like Shukadev Goswami, to whom one surrenders as a humble disciple.

Now, Srila Prabhupada further explains, we have heard about his dharma, the Supreme Lord's eternal abode, Goloka Vrindavana. But there are many scholars who interpret this abode in different ways. What is this abode of the Lord, Goloka Vrindavana? Different scholars say, explain it differently. But by the grace of a spiritual master like Shukadev Goswami, unto whom the king gave himself up as a most humble disciple, one is able to understand the actual position of the Lord, his eternal abode, Goloka Vrindavana, and his transcendental paraphernalia in that dharma or abode. These things are very, very confidential knowledge. Such highly confidential knowledge cannot be realized simply by scholarship.

### Mayavadis, impersonalism, spiritual form
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=450

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the understanding of Goloka Vrindavana with the impersonalistic view of the Mayavadis, who believe God is formless and without variegatedness.

Knowing the transcendental position of the Lord and the transcendental method by which one can approach the transcendental dharma, the king was confident about his ultimate destination. So this Goloka Vrindavana is not some impersonal situation as the Mayavadis think. The Mayavadis, their philosophy is, God is formless, and if there is any description of kingdom of God, that is also formless. And everything about the Absolute Truth is ultimately without any variegatedness, without any name, without any personality, without any place, without any situation, nothing. So, it is basically some concoction of these Mayavadi philosophers or scholars, what is, what is the kingdom of God, or who is God, or what is the meaning in descriptions of God's form, paraphernalia, his abode, his associates, his qualities, what is the meaning in those descriptions, the Mayavadis don't have any understanding.

### detachment, Krishna Loka, material attachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=618
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you know that you don't belong to this world, you know that you have to quit this body sometime, you know that you as spirit-soul are eternal, then what do you do about all this material paraphernalia?"
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the understanding of Parikshit Maharaj, who understood the personality of Godhead and fixed his destination as Krishna Loka, with those who are attached to material possessions and relationships.

But Parikshit Maharaj, when he understood the personality of Godhead, he was able to understand the transcendental position of the Lord and the method by which one can approach that transcendental dharma. And the king was confident about his ultimate destination. That means he fixed his destination as I will go back to Krishna Loka, Krishna Dhamma. And by knowing this, he could leave aside everything material, even his, even his own body without any difficulty of attachment. If you know that you don't belong to this world, you know that you have to quit this body sometime, you know that you as spirit-soul are eternal, then what do you do about all this material paraphernalia, the material body, the material possessions, material relationship, what do you do with all that? So, Parikshit Maharaj clearly knew that he has to leave everything at the time of death. So before death comes, he cut off all attachments to all these various things.

### detachment, Krishna Katha, superior energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=708
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One can give up all connection with material attachment when one is able to see the param or the superior quality of things."
**Summary:** The speaker mentions that the second canto will describe how Parikshit Maharaj cut off all attachments and came to hear from Shukadeva Goswami in the Krishna Katha, referencing the Bhagavad Gita's statement about giving up material attachments by seeing the superior quality of things.

That will be described in the second canto, how Parikshit Maharaj, Parikshit Maharaj cut off all attachments. Shukadeva Goswami, the description of the meeting of Parikshit Maharaj and Shukadeva Goswami, in that description, there will be details about how Parikshit Maharaj quit his kingdom, gave up all attachments and came to hear from Shukadeva Goswami in the Krishna Katha, in the form of Bhagavad. In the Bhagavad Gita, it is stated, param drishtva nivartate, one can give up all connection with material attachment when one is able to see the param or the superior quality of things. The Prabhupada is explaining how Parikshit was able to give up. From Bhagavad Gita, we understand the quality of the Lord's energy that is superior to the material quality of energy and by the grace of a bona fide spiritual master like Shukadeva Goswami, it is quite possible to know everything of the superior energy of the Lord by which the Lord manifests his eternal name, eternal quality, eternal pastimes, eternal paraphernalia and variegatedness.

### spiritual variety, material attachments, theoretical knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=793
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Unless one thoroughly understands this superior or eternal energy of the Lord, it is not possible to leave the material energy."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that unless one understands the superior energy of the Lord, it is impossible to leave the material energy, and that theoretical speculation alone is insufficient.

Unless one thoroughly understands this superior or eternal energy of the Lord, it is not possible to leave the material energy. However, one may theoretically speculate on the true nature of the Absolute Truth. See, there are transcendentalists who are not devotees, but they know ultimately Absolute Truth is spiritual. They know that, but they do not know the nature of the Absolute Truth in terms of the spiritual variegatedness. So, because they do not know of the spiritual variegatedness, they are unable to give up this material variety, the material attachments, they are unable to give up. So, Bhagavad Gita says, param drishtva nivartate, one can give up material attachments or material paraphernalia, material connections, material relationships, if one really knows and one realizes what is spiritual variety, what is spiritual abode, what is spiritual name, what is spiritual form, until one realizes that there is no possibility of giving up any of the material varieties. Simply by theoretical knowledge you cannot.

### mercy, liberated devotee, Shukadeva Goswami
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/7LOKJQugbb4?t=881
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is very difficult to find the Lord through Vedic literatures, but it is very easy to know the Supreme Lord by the mercy of a liberated devotee like Shukadeva Goswami."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that Parikshit Maharaj was able to know the actual position of the unconquerable Lord through the mercy of Shukadeva Goswami, emphasizing that it is easier to know the Lord through a liberated devotee than through Vedic literatures alone.

By the grace of Lord Krishna, Maharaj Pariksit was able to receive the mercy of such a personality as Shukadeva Goswami and thus he was able to know the actual position of the unconquerable Lord. It is very difficult to find the Lord through Vedic literatures, but it is very easy to know the Supreme Lord by the mercy of a liberated devotee like Shukadeva Goswami. There is only way and there is no other way. And Pariksit was fortunate to meet Shukadeva Goswami and learn from him and realize the Supreme Lord by the mercy of Shukadeva Goswami. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

**Summary:** The speaker explains how Shukadeva Goswami appeared and spoke the Srimad Bhagavatam to Pariksit, emphasizing the significance of the Rasaleela pastime in destroying mundane desires.

what was that preparation? Shukadeva Goswami who appeared in that place where Pariksit was sitting in order to prepare for his death. Pariksit received Shukadeva Goswami nicely and began to inquire from him what should I do to prepare for my death and Shukadeva Goswami recommended that you sit and hear the glories of the Supreme Lord Krishna and he began to speak the Shukadeva Bhagavatam which he had learned from his father Vyasadeva who had combined the Bhagavatam that is spoken by Shukadeva to Pariksit and in that way Bhagavatam came into being.
