### divine arrangement, frustration, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NemEJCgqxdI?t=676
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Apparently Parikshit was frustrated therefore he did this. But actually it was arranged by the Lord that his devotee becomes an instrument for Bhagavatam to prosper."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Pariksit's frustration was part of a divine arrangement for the Bhagavatam to prosper, drawing a parallel with Arjuna's frustration leading to the Bhagavad Gita.

The pure devotee's mundane frustration is meant to elevate the devotee to a higher transcendental position. Apparently Parikshit was frustrated therefore he did this. But actually it was arranged by the Lord that his devotee becomes an instrument for Bhagavatam to prosper. Now Prabhupada gives another example of this. By placing Arjuna and the Pandavas in frustration due to the intrigue of their cousin brothers, the prelude of the Bhagavad or the battle of Kurukshetra was created by the Lord. This was to incarnate the sound representative of the Lord Bhagavad Gita.

---
### Bhagavad Gita, divine plan, Srimad Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NemEJCgqxdI?t=720
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Brahma says by placing King Pariksit in an awkward position, the incarnation of Sriman Bhagavatam was created by the will of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the divine plan behind the Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam, emphasizing that Krishna orchestrated Arjuna's and Pariksit's situations to deliver these scriptures.

See even Bhagavad Gita Krishna spoke on the battlefield of Kurukshetra just about when the battle is about to begin. Now that is because Arjuna was put in a position where he had to fight against his own cousins due to the frustration by the due to the frustration the Pandavas had to undergo due to the harassment of this Duryodhana and Pandavas. Unnecessary harassment. So then war became essential. They were deprived of their kingdom. But even before fighting the battle, when all peace negotiations failed, Arjuna being a pure devotee, he was considering should I fight this battle and kill my kinsmen simply because we want the kingdom and as a pure devotee he is not interested in having a big knife and big kingdom. No. So Krishna spoke the Bhagavad Gita to actually address the problem of Arjuna. But that was primarily Krishna only who put Arjuna in that situation. Sir Krishna could speak through Arjuna to all of us the Bhagavad Gita. So Brahma says by placing King Pariksit in an awkward position, the incarnation of Sriman Bhagavatam was created by the will of the Lord.

### divine arrangement, Pariksit Maharaj, Srimad Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NemEJCgqxdI?t=826
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The situation in this case is therefore all planned by the Lord. This is the conclusion."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes that Pariksit's distress was a divine arrangement, highlighting his endurance and the Lord's plan for the Bhagavatam to be spoken.

You see, being distressed by hunger and thirst was only a show because the King endured much even in the womb of his mother. See the history of Pariksit is when he was in the womb of his mother, one Brahmastra was released by Ashwathama to kill that child in the womb. But Pariksit endured that experience. Endured that extreme heat of the Brahmastra. And if he could endure so much of extreme condition, a little thirst while hunting, why he couldn't endure? That's the argument. The King's distressed condition was certainly unprecedented. The devotees like Maharaj Pariksit are powerful enough to forbear self-distress by the will of the Lord. And they are never disturbed. The situation in this case is therefore all planned by the Lord. This is the conclusion. So the Acharyas are telling us that Pariksit was not at all at fault because of an arrangement of the Lord so that Bhagavatam could be spoken by Shukadeva Swami to Pariksit Maharaj, Pariksit Maharaj and their relevant inquiries for such a Bhagavad Gita.

## SB 1.18.32-33 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 21-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, introduction, context
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** introduction
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In this session we discussed the 32nd and 33rd verses of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Shreemad Bhagavatam."
**Summary:** The speaker begins with prayers and introduces the topic of discussion: verses 32 and 33 of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Maharaj Pariksit being cursed by a Brahmana boy.

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananjali Shalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvichesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we discussed the 32nd and 33rd verses of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Shreemad Bhagavatam. This chapter is entitled Maharaj Pariksit Cursed by a Brahmana Boy. The incidents leading to this cursing are being discussed in these verses. So the 32nd verse.

---
### brahminical power, kali yuga, deterioration
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=77
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But because the age of Kali was seeking an opportunity to spoil the culture to heritage of the four orders of life, the inexperienced boy gave a chance for the age of Kali to enter into the field of Vedic culture."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of verse 32, focusing on the power of the Brahmana boy Sringi, the influence of Kali Yuga, and the beginning of the deterioration of Vedic culture.

Tasyaputro Atitejasvi Viharan Balako Arbhakaihi Rajnagam Praapitam Tatam Shrutvata Tredam Abhravit. The meaning of these 32nd verses: This sage had a son who was very powerful, being a Brahmana's son. While he was playing with inexperienced boys, he heard of his father's distress just occasioned by the king. Then and there, the boys started to play with him. Indeed, In the Purport Shrila Prabhupada says, due to Maharaj Pariksit's good government, even a boy of tender age who was playing with other inexperienced boys could become as powerful as a qualified Brahmana. This boy was known as Sringi, and he achieved good training in Brahmacharya by his father. He could be as powerful as a brahmana even at that age. But because the age of Kali was seeking an opportunity to spoil the culture to heritage of the four orders of life, the inexperienced boy gave a chance for the age of Kali to enter into the field of Vedic culture. Hatred of the lower orders of life began from this brahmana boy under the influence of Kali and thus cultural life began to dwindle day after day. So this is the explanation given here.

---
### kali yuga, protection, maharaj pariksit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=190
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we want to protect ourselves against the evil influence of Kali, we should employ 50% of our wealth in propagating Sankirtana."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on how Kali Yuga deteriorates society, focusing on the degradation of Brahmanas and their mistreatment of other social orders. He then recounts the story of Maharaj Pariksit's encounter with Kali and how he initially restricted Kali's influence, emphasizing the importance of using wealth for spiritual purposes like Sankirtan to counter Kali's influence.

How the age of Kali things deteriorate to earth. To a great extent, that the brahmanas who are supposed to be the head of the society, they become degraded and they look down upon the other sections of society. Just like this boy, he wrongly thinks that he is the head of the society. He thought that the king had overstepped his position and he cursed the king. That's going to be explained later in the next few verses. He shouldn't have cursed the king, but he did so. And it happened under the influence of Kali. He was still seeking a chance to enter into the field of Vedic culture, as Shruta Prabhupada says here. The first victim of brahmanical injustice was Maharaj Pariksit. And thus, the protection given by the king against the onslaught of Kali was slackened. We should remember how Kali was trying to enter the kingdom of Pariksit. And Pariksit prevented that by, first of all, threatening to kill Kali because Kali Purusha was harming the coven bull. And when Kali Purusha surrendered, Pariksit Maharaj had to give shelter as a king. So he said, you cannot stay in my kingdom because you are a friend of a religion. And then Kali Purusha was... Kali Purusha was given, still, when he begged for some place, he was given four places, places where meat-eating, intoxication, gambling and illicit sex are going on. And Kali Purusha could not find any such place in Pariksit's kingdom. So good was Pariksit's rule. And then Kali Purusha was given a fifth place when he couldn't find any place. He was given the place where gold was found. And gold is accumulated without being used for spiritual purposes. And that way Kali Purusha was given some place. But even then, Pariksit protected the citizens by engaging their stored-up wealth, stored-up gold in Sankirtan Yagna.

---
### protection, kali yuga, injustice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=379
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Oh, just look at the sins of the rulers who, like crows and watchdogs at the door, perpetrate sins against their mothers."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of using wealth for Sankirtan to protect against Kali's influence. He then transitions to verse 33, explaining Shringi's angry reaction and his derogatory comparison of rulers to crows and watchdogs who betray their masters.

If we want to protect ourselves against the evil influence of Kali, we should employ 50% of our wealth in propagating Sankirtana. So this way Kali cannot influence the people of this age. So here, the description is that Maharaj Pariksit was the victim of Brahmanical injustice. Wrongly, he was cursed. And that's how the onslaught of Kali was slacked through this injustice. The 33rd words, Aho Adharma Palaanam. Peevnam Balibhujamiva. Swamin Yagam Yad Dasanam. Dvarapanam Shunamiva. The meaning of this 33rd verse is, The Brahmana's son Shungi said, Oh, just look at the sins of the rulers who, like crows and watchdogs at the door, perpetrate sins against their mothers. Oh, just look at the sins of the rulers who, like crows and watchdogs at the door, perpetrate sins against their mothers. Contrary to the principles governing servants. Contrary to the principles governing servants.

---
### social order, varnashrama, brahmanas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=476
**Verse References:** BG 4.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That is a natural arrangement made by the supreme order for it is confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita that four social orders or castes namely the brahmanas, the kshatriyas, the vaisyas and shudras are set up according to quality and work done by them."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Shringi's anger at the king's perceived insult to his father. He then delves into the social order, comparing the Brahmanas to the head and brain, and the Kshatriyas to the arms, emphasizing the importance of the head guiding the arms. He references the Bhagavad Gita to support the idea that the four social orders are based on quality and work.

So Shungi, this Brahmana's son, when he heard that his father was insulted by the king, who put a dead snake around his father's neck, so he became very, very angry. And what is he saying? Look at these rulers. What is their position? They are like crows and watchdogs at the door of the Brahmanas. And instead of behaving properly as a servant of the Brahmanas, they are perpetrating sins against their masters. So in the parpochana Prabhupada says, The Brahmanas are considered to be the head and brains of the social body. And the Chakras are considered to be the arms of the social body. The arms are required to protect the body from all harm. But the arms must act according to the directions of the head and brain. See the comparison is made, the orders, the spiritual orders, or the social orders. Brahmana, Chakra, and Vaishya, Shudra. They are compared to the four parts of our own body. It's like we have got the head and brain, we got the arms, we have got the belly and we got the legs. So brahmanas are compared to the head and brains of the social body. Most important. And kshatriyas are considered to be the arms. So the arms are required to protect the body from all harm but the arms must act according to the directions of the head and brain. That's how this body is organized. Same way society should be organized like that. That is a natural arrangement made by the supreme order for it is confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita that four social orders or castes namely the brahmanas, the kshatriyas, the vaisyas and shudras are set up according to quality and work done by them. This is the statement of the Bhagavad Gita, the fourth chapter. Krishna explains this.

---
### qualification, brahmanas, kali yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=626
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Herein Shrunge, a qualified son of a great brahmana, attained the required brahmanical power both by birth and by training. But he was lacking in culture because he was an inexperienced boy."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the importance of both birth and qualification in becoming a Brahmana, emphasizing that simply being born into a Brahmana family is not enough. He criticizes the modern trend of people claiming to be Brahmanas without proper training or qualifications. He then returns to the story of Shringi, pointing out that while he had the power of a Brahmana, he lacked the culture and experience to use it wisely, leading him to inappropriately compare Maharaj Pariksit to crows and watchdogs.

So according to guna and karma it has to be set up. Naturally the son of a brahmana has a good chance to become a brahmana by the direction of his qualified father as the son of a brahmana. The son of a brahmana or a medical practitioner has a very good chance to become a qualified medical practitioner. So the caste system is quite scientific. The son must take advantage of the father's qualification and thus become a brahmana or a medical practitioner and not otherwise. Without being qualified one cannot become a brahmana or a medical practitioner and that is the verdict of all scriptures and social orders. You see, it is true that the son of a brahmana has a good opportunity to learn from his father and become a qualified brahmana. But without the qualification he cannot claim I am a brahmana. What is happening nowadays? People are claiming to be brahmanas without being trained, without being qualified. And that is where the whole system has become topsy-turvy. So Prabhupada points out here, Herein Shrunge, a qualified son of a great brahmana, attained the required brahmanical power both by birth and by training. But he was lacking in culture because he was an inexperienced boy. Even though he was powerful, he was not sufficiently experienced how to utilize the power that he had acquired. By the influence of Kali, the son of a brahmana became puffed up with brahmanical power and thus wrongly compared Maharaj Pariksit to crows and watchdogs. This comparison is completely inappropriate.

---
### downfall, brahmanas, birthright
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=756
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Thus the downfall of the brahmanical powers began as they gave importance to birthright without culture."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to discuss Shringi's inappropriate comparison, emphasizing that while a king is a protector, he should not be treated as a low-class servant. He highlights that the downfall of the Brahmanical order began when they prioritized birthright over culture, education, training, qualification, and experience. He states that the Kali Yuga began 5000 years ago, and that is when unqualified people started claiming to be Brahmanas based solely on birthright, leading to a loss of respect and a topsy-turvy social order.

Just because this boy, inexperienced boy, was very proud of his brahmanical power. The king is certainly the watchdog of the state in the sense that he keeps vigilant eyes over the border of the state for its protection and defense. But to address him as a watchdog is a sign of a less cultured boy. He is supposed to protect, he is supposed to govern the country or the state or the province. But he cannot be treated like a low-class servant the way Sringi is thinking that the king has become an upstart. Thus the downfall of the brahmanical powers began as they gave importance to birthright without culture. If a brahmana is uncultured then he may wrongly claim to be a brahmana simply by birthright. I am born in a brahmana family therefore I should be treated as a brahmana. But what about your culture? What about your education? What about your training? What about your qualification? What about your experience? All these are important factors. The downfall of the brahmana caste began in the age of Kali. According to scriptures 5000 years back Kali Yuga began and that is when unqualified people started claiming this because of birthright that we are brahmanas we should be respected as brahmanas by all the others. Whereas the others are observing these brahmanas are not qualified and they are wrongly claiming to be brahmanas. So they started losing respect and that is when everything became topsy-turvy.

---
### deterioration, social order, future discussion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XMxOjTKKrGM?t=883
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** summary
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This beginning of brahmanical deterioration was highly deplored by the father Shringi as we will find."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that because Brahmanas are the heads of the social order, their deterioration leads to the deterioration of all other orders. He explains that unqualified Brahmanas cannot give proper directions to the other orders, leading to a decline in society. He mentions that Shringi's father deplored this deterioration and that his reaction will be discussed in the next few verses, before ending the session with a thank you and Hare Krishna.

Since brahmanas are the heads of the social order all other orders of society also began to deteriorate. The brahmana should give directions to the other orders of society which are triyas, vashyas and shudras. But brahmanas themselves are not qualified. How can they give directions proper directions to the other orders? So the other orders also start deteriorating. This beginning of brahmanical deterioration was highly deplored by the father Shringi as we will find. So what is Shringi's father's reaction that we will see in the next few verses. I will stop here. Thank you. Hare Krishna. Thank you.

### Curse, Anger, Brahminical Power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.36
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The son of the Rishi, his eyes red hot with anger touched the water of the river Kaushiki while speaking to his playmates and discharged the following thunderbolt of words."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the context of the lecture, focusing on verse 36 of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, which describes the son of a Rishi cursing someone out of anger.

In this session we will be discussing from two verses, the 36th and the 37th verses of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. The 36th verse is as follows: Ityuktva Roshatam Raksho Vayasyan Rishi Balakah Kaushiki Aapa Upas Prishya Vagvajram Visasarjaha. The meaning of this verse is: The son of the Rishi, his eyes red hot with anger touched the water of the river Kaushiki while speaking to his playmates and discharged the following thunderbolt of words.

---
### Parikshit, Insult, Anger, Curse
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=104
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.36
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the son of this Rishi, Shringi heard that his father was insulted by the king, the boy Shringi became very angry against this insult by the king of his father, insult of his father and he decided to curse him, curse the king."
**Summary:** The speaker provides the background story of King Parikshit's actions that led to the curse by Shringi, the son of Shamika Rishi, emphasizing Shringi's anger and decision to curse the king.

So the background for this is that Parikshit while hunting became very thirsty, tired, hungry, starved. He started searching for water, came to the ashram of Shamika Rishi, was not able to find any water in the ashram, was not received by the Rishi and he became upset and saw one dead snake lying on the side outside the ashram of the Muni, picked up the dead snake and just put it around the neck of the Rishi who was deep in meditation and walked away. The son of this Rishi was playing with his playmates. He was a young boy and but he was very nicely trained up as a Brahmana. So when the son of this Rishi, Shringi heard that his father was insulted by the king, the boy Shringi became very angry against this insult by the king of his father, insult of his father. And he decided to curse him, curse the king. So it is described here, his eyes red hot with anger, rosha anger, tamra aksha, like copper. When copper is hot, it is already reddish, but it becomes even more reddish. So red hot, red hot eyes, he was displaying his anger. He touched the water of the river Kaushika while speaking to his playmates. That means he used the power, the Brahmanical power of cursing by using one mantra. And before chanting that mantra, he purified himself.

---
### Mantra, Power, Childishness, Mistake
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=279
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.36
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Any sane man would have prevented him from doing such a great harm to all human society."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the power of mantras and then quotes Srila Prabhupada's commentary, which criticizes Shringi's curse as childish, impudent, and a great mistake that harmed society.

And he discharged the following thunderbolt of words. The mantra power is such that it is like a weapon, but it is not actually a gross weapon. It is a subtle weapon. He discharged the following thunderbolt of words, that mantra by which the king was cursed is as powerful or more powerful than a thunderbolt. That is the sense in which it is used here. So in the Parpo Trila, Shila Prabhupada says, the circumstances under which Maharaj Parikshit was cursed were simply childish as it appears from this verse. Small boy. He is not mature. He is not aware of proper etiquette. He doesn't know culture. So Shila Prabhupada says, the circumstances under which Maharaj Parikshit was cursed were childish. Shringi was showing his impudency amongst his playmates who were innocent. Those playmates, they could not make out what was the seriousness involved in either the king's action or the action, the counteraction by this boy, the playmates were small boys. They were just innocent boys. They couldn't judge or gauge or understand anything. So Shila Prabhupada says, any sane man would have prevented him from doing such a great harm to all human society. So this cursing of the king who is the protector of the entire human society because he is the emperor of the world. And cursing him to be killed for a very very minor mistake is actually improper on the part of this brahmana boy, small boy. By killing a king like Maharaj Parikshit just to make a show of his brahmanical power, the inexperienced son of a brahmana committed a great mistake. This will later on be explained. But here Prabhupada is just mentioning this Shringi made a very very very big mistake. It's called a blunder. He made a blunder. And he was inexperienced son of a brahmana. He did not know what is the proper way to actually react.

---
### Curse, Etiquette, Brahminical Power, Misuse
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=471
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.37
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Thus the beginning of the misuse of brahmanical power began."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 37, which contains the actual curse uttered by Shringi, and then quotes Srila Prabhupada's commentary, highlighting the beginning of the misuse of brahminical power.

The 37th verse. These are the words spoken by the brahmana's son Shringi. So the translation of this 37th verse: The brahmana's son cursed the king thus, 'On the seventh day from today a snake bird will bite the most wretched of that dynasty, Maharaj Parikshit because of his having broken the laws of etiquette by insulting my father.' This is the justification of this boy in cursing Maharaj Parikshit. The Rajarshi, the great emperor, the protector of all the citizens, the ideal king. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada explains, thus the beginning of the misuse of brahmanical power began.

---
### Brahminical Power, Misuse, Downfall, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=551
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.37
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The downfall of the brahmanas began with this misuse of brahmanical power by Shringi."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's commentary, explaining how the misuse of brahminical power by Shringi led to the downfall of the brahmana community in the Kali Yuga, resulting in a loss of both power and culture.

Brahmanas are very powerful. By training by culture by education by their acquired power due to their austerity due to their controlling their senses due to their purity but such power is never to be misused. It has to be carefully used, properly used. So Prabhupada says the beginning of the misuse of brahmanical power began with this Shringi cursing Maharaj Parikshit and then what happened gradually the brahmanas in the age of Kali became devoid of both brahmanical powers and culture. So the brahmanas of present day they don't have the power like this Shringi had or the other brahmanas had in the past. Nowadays brahmanas don't have that kind of power. Neither they have the brahmanical culture they don't have brahmanical culture either. So the downfall of the brahmanas began with this misuse of brahmanical power by Shringi.

---
### Misunderstanding, Degradation, Powerless, Lineage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=655
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.37
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only from him did the brahmana caste become powerless."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain Prabhupada's commentary, emphasizing how Shringi's actions led to the degradation of the brahmana lineage and the loss of their power, highlighting the irony of Shringi calling Parikshit the 'wretched of the dynasty'.

The brahmana boy considered Maharaj Parikshit to be Kulaangara, Kulaangara, the wretched of the dynasty. Parikshit is the best of the dynasty of the kings. Of the Bharata Vamsha, the best of the kings and wrongly this brahmana boy Shringi is misunderstanding, total misunderstanding that Parikshit is wretched of the dynasty. For a small mistake he is wrongly accusing Parikshit to be the wretched of the dynasty. That means the most fallen, most degraded. But factually the brahmana boy himself was so by cursing Parikshit this brahmana boy became the most wretched descendant of the glorious brahmana brahmana lineage. He himself became a wretched descendant. But he is wrongly accusing Parikshit to be the wretched descendant of the glorious Bharata dynasty or Kuru dynasty. Why is it said here by Srila Prabhupada the brahmana boy himself became the wretched of this lineage because only from him did the brahmana caste become powerless.

---
### Powerless, Analogy, Snake, Kali Yuga, Misuse
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jOPjlYMs98g?t=797
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.37
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Similarly in this Kali Yuga the brahmanas are powerless because Sringi misuse that is where the misuse began and that misuse continued for some time till most of the brahmanas became powerless."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a snake with broken poison teeth to illustrate how the brahmanas have become powerless in the Kali Yuga due to the continued misuse of brahminical power that began with Shringi.

Moment the misuse is there they lose their power. Then the other descendants of such a brahmana who has lost his brahmanical power will also be powerless. So the seminal descendants that is by virtue of taking birth in a particular family the present day descendants in that brahmanical families have no power, brahmanical power. They don't have brahmanical power. So they have become powerless. And the example is given of becoming powerless like the snake whose poison teeth are broken. If there is a very poisonous snake it is very dangerous as long as it has got its poison teeth. But if a snake has a very poisonous snake has its poison teeth completely removed it is possible to remove the poison teeth. Then that snake is not at all having any power. So Srila Prabhupada says the snake is fearful as long as his poison teeth are there. Otherwise he is fearful only to children. He can make a show that I will be able to bite you but if he actually bites nothing happens because his poison teeth are removed. Similarly in this Kali Yuga the brahmanas are powerless because Sringi misuse that is where the misuse began and that misuse continued for some time till most of the brahmanas became powerless. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## SB 1.18.38-41 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 24-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### anger, consequences, impudence, regret
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KDfTUr7m6lY?t=34
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This was completely impudent on his part to have cursed the king like that."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the story of Sringi cursing King Pariksit and his subsequent regret, highlighting the impudence of the act and the realization of the mistake.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deviim Saraswatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing from four verses 38, 39, 40, and 41 of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Sriman Bhagavatam. The 38th verse. The meaning of this verse is: Thereafter, when the boy returned to the hermitage, he saw a snake on his father's shoulder, and out of his grief, he cried very loudly. This boy has not even seen what has happened. He just heard from his playmates. And immediately his reaction was, "How deadly! How dare the king insult my father! What does he think of himself? Watch my power! Now I am going to teach him a lesson!" And he became extremely angry, immediately cursed the king to die in seven days. So this was completely impudent on his part to have cursed the king like that. So he saw the snake on his father's shoulder after having cursed the king already, and he began to cry very loudly, realizing what a great mistake he had done. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, the boy was not happy because he committed a great mistake and he wanted to be relieved of the burden on his heart by crying. A small boy has made a very, very big mistake. He is realizing his mistake, but it's too late. So after entering the hermitage and seeing his father in that condition, he cried loudly so that he might be relieved, but it was too late. The father regretted the whole incident. That will be described in the later verses.

---
### meditation, detachment, circumstance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KDfTUr7m6lY?t=198
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He was not conscious of his external surroundings. He was deep in trance."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the 39th verse, describing Shamika Rishi's trance and his gradual awakening upon hearing his son's cries, and seeing the dead snake around his neck.

39th verse. Sava Angiraso Brahman Shrutva Suta Vilapanam Unmilya Shanakair Netre Drishtva Chamse Mruthoragam. The meaning of this 39th verse. O Brahmanas! Now who is addressing this? O Brahmanas! It is Suta Goswami who is addressing the sages of Naimisharanya while describing this incident. The rishi who was born in the family of Angira Muni. This rishi refers to Shamika Rishi, whose ashram Pariksit had entered, searching for water, and whose son was Sringi. So this rishi, that is Shamika Rishi, was born in the family of Angira Muni, a very glorious dynasty or lineage of Brahmanas. Hearing his son crying, gradually opened his eyes and saw the dead snake around his neck. This rishi was sitting in trance in meditation when Pariksit entered the ashram. So he could not properly, he could not receive the king. He could not offer him any hospitality. He was not conscious of his external surroundings. He was deep in trance. So when he heard the sound of his son crying, loudly crying, he gradually opened his eyes, that is coming out of trance, and he saw the dead snake around his neck. Now what is his reaction?

---
### forgiveness, influence of Kali, degradation of society
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KDfTUr7m6lY?t=318
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, there was nothing seriously wrong in Maharaj Pariksit's act, but the foolish son took it very seriously."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the 40th verse, focusing on Shamika Rishi's reaction to the snake and his son's explanation, highlighting Srila Prabhupada's commentary on the insignificance of Pariksit's act, the foolishness of the son, and the influence of Kali in the subsequent degradation of society.

The fortieth verse. Visrjya tamcha papracha vatsakasmadhi rodhisi kenavate apakritam ityuktasa nyavedayat. The meaning of this fortieth verse. He threw the dead snake aside, Visrjya, and asked his son why he was crying, whether anyone had done him any harm. On hearing this, the son explained to him what had happened. So the rishi is hearing from his son, what happened, why are you crying? So the boy explained what all happened. In the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, the father did not take the dead snake on his neck very seriously. He just threw away the snake and did not take it very seriously. Who has done this? Why has he done this? Why this dead snake around my neck? He never thought anything very seriously wrong had happened by that snake, that snake being placed on his neck. He simply threw it away. Actually, there was nothing seriously wrong in Maharaj Pariksit's act, but the foolish son took it very seriously, and being influenced by Kali, he cursed the king, and thus ended a chapter of happy history. With the cursing of Maharaj Pariksit, the Brahmanical power is lost because of misuse, and then with the downfall of the Brahmanas, gradually all the other sections of the society will fall down, and thus the whole society becomes degraded, the human society, and it is definitely the influence of Kali. This is the beginning of Kali Yuga. So the influence of Kali.

---
### irreligion, proportionality, punishment, mistake
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KDfTUr7m6lY?t=469
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Always the punishment for a mistake should be in proportion to the degree of the mistake that is committed."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain the influence of Kali, then transitions to the 41st verse, emphasizing the importance of proportional punishment and Shamika Rishi's regret over the excessive punishment given to King Pariksit for a minor offense.

Kali means irreligion personified, the friend of irreligion, Adharma bandhu. So Kali is meant for spreading irreligion among the people of this Kali Yuga. The forty-first words. nishamya shaptam atad arham narendram sa brahmano na atmajam abhyanandat aho bata amho mahad adhyate krutham alpi yasi droha urur dhamo dhritah. The meaning of this forty-first verse. The father heard from his son that the king had been cursed, although he should never have been condemned, for he was the best amongst all human beings. When Shamika Rishi heard from his son that the king was cursed, he understood that just for a very minor mistake, the king was condemned to death. This is one important point to note that always the punishment for a mistake should be in proportion to the degree of the mistake that is committed. That means a severe punishment for very big blunders and small punishment for minor mistakes. There has to be some consideration like this always that is there in punishing somebody. The Rishi did not congratulate his son, but on the contrary began to repent saying, alas, what a great sinful act was performed by my son. He was awarded heavy punishment for an insignificant offense. He refers to the king. The king was awarded heavy punishment for an insignificant offense. Death sentence or death punishment, punishment by being condemned to die by snake bite, is a very, very, very, very heavy punishment. And that too, who is being punished like that? The emperor of the world, who is the Rajarishi, who is the protector of all the citizens, who was able to keep away such a king, such a righteous, glorious, wonderful, full of wonderful qualities, such a king is being condemned to death. It's a heavy punishment for an insignificant mistake.

---
### king, representative of God, Vedic injunctions, punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/KDfTUr7m6lY?t=688
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The king is the best of all human beings. He is the representative of God and he is never to be condemned for any of his actions."
**Summary:** The speaker continues with Srila Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing the king's role as the representative of God, the Vedic injunction against condemning the king, and the importance of appropriate punishment, concluding with a thank you and Hare Krishna.

So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, the king is the best of all human beings. He is the representative of God and he is never to be condemned for any of his actions. So king means he is meant to rule over the citizens unless he himself is the best of all human beings. He cannot really rule over the citizens. So he himself is qualified, qualified, exceptionally qualified. And not only that, in the very culture, he is the representative of God because God is the real controller of everyone. The supreme lord, Krishna, he is the supreme controller. He is the supreme controller. But he has his representatives and particularly in human society, he has his the king as his representative. So such a personality is never to be condemned for any of his actions. In other words, the king can do no wrong. This is the Vedic injunction. The king is never to be condemned. The king is never to be considered as acting wrongly if he is acting as the representative of God and he is ruling according to the Vedic injunctions. Now here is an example given by Srila Prabhupada. The king may order hanging of a culprit's son of a Brahmana, but he does not become sinful for killing a Brahmana. Shastras say Brahmana should never be killed, but the king, in exceptional cases, when the son of a Brahmana misbehaves, or deserves to be punished by capital punishment, then the king can give that punishment to somebody who is deserving such a punishment, and in that case, the king does not incur the sin for killing a Brahmana. Further, Srila Prabhupada says, even if there is something wrong with the king, he is never to be condemned. So, what to speak of a good and pious king like Maharaj Pariksit? So, the king is trained to become a Rajarshi, and a Rajarshi or a great saint, he is never to be condemned the way Shringi treated this great Maharaj Pariksit. So, the instruction of the Vedas is, the king is called Narendra or Naradeva, and he is the best amongst the human beings. So, he should not be condemned like this. For a minor mistake, he may be given some very minor punishment, but never such a grave punishment or a severe punishment. For a minor mistake, that is completely wrong. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

Om Agyanati Miran Dhasya Gyananj Nishalakaya Chakshurun Militam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Saraswati Narottamam Vyasam Tatho Jayam Udi Rayet.

---
### devotion, transcendental knowledge, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mtCACpI1Yt4?t=61
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those who have dedicated their lives to the transcendental topics of the personality of Godhead and who are constantly engaged in remembering the lotus feet of the Lord do not run the risk of having misconceptions even at the last moment of their lives."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces Srimad Bhagavatam 1.18.4, explaining that those dedicated to transcendental topics and remembering the Lord's lotus feet avoid misconceptions at life's end, connecting it to Parikshit Maharaj's surrender to Sukadeva Goswami.

In this session we will be discussing from the first canto, 18th chapter of the Srimad Bhagavatam, verse numbers 4 and 5. The fourth verse we will discuss first. Nottama Shloka Vartanam Jushatam Tath Kathamritam Syat Sambhramo Antakalepi Smaratam Tath Padambhujam. The meaning of this verse is this was so because those who have dedicated their lives to the transcendental topics of the personality of Godhead, of whom the Vedic hymns sing, and who are constantly engaged in remembering the lotus feet of the Lord, do not run the risk of having misconceptions even at the last moment of their lives. So, this verse has got a reference to the previous verse because the verse says, 'This was so because.' What was so? That Parikshit Maharaj surrendered himself unto Shri Lashukadeva Goswami and therefore he was able to understand the actual position of the personality of Godhead. So, the fourth verse that today we are discussing now has got relevance to this point made in the third verse, that he was able to understand the actual position of the personality of Godhead because the nature of a person who has dedicated his life to the transcendental topics of the personality of Godhead, and such people also are constantly engaged in remembering the lotus feet of the Lord. Two characteristics. First one is their lives are dedicated to the transcendental topics of the personality of Godhead. They live their life based on the scriptures which glorify the Supreme Lord and His pure devotees. And they also constantly think of the lotus feet of the Lord, Supreme Lord. Such devotees, they do not have the risk of having misconceptions at the end of their lives.

---
### Krishna consciousness, death, liberation, spiritual nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mtCACpI1Yt4?t=253
**Verse References:** BG 8.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The highest perfection of life is attained by remembering the transcendental nature of the Lord at the last moment of one's life."
**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada explains that remembering Krishna at the end of life leads to the transcendental abode, eternal life, and devotional service, emphasizing the importance of cultivating Krishna consciousness.

So, Srila Prabhupada explains what is this risk that somebody may have. So, in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, the highest perfection of life is attained by remembering the transcendental nature of the Lord at the last moment of one's life. We are spiritual beings. The Supreme Lord is completely a spiritual personality. We belong to the Supreme Lord's personal abode. So, in this human form of life, we are meant to cultivate Krishna Consciousness to be able to remember the Supreme Lord at the end of our life. Ante Narayanas Pratihi. Antakale Chamameva Svaranmuktva Kalevaram. Both the Bhagavatam and the Bhagavad Gita describe this. At the end of life, to successfully terminate this life, one has to remember Krishna at the last moment. And that is the highest perfection because simply by remembering Krishna alone at the end of one's life, one is able to attain immediately the transcendental abode of the Supreme Lord, never to take birth again in this miserable material world. And having gone there, what do they do? They live eternal life full of bliss and knowledge in the association of the Supreme Personality of Godhead, being engaged in His loving devotional service very naturally.

---
### disciplic succession, knowledge, service, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mtCACpI1Yt4?t=375
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This perfection of life is made possible by one who has learned the actual transcendental nature of the Lord from the Vedic hymns sung by a liberated soul like Shukadev Goswami or someone in that line of disciplic succession."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes learning about Krishna's transcendental nature from a liberated soul in disciplic succession, stressing the importance of service and surrender to the spiritual master for clear understanding and transcendental living.

So Srila Prabhupada further explains, this perfection of life is made possible by one who has learned the actual transcendental nature of the Lord from the Vedic hymns sung by a liberated soul like Shukadev Goswami or someone in that line of disciplic succession. So how do you actually ensure that you'll be able to remember the Supreme Lord at the end of your life? So for that, you have to learn what is this transcendental nature of the Supreme Lord Krishna by hearing from a liberated soul like Shukadev Goswami or someone in the parampara from Shukadev Goswami who is also a liberated soul. Now, if somebody simply hears this type of Krishna Katha, Bhagavad Katha from some mental speculator, Srila Prabhupada says there's no use. You have to hear from an actual self-realized soul and the hearing process is also directed that one has to properly understand the transcendental nature of Krishna by service and submission. One should be surrendered to the spiritual master and one should render service to the spiritual master. Then the spiritual master reveals the secret, the truth, the most confidential knowledge about the transcendental nature of the Supreme Personality of God, Krishna. And everything becomes transparently clear, very, very clear. So, by such clear understanding, by realization, one is able to live transcendentally even before death comes. One's life is life dedicated to the Supreme Lord. And such a transcendental living continues till the end of life. And, Srila Prabhupada says, by scientific adaptation, one is able to remember the Lord even at the time of death.

---
### death, grace, devotion, spiritual master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mtCACpI1Yt4?t=552
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, it is the grace of the Supreme Lord and His pure devotees, the spiritual masters. That is the real secret."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the difficulty of remembering the Lord at death due to bodily derangement, emphasizing the importance of the Lord's grace and the guidance of a bona fide spiritual master, exemplified by Parikshit Maharaj and Sukadeva Goswami.

What happens at the time of death? Why is it difficult to remember normally for an ordinary person? That is because at the time of death, the power of remembrance is slackened due to derangement of bodily membranes. Something happens in this body when the time of death comes. We have to leave the body. The bodily functions are going to completely stop working. So, at that moment, last moment, it's not easy, it's not natural to remember the Supreme Lord. For a common man, it is very difficult to remember things as they are at the time of death. But by the grace of the Lord and His bona fide devotees, the spiritual masters, one can get this opportunity without difficulty. So, what really works? Actually, it is the grace of the Supreme Lord and His pure devotees, the spiritual masters. That is the real secret. And who can have that mercy or grace? Those who have dedicated their life for serving the Supreme Lord under the guidance of the bona fide spiritual master. And that was the case with Maharaj Pariksit. His life was dedicated for Krishna's service. And he got a very, very able spiritual master like Shukadeva Goswami.

---
### Kali Yuga, governance, dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mtCACpI1Yt4?t=661
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As long as the great powerful son of Abhimanyu remains the emperor of the world, there is no chance that the personality of Kali will flourish."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Srimad Bhagavatam 1.18.5, explaining that Kali's influence cannot flourish as long as a righteous emperor like Pariksit Maharaj rules, preventing irreligion and promoting the opportunity for people to return to Godhead.

The fifth verse, Tavit kalina prabhavet pravishto piha sarvatah yavad isho mahan urvyam abhimanyava ekarat. The meaning of this fifth verse is, as long as the great powerful son of Abhimanyu remains the emperor of the world, there is no chance that the personality of Kali will flourish. We discussed in detail the influence of Kali. Kali's influence is to entice people to engage in irreligion, adharma bandhu. So people become sinful and they miss the valuable opportunity in the human form of life to prepare oneself to go back to Godhead. So Kali cannot influence people. Kali cannot flourish as long as there is an emperor like Pariksit Maharaj ruling the entire world. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, as we have already explained, the personality of Kali had entered the jurisdiction of this earth long ago and he was looking for an opportunity to spread his influence all over the world. This will be explained in the next verse. The very day Krishna left this planet, after winding up his pastimes at the end of Dwapar Yuga, Kali, Purusha, tried to enter the earth planet. Kali was looking for an opportunity but he could not do so satisfactorily due to the presence of Maharaj Pariksit. That is a way of good government. The disturbing elements like the personality of Kali will always try to extend their nefarious activities. But it is the duty of the able state to check them by all means.

---
### governance, Krishna consciousness, wealth, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mtCACpI1Yt4?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If people do not use their excess wealth beyond what they require, minimum required for their bodily maintenance, beyond that, if they don't use the excess wealth for Sankirtana, for Krishna consciousness, then that accumulated wealth becomes the place for breeding all kinds of sinful activities."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Pariksit Maharaj stopped Kali by utilizing wealth for Krishna's Sankirtana, emphasizing that unused excess wealth breeds sinful activities, contrasting it with the current misuse of wealth for sense gratification.

Pariksit stopped Kali. Of course he was ready to kill Kali Purusha. But Kali Purusha surrendered. Pariksit Maharaj spared his life. That is the code of conduct for the king. That surrendered souls should never be killed. But at the same time, Kali was not given any opportunity for spreading his evil influence among the citizens of Pariksit Maharaj's kingdom. Although Maharaj Pariksit allotted places for the personality of Kali, at the same time he gave no chance for the citizens which swayed by the personality of Kali. How did he do that? He actually utilized all the stored gold for propagating the glories of the Supreme Lord Krishna for Krishna's Sankirtana. And that is the way to prevent the influence of Kali. If people do not use their excess wealth beyond what they require, minimum required for their bodily maintenance, beyond that, if they don't use the excess wealth for Sankirtana, for Krishna consciousness, then that accumulated wealth becomes the place for breeding all kinds of sinful activities. Like it is happening now, we can see that people use their excess wealth simply for sinful ways of enjoying sense gratification. So that chance was not given because of the strong governance of Pariksit Maharaj and that's being said here. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## SB 1.18.42-43 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 25-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### king, responsibility, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rwPW4PpMtpM?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.42
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He is as good as the personality of Godhead; he is never to be placed on equal footing with common men."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains verse 42, describing how Shringi is immature and doesn't understand the position of the king, who is like God and protects the citizens.

In this session, we will be discussing two verses, the 42nd and 43rd verses of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 42nd verse is as follows. The meaning of this 42nd verse is that Shamika Rishi is addressing his son, Shringi, and therefore you have no knowledge that the king, who is the best amongst human beings, is as good as the personality of Godhead because he is trained. He represents the Supreme Lord. He acts according to the Vedic injunctions given by the Supreme Lord, so he is as good as the personality of Godhead. He is never to be placed on equal footing with common men. The citizens of the state live in prosperity, being protected by his unsurpassable prowess. The sage himself was able to nicely sit in his ashram and do tapasya without. So the Rishi is describing his son as immature. He is a small boy. He definitely doesn't have the maturity when to use his brahmanical power to curse somebody to die. He doesn't have the maturity, and he doesn't know the position of the king, especially the emperor who is ruling the world so nicely, that everyone is well protected by the unsurpassable prowess of Parikshit Maharaja. He is such a great king that people are living, they are experiencing safety, and they are prosperous under the rule of Parikshit Maharaja. They are prosperous. Earlier, there is a description when Parikshit Maharaja was touring the state, all the citizens received him very nicely, and they were glorifying his rule, that because he was the descendant of the great devotees of Krishna, the Pandavas, therefore they were glorifying not only Parikshit, but also glorifying the Pandavas and glorifying Krishna, who was actually guiding the Pandavas as they.

---
### leadership, democracy, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rwPW4PpMtpM?t=740
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One trained monarch is far better than hundreds of useless ministerial rogues."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami compares monarchy to democracy, stating that a qualified king is better than elected officials who may lack training and good qualities, and that the absence of a good king leads to the problems of Kali Yuga.

By qualification, not merely by occupying some post, decorating himself with a position. I am the king, I am the greatest, no, not simply some empty words or pride, because pride somebody declares themselves as great, no. He actually has got the qualities of God and he is empowered by the Supreme Lord to protect the citizens. The battle of Kurukshetra was planned by the Lord to establish the real representative of the Lord, Maharaja Yudhishthira. Krishna is interested in protecting all the citizens by having his representative rule over the citizens. So the example is given that Krishna established Yudhishthira Maharaj as the emperor of the world. An ideal king thoroughly trained by culture and devotional service with the martial spirit makes a perfect king. So Prabhupada is comparing the present day government system of government with the previous system of government where the king was ruling. Such a personal monarchy is far better than the so called democracy of no training and responsibility simply by votes. These are representatives of the government. They rule. They sit in the occupy the seat of power and they rule as elected representatives. Now what is his qualification? He has secured votes, that's all. That's the only qualification he has. He doesn't have any training. He doesn't have any he has not acquired good qualities by education, training, culture. Not necessarily, I mean, not necessarily I'm not condemning all the representatives, but I'm just telling even without qualification somebody may by simply acquiring votes occupy the post. The thieves and rogues of modern democracy seek election by misrepresentation of votes and the successful rogues and thieves devour the mass of people. One trained monarch is far better than hundreds of useless ministerial rogues and it is hinted herein that by abolition of a monarchical regime like that of Maharaj Parikshit the mass of people become open to many attacks of the age of Kali. So Kali Yuga is characterized by the garment ruling over the people not having the proper qualification. So in the absence of a good king what really happens in the society will be described in the later verses. So I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## SB 1.18.44-45 | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 26-04-2023 | Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### social disruption, degradation, Kali Yuga

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment discusses the meaning of Srimad Bhagavatam verse 1.18.44, focusing on the social disruptions caused by the decline of monarchical regimes and the rise of thieves and rogues who plunder the wealth of the people.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing verses 44 and 45 of the 18th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This chapter describes Maharaj Pariksit cursed by a Brahmana boy. The 44th verse: Vasor Vilumpakat Parasparamgnanti Shapanti Vrnjate Pashun Striyo Arthan Puru Dasyavo Janaha. The meaning of this 44th verse is due to the termination of the monarchical regimes and the plundering of the people's wealth by rogues and thieves, there will be great social disruptions. People will be killed and injured, and animals and women will be stolen. And for all these sins, we shall be responsible.

---
### social degradation, Kali Yuga, lack of protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rw5z0UiAzPQ?t=126
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Nowadays there is no value for human life, especially if the person who dies is a most ordinary citizen. Nobody cares that everybody has a right to live."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the degraded state of society in Kali Yuga, highlighting the lack of safety for people, the increase in thieves and rogues, the devaluation of human life, and the exploitation and lack of protection for women.

This is Rishi Shamika foreseeing the consequences of the abolition of the monarchical regime of such a great Rajarshi as Pariksit Maharaj. What will happen? Plundering of the people's wealth by rogues and thieves. What is exactly happening now? In many places, there is no safety for people in general. And as Kali Yuga progresses, the number of thieves and rogues will increase. And people's hard-earned money, honest people's hard-earned money, will be plundered by these rogues and thieves. Might is right. That's going to be the situation. When the government is weak or corrupt, or untrained, unqualified people occupying the post of the head of state, there will be great social disruptions. Society will be completely spoiled. That means people will be killed and injured. Nowadays there is no value for human life, especially if the person who dies is a most ordinary citizen. Nobody cares. Nobody cares that everybody has a right to live. But ordinary, insignificant, common people, if they somehow manage to survive or subsist, that's okay. But otherwise, nobody cares for them. In this age of Kali, as Kali Yuga progresses. And animals and women will be stolen. Here, animals means the domestic animals. And women, women are unprotected in Kali Yuga. So, so many cases are being reported. More and more we can hear how women are exploited, women are unprotected, women are insulted. So, this is a degraded society.

---
### social order, protection, corrupt governance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rw5z0UiAzPQ?t=299
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The so-called Democrats are generally plunderers of the wealth of the state subjects."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts a cultured society, which protects Brahmanas, women, children, old people, and cows, with the present age where such protection is absent. It then discusses Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the verse, highlighting the responsibility of Brahmanas for the decline of the monarchical regime and the rise of corrupt democratic systems that plunder the wealth of the state subjects.

In a cultured society, five categories of people are offered protection: Brahmanas, women, children, old people, and cows. This is a proper cultured society where there is perfect protection for everyone, and especially these five categories of vulnerable people are specially protected. Whereas, in present age, we don't see special protection for any of these five categories of personalities. So, in the Parpotriya, Prabhupada explains, the word Naha, V, is very significant in this verse. The sage, that is Shamika Rishi, rightly takes the responsibility of the Brahmanas as a community for killing monarchical government. His own son wrongly cursed Parikshit Maharaj. So, Rishi Shamika is taking responsibility that the Brahmanas as a community have destroyed this monarchical regime, and thus giving an opportunity to the so-called Democrats, who are generally plunderers of the wealth of the state subjects. So, Srila Prabhupada explains what is going on in the present time because he is the Acharya who has appeared in this particular time of the Kali Yuga. Acharya points out through the scriptural statements how there are anomalies in the society that needs to be corrected if people expect to live peacefully, happily, and with prosperity. Then, what is required to be done? First of all, the government by so-called Democrats, democratic system. Democratic system means simply a person gets votes, and he occupies the position of a ruler. So, the so-called Democrats are generally plunderers of the wealth of the state subjects.

---
### corrupt governance, taxation, social disturbance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rw5z0UiAzPQ?t=477
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone captures the post for personal gratification. That is happening more and more now than earlier times."
**Summary:** This segment continues to critique democratic systems, explaining how leaders capture power without responsibility for the citizens' well-being, leading to multiple irresponsible leaders who heavily tax the population. It contrasts this with the traditional Vedic system where only Vaishyas were taxed by a single king.

How does it happen? Prabhupada explains, the so-called Democrats capture the administrative machine without assuming responsibility for the prosperous condition of citizens. They have no responsibility that I will ensure the citizens will be taken care of nicely. There is no guarantee, there is no assurance, there is no proper mechanism to ensure that the rulers will properly take care of the subjects. Everyone captures the post for personal gratification. That is happening more and more now than earlier times. And they want their own enjoyment. And thus, instead of one king, a number of irresponsible kings grow up to tax the citizens. Formerly, the king used to collect taxes from the citizens who would be wealth generators, the Vaishyas. Only the Vaishyas were taxed. The Kshatriyas are protectors of the citizens, so they are not taxed. The Brahmanas don't accept any salary, so there's no question of asking tax from them. The Shudras are not given money, but they're given all the necessities. So they happily are taken care of, and they serve very nicely, ensuring that they actually make spiritual progress to achieve spiritual perfection, protected by the upper three classes. The Brahmanas, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas protect the Shudras. So only the Vaishyas, who are wealth generators, they are taxed by one king, not multiple kings. But here, the taxes are so many. So many taxes. And there is state tax, central tax, and earlier there used to be something called city tax. Now that has been abolished.

---
### social degradation, unwanted population, sense gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rw5z0UiAzPQ?t=633
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.45
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is foretold herein that in the absence of a monarchical regime, the general mass of people will be an unwanted population, like dogs and monkeys."
**Summary:** This segment begins with verse 45, explaining that in the absence of good monarchical government, everyone will cause disturbance by plundering riches, animals, and women. It then discusses the meaning of Srimad Bhagavatam verse 1.18.45, focusing on the fall from progressive civilization, the attraction to economic development for sense gratification, and the resulting unwanted population compared to dogs and monkeys due to illegitimate connections.

This is a fact. We all have experience of this. The forty fifth verse, tadarya dharmah praviliyate nrnam, varnashramacharayutas trayimayah, tato artha kamabhi niveshitatmanam, shunam kapinam, iva varna sankaraham. This is a very strong verse. The meaning of this verse is at that time, the people in general will fall systematically from a path of a progressive civilization in respect to the qualitative engagements of the castes and orders of society. There is a standard that civilized society, there are brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, shudra, brahmachari, all of them are responsible. They properly follow the injunctions, and the whole society in this way is progressive. But people will fall from the standard of following the respective rules and regulations of the varna divisions and ashrama divisions. Thus they will be more attracted to economic development for sense gratification. They are simply interested in money. And money for what? Simply for enjoying their senses, some or the other. And as a result, there will be an unwanted population on the level of dogs and monkeys, shunam, kapinam, varna sankaraham. This is a unwanted population. And the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, it is foretold herein that in the absence of a monarchical regime, the general mass of people will be an unwanted population, like dogs and monkeys. Why they are compared to dogs and monkeys? As the monkeys are too sexually inclined, and dogs are shameless in sexual intercourse, the general mass of population, born of illegitimate connection with illegitimate connection, will systematically go astray from the Vedic way of good or manners, and qualitative engagements in the castes and orders of life.

---
### Vedic way of life, spiritual progress, degraded society
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rw5z0UiAzPQ?t=777
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Vedic way of life is a progressive march of the civilization of Aryans. Aryans are those who know the values of life and they always have the goal of going back home, back to Godhead."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of good progeny through Vedic practices and the role of a proper ruler in enforcing good practices. It highlights the Vedic way of life as a progressive march towards spiritual realization and criticizes the degraded state of society in Kali Yuga, where people lack good progeny and are becoming increasingly degraded, losing the opportunity for eternal happiness and knowledge.

Unless there is good progeny, sat santana, that happens by the husband and wife combining together according to the rules and regulations of the Vedic scriptures. There is a certain way of actually generating good population. It is not that by accident somebody is born with bad qualities, and somebody else is born with good qualities. No. There is no accident. So this is science. Complete science. So, when there is no proper ruler who trains, and who directs, and who enforces good practices, the population will become completely degraded. The Vedic way of life is a progressive march of the civilization of Aryans. Aryans are those who know the values of life and they always have the goal of going back home, back to Godhead, where there is no birth, no death, no disease, no old age, and no miseries. The Vedas direct everyone not to remain in the darkness of material world, but to go towards the light of spiritual kingdom, far beyond the material sky. So this whole varnashrama is designed in such a way that the entire population of human beings will actually follow the Vedic directions, and in that way, they'll produce good progeny, and the whole population will simply make progress towards going back home, back to Godhead. So people will not misuse or lose this opportunity of the human form of life to prepare themselves for eternal life of happiness and knowledge. So, this is the unfortunate situation in this Kali Yuga. People are degraded, they don't have good progeny, and gradually society is becoming more and more degraded. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### curse, responsibility, dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZXqqKaehl1g?t=40
**Verse References:** SB 1.18.46
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When such a king is tired and fatigued, being stricken with hunger and thirst, he does not at all deserve to be cursed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 46th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Shamika Rishi's reaction to his son cursing Emperor Pariksit for a minor offense.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session we will be discussing the 46th verse of the 18th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The verse: Dharmapalo Narapati Hi Satu Samrat Brihat Shavaha Sakshat Mahabhagavato Rajarshir Hayamedhayat Rajarshir Hayamedhayat Kshutrut Shrama Uthodino Naivasmat Sapa Arhati. The meaning of these verses: The emperor Pariksit is a pious king. He is highly celebrated and is the first-class devotee of the personality of Godhead. He is a saint amongst royalty and he has performed many hard sacrifices. When such a king is tired and fatigued, being stricken with hunger and thirst, he does not at all deserve to be cursed. These are words spoken by Shamika Rishi on learning from his son that his son had cursed Emperor Pariksit just because Emperor Pariksit when he was thirsty and hungry he came looking for water in Shamika Rishi's ashram. Not finding water he became upset and he took a dead snake and put it round the shoulder of the Rishi who was sitting in meditation in trance and walked away.

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Shamika Rishi's disapproval of his son's curse, emphasizing Emperor Pariksit's qualifications and the disproportionate nature of the punishment for a minor transgression.

So the reaction of the son of Shamika Rishi Shringi was to become very angry against the king and curse the king to die within seven days. So here Shamika Rishi is saying that he has cursed the king and cursed the king to die within seven days. So here Shamika Rishi is telling, who have you cursed foolish boy out of your immaturity. He is particularly telling because that king is emperor of the world and his qualifications are being mentioned here by Rishi Shamika. He does not at all deserve to be cursed. So what is the meaning of this? What are all the qualifications of this king? Firstly he is a pious king. He is highly celebrated and is a first-class devotee of the personality of Godhead. How can such a person be ever cursed by anybody? And that too what he has done is very very very very small mistake. Such a great personality for a small mistake has been cursed to die. It is completely wrong on the part of this boy Shringi to have cursed the emperor of the world.

**Summary:** The speaker continues to describe the duties and responsibilities of a king, highlighting the importance of performing sacrifices for the welfare of the citizens and emphasizing the injustice of cursing such a responsible ruler.

He is a Rajarishi, saint amongst royalty. He has performed many horse sacrifices and such sacrifices were performed by the king or the emperor for the welfare of the entire state, for the welfare of all citizens. As a duty a king is supposed to perform sacrifices for the protection of welfare prosperity of these citizens of the world of the entire kingdom. When such a king is tired, fatigued, thirsty, hungry, he does not at all deserve to be cursed. And what kind of curse is it? To die within seven days? It is not at all justified to curse such a king. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says, after explaining the general codes relating to the royal position and asserting the king can do no wrong and therefore is never to be condemned because a king has a great responsibility for ruling the kingdom and ruling the kingdom is no small task. His kingdom is the entire world.

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the exalted position and heavy responsibilities of a king, suggesting that even minor mistakes should not warrant severe punishment, and then transitions to discussing the specific qualifications of Emperor Pariksit.

Such a responsible king, such a heavy duty he has to perform and his position is so exalted that in his position when he is taken responsibility, it is to be considered that such a king can do no wrong in the sense that even if he does some small mistake. He should never be considered as the object of punishment by anyone. The sage Shamika wanted to say something about Emperor Pariksit specifically. First he spoke in general about a king who is a responsible king should not be considered to do something wrong for a small mistake. But now the king who has been cursed is not just a dutiful king. He is the emperor of the world. He is Emperor Pariksit, descendant of the Kuru dynasty, Bharata Vamsa, descendant of the Pandavas. That emperor has been cursed. So what are his specific qualifications? So the specific qualifications of Maharaja Pariksit is summarized here in by Shamika Rishi. The king, even calculated as a king only was most celebrated as a ruler who administered the religious principles of the Royal Order. According to the scriptures, the kings have got specific duties to perform and they are very clearly defined. The kings have specific duty to perform and they are very clearly elaborated in the scriptures.

### dharma, kshatriya, qualifications
