## Srila Bhaktisiddhanta Sarasvati Thakura Appearance Day | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | 21-02-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### appearance day, biography, divine qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=80
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** biography
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This child will become world famous as a brilliant teacher of life's ultimate goal."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur and describes his birth and the auspicious signs observed at that time, including the astrologer's prediction.

Today is the most auspicious appearance day anniversary of His Divine Grace, Shri La Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur Prabhupāda. Shri La Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur is the spiritual master of the founder āchārya of ISKCON, His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupāda. So, a little about his biography on this day of his divine appearance. Shri La Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur was born on February 6th, 1874, corresponding to Māghamāsā Krishna Paksha Panchami. So, according to the Vedic calendar, today is that day, Māghamāsā Krishna Paksha Panchami. He was born at 3:30 in the afternoon. His name before initiation was Bhimala Prasādatta. He was born in Jagannātpuri, in a house in which Shri La Bhaktivedanta Thakur resides. His father was staying very near to the temple of the famous Jagannāt Baldev Subhadrā in Jagannātpuri. He was the fourth son of Shri La Bhaktivinoda Thakur and Shri Yukta Bhagavati Devi. At the time of his birth, all 32 bodily symptoms of a Mahāpūruṣa, a great personality, were pointed out by an experienced astrologer. Furthermore, the boy was born with his umbilical cord wrapped around his neck like a Brahmin's thread. The astrologer commented at the time of his birth, "I have done many horoscopes in my life, but I have never before seen such a horoscope filled with all the signs of a great personality. This child will become world famous as a brilliant teacher of life's ultimate goal." This was the comment by the astrologer upon preparing the horoscope of the newly born child.

---
### divine favor, childhood, renunciation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=234
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore, from the very beginning of his appearance, Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur, exhibited all the signs of an exalted eternal associate of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates two childhood incidents of Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur: being garlanded by Lord Jagannath during Rathayatra and choosing the Srimad Bhagavatam over coins at his grain ceremony, highlighting his early signs of devotion and renunciation.

When the child was six months old, Lord Jagannāt's Rathiyatra festival was in procession. Bhagavati Devi, that is the mother of the child, brought the child before the cart, Jagannath Ratha, and offered garland fell from the neck of Lord Jagannath, encircling the boy. This was taken as a sign of special favor by all present, the child is blessed by Lord Jagannath. At the time of the grain ceremony, it was customary to offer the child both coins and books, in order to get some indication of the future tendencies of the child, means one side they will keep some money, and one side they will keep the bhagavatam, the books. So he was offered the child, on one side coins and other side the Srimad Bhagavatam. He accepted the bhagavatam instead of the coins, so this also indicates the future of the child. Therefore, from the very beginning of his appearance, Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur, exhibited all the signs of an exalted eternal associate of the Lord.

---
### ray of Vishnu, preaching, empowerment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=346
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Goswami, was specially empowered by Lord Chaitanya, simply for the sake of preaching Krishna Consciousness all over the world."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Srila Prabhupada's definition of a 'ray of Vishnu' in the context of Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's prayer for a son who could widely preach Krishna consciousness, emphasizing Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur's empowerment for this purpose.

He had descended in answer to Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's prayer, for a ray of Vishnu, to help him spread the Krishna consciousness movement. Now Srila Prabhupada explains the meaning of a ray of Vishnu. Prabhupada says, there are two kinds of children born of good fathers, one is educated in Krishna consciousness, so that he can be delivered from the clutches of Maya, in that very life, and the other is a ray of Vishnu, who teaches the world the ultimate goal of life. So Thakur Bhakti Vinod also wanted to beget a child who could preach the philosophy and teachings of Lord Chaitanya to the fullest extent. Bhakti Vinod Thakur himself was a preacher, a great Acharya, but he prayed to the Supreme Lord, "I want to have a child who can preach the teachings of Lord Chaitanya to the fullest extent, preach all over the world." By his prayers, that is by Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's prayers to the Lord, he had as his child, Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Goswami Maharaj, who at the present moment is preaching the philosophy of Lord Chaitanya, throughout the entire world, through his bona fide disciples. This is what Srila Prabhupada explains, what is the meaning of the word, term, ray of Vishnu, that means, Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Goswami, was specially empowered by Lord Chaitanya, simply for the sake of preaching Krishna Consciousness all over the world, that is the exclusive purpose for which he appeared in this world.

---
### renunciation, discipline, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=474
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Throughout my whole life, I shall not eat these fruits again, this is the right punishment for one ruled by greed."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts an incident where the young Bimal Prasad ate a mango before it was offered to Krishna, leading to his lifelong vow to abstain from eating mangoes, illustrating his strict adherence to devotional principles.

When he was a small boy, perhaps two or three years old, his father Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur brought some ripe mangoes from the market, it was the season. Bimal Prasad, that was his name, when he was born, Bimal Prasad took one to eat, saying, "This is mine." Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur got up and said in a grave voice, "What is this, new fruit has come into the house, it has not been offered to Lord Krishna, and you have taken it first. Remember, new fruit is to be given first to the Lord, without giving the first share to the Lord, nothing is to be taken." The child was very repentant upon hearing this from his father, and he exclaimed, "What evil thoughts I have had, throughout my whole life, I shall not eat these fruits again, this is the right punishment for one ruled by greed." So this is what the child exclaimed, he made the promise and observed it throughout his whole life. Later on when somebody would offer him nice mangoes, Bhakti Siddhartha Srila Vinod Thakur would say, "I am an offender, I don't deserve to eat these mangoes." In this way he observed that, wow, never again to eat mangoes, just because a small child had done a small mistake.

---
### early education, devotion, intelligence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=587
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** biography
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By the age of seven, he had committed the entire Bhagavad Gita to memory, and could wonderfully explain the verses as well."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur's early education, including his worship of Lord Kurma, his exceptional understanding of Krishna consciousness, memorization of the Bhagavad Gita, and training in publishing.

But he took it very very seriously, this is the characteristic of the Acharya. When Bimal Prasad was seven years old, Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur was constructing the Bhakti Bhavan in Calcutta at Rambhagan. During that construction, a deity of Lord Kurma was unearthed. Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur ordered his son Bimal Prasad, to begin regulated worship of that deity of Lord Kurma. From the very beginning, the boy used to hear the preaching of his father, Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's preaching, with rapt attention. Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur as well as his audience were amazed, to see how a small boy could hear the topics of Krishna consciousness, with such total absorption. By the age of seven, he had committed the entire Bhagavad Gita to memory, and could wonderfully explain the verses as well. See this is a special thing, there may be some children who can commit to memory all the seven hundred verses, but a seven year old small boy, being able to explain all the verses very nicely, that is extremely unusual. Later on, he was trained in publishing and proofreading at Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's printing press, in connection with Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's well known spiritual journal, the Sajjana Toshani. At the age of eleven, Bimal Prasad toured the various places of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's leelas with his father. Bimal Prasad had a photographic memory, whatever he would read once, he could later recall with perfect clarity. He was trained in mathematics and astrology at Sri Rampur school, and he achieved such astounding scholarship, that he was eventually awarded the title Siddhanta Saraswati by his tutors, Pandit Mahesh Chandra Chowramani and Pandit Sundar Lal, just when he was eleven years old, he was awarded this title, Siddhanta Saraswati. During this period, he used to attend the Sunday weekly meetings of Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur's Vishwa Vaishnava Rajya Sabha.

---
### detachment, spiritual focus, scholarship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=757
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** biography
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "I left the Sanskrit College, and in order to maintain my life for the service of Hari, I was desirous of getting an honest occupation, which I could perform with the intention of earning a humble income."
**Summary:** The speaker continues with Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur's biography, highlighting his focus on devotional literature over school assignments, his initiation into chanting the Narsimha mantra, his continued studies of Bhakti Vinod Thakur's books, and his decision to leave Sanskrit College to dedicate himself to serving Hari.

And hear his father lecture on the Bhakti Rasamrita Sindhu of Srila Rupa Goswami. He was never known to study his school assignments, and while sitting at home, he placed those assignments with studies of devotional literatures, like Srila Narottam Das Thakur's Prarthana and Prema Bhakti Chandrika. However, whenever questions were asked by his teachers, he would immediately answer, and he got excellent results on all of his examinations at school. When the boy was in seventh class, Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur, brought Tulsi beads for his son from Jagannath Puri, and gave him the name of Hari, and a Narsimha mantra to chant, which he executed very faithfully, along with the worship of his Kurma Deity, according to the instructions of his father, Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur. At the age of sixteen, while his schooling continued, the books of Srila Bhakti Vinod Thakur formed the serious content of his studies. He also began writing books on astrology. In 1892, at the age of eighteen, Bimal Prasad, who had got the title of Siddhanta Saraswati, took an entrance examination, and entered Calcutta Sanskrit College. As before he paid scant attention to the prescribed books, but he found the time to read all of the philosophical books contained in the library. In 1895, he left the college, as he was very eager to execute his worship of the Lord, and to remain free from the pressure to enter family life. He stated in his autobiography, "If I remain studying with careful attention at the college, Sanskrit College, then extreme pressure will be brought upon me to enter family life, but if I am considered to be foolish and inefficient, then no one will be, no one will put such an inducement upon me, for becoming so engaged." By this consideration, I left the Sanskrit College, and in order to maintain my life for the service of Hari, I was desirous of getting an honest occupation, which I could perform with the intention of earning a humble income.

---
### criticism, deviation, genuine teachings
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=970
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Only because of such acharyas, as Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur, and later on, Srila Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur, are we able to distinguish between the genuine teachings of Lord Chaitanya, strictly according to the authorized scriptures, and the deviant teachings."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Srila Bhakti Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur's early engagements in spiritual activities, including chanting mantras, worshipping the Kurma Deity, and training in printing and editing. He also addresses the prevalence of pseudo-gurus and deviated teachings during that time, emphasizing the importance of Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur and Srila Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur in preserving the genuine teachings of Lord Chaitanya.

During his student days, he also wrote essays on spiritual topics, for various magazines, and strongly condemned various religious malpractices, which had become prominent in society. At the request of Srila Jagannath Das Babaji, he composed the Navadvipa Panjika, a Krishna iced almanac, in which the months, lunar fortnights, days, lunar days, stars, etc, were all given names of Vishnu. He did not spend very long in his astronomical pursuits, however, and soon dedicated himself fully, to the worship and distribution of the teachings of Lord Chaitanya. Siddhanta Saraswati had been engaged in regularly chanting the Maha Mantra, and a Nusimha Mantra, given to him by Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur, when he was in the seventh class, he had been worshipping the Kurma Deity since the age of seven, from 1885 onward, he had received special training in printing, editing, etc, at the press, at the Bhakti Bhavan. Thus, the young man was a very qualified candidate, for becoming the disciple of a guru. Many pseudo-gurus of the Sahajiya category wanted to have him, as their disciple, to increase their false prestige, but Siddhanta Saraswati, was not interested in such pretenders. Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur explains, during his time, there were so many upper sampradayas, claiming to be representing Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, so Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur, rightly pointed out, how people had deviated, many different groups of people, claiming to be followers of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, but not strictly following the actual regulated principles, compromising on the strict rules and regulations of Sadhana Bhakti, but externally dressing themselves as devotees, and concocting their own methods of worship, deviating from the philosophy, taught by Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, the philosophy of Bhakti, and misleading the mass of people. So only because of such acharyas, as Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur, and later on, Srila Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur, are we able to distinguish between the genuine teachings of Lord Chaitanya, strictly according to the authorized scriptures, Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, Chaitanya Charitamrita, and the deviant teachings.

---
### renunciation, humility, guru-disciple relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=1121
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "His only possessions were the Tursi Mala he wore around his neck, and another set he kept in his hand for counting. He was extremely renounced, and genuinely so."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the arrival of Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj in Navadvipa Dhamma and his meeting with Siddhanta Saraswati, highlighting Babaji Maharaj's renunciation and the gifts he offered to Siddhanta Saraswati.

In 1898, after Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur had constructed his residence in Navadvipa Dhamma at Godruma Dwipa, and called it Svananda Sukhada Kunjal, and had also discovered Lord Chaitanya's birth site, Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj arrived in Navadvipa Dhamma, to see the site of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's birth, and to hear the discourses of Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur. Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj was singing in ecstasy when he arrived in Navadvipa. His only possessions were the Tursi Mala he wore around his neck, and another set he kept in his hand for counting. He was extremely renounced, and genuinely so. He kept a few books such as Narottam Das Thakur's Prarthana and Premabhakti Chambrika. Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji had been performing bhajana for many many years in Vrindavan. He was extremely renounced and was the disciple of another great renunciant, Bhagavad Das Babaji, who was the disciple of Jagannath Das Babaji Maharaj. Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji was not very well educated, and had been a grain merchant in his previous ashrama, but it was widely known that he was a liberated Paramahamsa of the highest caliber in the area of Vraja Mandala. At the time of his arrival, Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj was wearing a cap made of tiger skin, and was carrying a basket of various items and materials for his devotional service. He spotted Siddhanta Saraswati, and offered him four or five pieces of knotted rope for chanting rounds, a Tilak stamp with the initials for Hare Krishna, a tiger skin cap and paraphernalia for worship. Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj had received the basket and the cap from his spiritual master, Bhagavad Das Babaji Maharaj.

---
### humility, guru-disciple relationship, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=1270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Upon seeing my spiritual master, the extent of my worldly false ego diminished."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Siddhanta Saraswati's reflections on his attraction to Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj, emphasizing the diminishing of his false ego and the realization of the exemplary character of a Vaishnava.

Siddhanta Saraswati was very attracted to Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj. At the first sight, he was later to comment with humility, saying, "I have been busy within this material world trying to bring sense gratification within the grasp of my hand. I have often thought by obtaining the objects of sense gratification, all my shortcomings will be fulfilled. I often attained different facets that were indeed very rare, but my own personal shortcomings were never mitigated. In this material world, I have had the association of very high-class aristocratic persons. However, seeing their various deficiencies, I could not offer them praise. The most merciful Supreme Lord Sri Gaurasundara, that is Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, seeing me in such a lamentable condition at such a time of adversity, gave permission to two of his dear most devotees, Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur and Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj, to grant their blessings to me. Because I was always intoxicated with a worldly false ego, wanting again and again self-appraisal, I deprived myself of my own real benefit. But because of the influence of my previous birth's activities and devotional service, I came into the association of Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur, who was the form of my spiritual well-wisher. My spiritual master, from whom I took diksha, Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj, would go and visit Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur and many times would reside with him. Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur, out of his compassion for other living entities, pointed out my spiritual master, Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj. Upon seeing my spiritual master, the extent of my worldly false ego diminished. I knew that all the other living entities who have taken the human form of life were also fallen and low like myself. But by gradually observing the spiritual character of my spiritual master, I realized that only a Vaishnava could reside in this material world and be of exemplary character.

---
### initiation, guru-disciple relationship, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=1449
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If there is no mercy, I will give up my life."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Siddhanta Saraswati's persistent requests for initiation from Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj, highlighting the Babaji's initial reluctance, his seeking permission from Mahaprabhu, and Siddhanta Saraswati's eventual acceptance as a disciple after threatening to end his life.

So, Shastras say, a true Vaishnava is decorated with all good qualities. At Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur's urging, Siddhanta Saraswati approached Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji for initiation in the month of Magha of the year 1900. Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj expressed reluctance to accept Siddhanta Saraswati as a disciple, knowing him to be a very, very great scholar. Of course, Srila Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj was not known to be well-educated, but he was a liberated Paramahamsa, a devotee of the topmost caliber through his devotional service. So, when Siddhanta Saraswati pleaded with Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj for initiation, he got the reply that Mahaprabhu will be asked and if Mahaprabhu gives me permission, then only I will initiate you. So, Siddhanta Saraswati approached him again after some days and asked him, what was Mahaprabhu's order? Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj replied, I forgot to ask. The third time Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj was requested, he replied, I asked Mahaprabhu, but I did not receive the permission from Mahaprabhu to initiate you. Siddhanta Saraswati then stood up, his heart breaking, and said, "If there is no mercy, I will give up my life." After thus indicating his readiness to take his life, Siddhanta Saraswati began to cry. Srilank Gaur Kishodas Babaji Maharaj's heart was melted to see this. So, he told, Siddhanta Saraswati, go take your bath in the Saraswati river and then come back to me. At that time of initiation, Siddhanta Saraswati received the name Sri Varsha Bhanavi Devi Daita Das. This is another name for Adarani, Varsha Bhanavi Devi. So he is given the name of the servant of one who is very very dear to Radharan and that is Krishna. So he got the name as servant of Krishna.

---
### scholarship, preaching, encyclopedia
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/0F7Hf9xneec?t=1645
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** biography
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Let us pray to him for his blessings so that we can very rapidly make advancement in Krishna consciousness by his mercy."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the lecture by mentioning Srila Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur's travels, his commentary on the Chaitanya Charitamrita, his compilation of a Vaishnava encyclopedia, and his study of Vaishnava scriptures. He ends by encouraging listeners to pray for his blessings to advance in Krishna consciousness.

In 1898, Siddhanta Saraswati had accompanied his father to Kashi, Prayag and Gaya and had gathered many facts about all these holy places which were later incorporated into his commentary on the Chaitanya Charitam Rishis. This Chaitanya Charitam Rishis are called Anubhashya. So Srila Siddhanta Saraswati Thakur, whose name later was Sri Varsha Bhanavi Devi Daita Das, he was engaged in compilation of a Vaishnava encyclopedia which contained an explanation of Vaishnava terminology, brief biographies of great Vaishnavas, an introduction to the books of the Vaishnava Acharyas, a description of Vaishnava, a description of Vaishnava Tirthas and various conclusions of Vaishnava sects. So this compilation is a very very valuable compilation and the work was begun in Puri and continued as Siddhanta Saraswati made his preaching rounds of Sri Mayapur Dham, Sri Jagannath Puri Dham, Sri Vraja Dham. He also studied the various books of Sri Ramadvacharya and Sri Ramanujacharya and published some of them in Bengali. He published biographies of these and other Vaishnava saints in the Sajjana Toshani magazine of Srila Bhaktivinoda Thakur. So there are a lot more details. I don't have time to narrate the whole biography. In conclusion, today being the most auspicious day of his divine appearance, let us pray to him for his blessings so that we can very rapidly make advancement in Krishna consciousness by his mercy. Thank you very much, Hare Krishna.

---

## Srimad Bhagavad-gita - Chapter 10 Recitation | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | ISKCON Bangalore Sanga
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### recitation, bhakti, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Gdz_os4dGbU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "dadhāmi buddhi yogam tam yena mām upayanti te"
**Summary:** The speaker recites verses from Chapter 10 of the Bhagavad Gita, focusing on the opulence of the Absolute Truth and the path to attain Him through knowledge and devotion.

Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 Sankhya Yoga. Sankalpa Yogī Bhavati Kaścana. Ārurukṣor Muneryogam Kalmakāraṇa Mucyate. Yogārūḍhasya Tasyaivā Shamaḥ Kāraṇa Mucyate. Yadāyīn Vrīyārthēśu Nagarmasvanu Śajyate. Sarva Sankalpa Sanyāsī Yogārūḍhasya Dōchyate. Uddhare Rākma Nākmānam Nākmānam Avasādaye Ātmaivahyātmano Vandhur Ātmaivaripur Ātmanah. Vandhur Ātmātmanas Tasyaiyēn Ātmaivātmanā Jitah. Anātmanas Tuśatrutve Vartey Ātmaivaśatruvat. Jitātmanaf Prāśāntasya Paramātmā Samāhitah. Śrī Tōṣṇasuka Dukkheśu Tathā Mānāpamānai. Oho Jñāna-Vijñāna-Triptātmā Pūtas-Dhōvici-Tendriyaha. Yutta-Ittyuccate Yogī-Samalośkāśma-Kāñcanahā. Sūraṇ-Mitrāryu-Dāsīnā-Madhyas-Tadveśya-Vandhuśu. Sāduśvapicha-Pāpeśu-Samabuddhir-Viśiśyate.

## Srimad Bhagavatam 2.9.32 Lecture by HG Stoka Krishna Prabhu
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### verse recitation, spiritual realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By causeless mercy, all of me, namely my actual eternal form and my transcendental existence, color, qualities and activities, let all be awakened within you by factual realization out of my causeless mercy."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with the recitation of a verse and its translation, emphasizing the awakening of spiritual realization through causeless mercy.

Tathaiva Tattva Vijnanam. Astu Te Madh Anugraha. Yavanaham Yatha Bhaavo, Yad Rupa Unadarmakah. Tathaiva Tattva Vijnanam. Astu Te Madh Anugraha. Yavanaham Yatha Bhaavo, Yad Rupa Unadarmakah. Tathaiva Tattva Vijnanam. Astu Te Madh Anugraha. Yavanaham Yatha Bhaavo, Yad Rupa Unadarmakah. Tathaiva Tattva Vijnanam. Astu Te Madh Anugraha. Yavanaham Yatha Bhaavo. Activities, katha, so and so, eva, certainly, tattva vijnanam, factual realization, astur, let it be, te, unto you, mat, mind, anugraha, by causeless mercy, all of me, namely my actual eternal form and my transcendental existence, color, qualities and activities, let all be awakened within you by factual realization out of my causeless mercy, please repeat the translation.

---
### devotion, impersonalism, transcendental qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=206
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The impersonal interpretation of the Lord is not according to the divine and personal. The interpretation of the mundane wranglers is completely refuted in this verse because it is clearly stated here that the Supreme Lord has His qualities, form, pastimes and everything that a person has."
**Summary:** The speaker reads a purport discussing the Lord's transcendental qualities, his affection for devotees, and refutes impersonal interpretations, emphasizing the Lord's personal attributes and pastimes.

I'll continue reading the purport from the sixth paragraph. The Lord has unlimited numbers of transcendental qualities, and one of them is his affection for his unalloyed devotee. In the history of the mundane world we can appreciate this transcendental quality. The Lord incarnates himself for the protection of his devotees and for the annihilation of the faithless. His activities are in relationship with his devotees. Srimad Bhagavatam is full of such activities of the Lord in relationship with his devotees and the non-devotees have no knowledge of such pastimes. The Lord lifted the Govardhan hill when he was only seven years old and protected his pure devotees at Vrindavan from the wrath of Indra who was over flooding the place with rain. Now this lifting of the Govardhan hill by a seven-year-old boy may be unbelievable for the faithless but for the devotees it is absolutely believable. The devotee believes in the almighty potency of the Lord while the faithless says the Lord is Almighty but do not believe it. Such men with a poor fund of knowledge do not know that the Lord is the Lord eternally and that one cannot become the Lord by meditation for millions of years or by mental speculation for billions of years. The impersonal interpretation of the Lord is not according to the divine and personal. The interpretation of the mundane wranglers is completely refuted in this verse because it is clearly stated here that the Supreme Lord has His qualities, form, pastimes and everything that a person has. All these descriptions of the transcendental nature of the person that you have got it are factual realizations by the devotee of the Lord and because of this mercy of the Lord they are revealed to his pure devotee and to no one else. That's instantly what we have to propose.

---
### personal qualities, material qualities, nirguna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=394
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Please understand that nirguna means without any material qualities."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Lord is telling Brahma that Brahma can get factual realization of the personal feature of the absolute truth, the personality of God. He discusses the concept of 'nirguna' and clarifies that it means without material qualities, contrasting material qualities with personal qualities like feelings and affection.

They have spoken to Brahma and the Lord is telling Brahma that Brahma can get factual realization of the personal feature of the absolute truth, the personality of God. So one of the attributes of the personal feature is the transcendental qualities of the Lord. The impersonalists, when they hear about the word quality, dhūna, they immediately think or consider God is nirgūna. So nirgūna literally means without any qualities. But, the devotees are not those who don't know about the whole picture of the Divine. Please understand that nirguna means without any material qualities. Our experience in this world is that there are so many different material qualities. Every object, material object has got quality. Every person has quality. Now, the objects have qualities based on the material they are made of. This table, if it is a wooden table, it has got qualities of wood. If there is a metal table, it will have qualities of metal. So that we can understand, these are all material qualities based on the material of which the object is made. But what about a person of this world? What are his qualities like? So we can understand there are qualities of a person which are different than the qualities of different material objects when we consider a person. So person means he has feelings. He has got affection. He has got anger. He has got some compassion. So these are all personal qualities which are not to be found in any material object, in any dead thing, material thing. So these are qualities or attributes of a person.

---
### changing qualities, material qualities, anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=569
**Verse References:** BG 2.62-63
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So naturally, the conclusion will be that anger is actually a material quality, which is not a desirable quality. And certainly we don't expect that the absolute truth will have anger. Because absolute truth is nirguna, without any material qualities."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that personal qualities in this world are not permanent and are influenced by material nature (gunas). He discusses how anger is a material quality that should be controlled and contrasts this with the concept of the absolute truth being 'nirguna' or without material qualities.

But our experience in this world also is that the personal qualities are not permanent qualities. A person's qualities keep changing. Just like a child may display some playful behaviour, or playful qualities. The same child grows up and becomes a mature person. Then the playful qualities are not there. So we see these qualities of a person can change. So even though we understand, person's qualities are different than the qualities of this world, material objects, still, because the qualities are changing, we do not attribute those qualities to the soul, to the eternal soul within the body, to the real person. And Bhagavad Gita also describes that every person who takes birth in this world has got what is called as an acquired nature. Itchaa dvesha samutthena dvanga mohena bharata sarva bhuttani sammoham sargeyati paramtaka. At the beginning of creation, every person has got a certain acquired quality due to association with the gunas, with material nature. Karanam gunasamkosthir sarasadyogijanvasu. Everybody takes birth according to the association with the gunas and accordingly they have different qualities. So the qualities a person displays or appears to have are not spiritual qualities. They are all material qualities. Even though they are not like the qualities of matter, still they are material qualities. Which means to say that if the absolute truth is without material qualities, nirguna, then the absolute truth will not have any of the qualities that we have or we display. So if we display anger, then the scriptures also say you should control anger. Anger is not good for you. Anger may lead to so many dangerous things. Just like somebody becomes very angry, then they lose their discrimination, they lose their intelligence. Grodhar bhavati sammoha. One becomes bewildered, one's intelligence is lost, one's memory is completely bewildered. So, anger should be controlled. Anger is not a good quality. One should conquer anger. So naturally, the conclusion will be that anger is actually a material quality, which is not a desirable quality. And certainly we don't expect that the absolute truth will have anger, because absolute truth is nirguna, without any material qualities.

---
### Narasimha, impersonalism, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=810
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sometimes even God becomes angry when He comes in the material form. And then His anger has to come down. Just like a human being of this world. He becomes angry then he has to pacify himself or somebody has to pacify him. He has to give up this anger. Like that God also."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of how the Supreme Lord can display anger, as seen in the incarnation of Narasimha, if the absolute truth is without material qualities. He explains the impersonalists' view that such incarnations are material forms and refutes this by discussing the nature of compassion and its potential for being misplaced.

So then how do we understand that the Supreme Lord appears in His form as Narasimha and He displays anger? So, the impersonalists will say, Oh, that incarnation of Narasimha is a material incarnation, a material form. Material form, naturally there will be anger. And that anger has to be controlled. And that is why the impersonalists will say, Oh, Bhagavatam is described, the Lord was very angry, Narasimha was very angry. Then Prahlad Maharaj went and pacified the Lord. And then the Lord became free from anger. And then very lovingly He placed His lotus hand on Prahlad and blessed him. Sometimes even God becomes angry when He comes in the material form. And then His anger has to come down, just like a human being of this world. He becomes angry then he has to pacify himself or somebody has to pacify him. He has to give up this anger. Like that God also. So they say that Absolute Truth actually doesn't have any form. But when the Absolute Truth incarnates then they may, the Absolute Truth may accept some material form. And that material form may display sometimes anger, sometimes display compassion, even compassion. Compassion also, the impersonalists think, if a person has compassion, that compassion sometimes we see is misplaced. Just like a mother is very very compassionate with her child. But we also know when the mother does not strictly correct the child and the child grows up with some pampering by the mother then everybody will say mother has spoiled the child. So she should not have shown compassion for the child when it was not properly placed, improperly placed compassion, undue compassion. So even this compassion, this materialistic, this impersonalism will say that's also a material quality. That quality also should be absent in the Lord or in the Absolute Truth, it should not be there. This is material quality. So impersonalists will argue that all these qualities we see in a person of this world are all material qualities.

---
### impersonalism, transcendental qualities, temporary personality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=974
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Still our experience is a temporary personality. A temporary personality."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain the impersonalists' arguments, stating that they believe the Absolute Truth is impersonal and without material qualities. He then contrasts this with the Bhagavatam's description of the Absolute Truth as a transcendental person with transcendental qualities, which cannot be understood through material conceptions.

So there is no question of Absolute Truth having compassion, Absolute Truth having anger, Absolute Truth having impersonal qualities. So it goes on to prove their point apparently that Absolute Truth is impersonal. It does not have personality, because person means having some material qualities. But the Bhagavatam describes that Absolute Truth is a transcendental person and Absolute Truth has got transcendental qualities. Now what are these transcendental qualities? What is this transcendental personality? This cannot be understood if we try to understand through our material conception of a person. In this world we only have experience of what is a material conception of a person. We know of people who take birth and they may be having a good birth, they may be having a very good education, they may be having so many good qualities. But we also know that ultimately even such persons with good qualities, good birth, good education, good upbringing, they also ultimately die. They also become old. They also lose their all their qualities. The qualities they may display for some time but it is all ultimately lost. So the experience we have of a person in this world however great the person may be, however powerful he may be, he may be a great person, however nice qualities the person may display, still our experience is a temporary personality.

---
### eternal person, transcendental qualities, incarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=1119
**Verse References:** BG 10.12-13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "You are eternally a person. Purusham shashvatam, purusham means person, shashvatam means eternal person. Dhiyam, you are a transcendental person."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the concept of an eternal person, contrasting it with the temporary personalities we experience in the material world. He quotes Bhagavad Gita, where Arjuna describes Krishna as the supreme Brahman, the supreme pure, and eternally a person with transcendental qualities.

But the Bhagavatam goes on to describe there is another kind of personality, an eternal person. Purusham shashvatam dhiyam. Bhagavad Gita describes in the words of Arjuna, Arjuna describes Krishna param brahma paramdhama pavitram paramam bhavan purusham shashvatam dhiyam eternally the supreme person, the transcendental person, param brahma, the supreme brahma, paramdhama, the supreme resting place for everyone and everything. param brahma paramdhama pavitram paramam supreme pure pavitram paramam bhavan you Krishna, you are the supreme Brahman, you are the supreme above, you are the supreme pure. You are eternally a person. Purusham shashvatam, purusham means person, shashvatam means eternal person. Dhiyam, you are a transcendental person. Adidevam, you are the origin of all devatas. Devatas have so many good qualities in this world. Compared to humans, compared to other created beings, devatas have so many good qualities. They have beautiful form. They have long lifespan. They have noble qualities. So, even those devatas, they have an origin. Adidevam, that is Krishna. Adidevam. Ajam, Krishna is unformed. But, Ajam, Ajopisan, Avyayatma, even though Krishna is actually unborn, he appears to take birth. Our Ajopisan, Avyayatma, Bhutana, Vishwopisan, Prakritim, Svamudishthaya, Sambhavami, Krishna appears in this world. Krishna takes birth, appears to take birth. That birth is described as Jarmakarma, Jame, Divyam, that is transcendental appearance, transcendental birth. That birth is part of his pastimes, Nila, transcendental activity, part of his transcendental activity, taking birth. So, there is a different kind of personality, not within our experience normally, but which is described in the scriptures like Bhagavatam and Bhagavad Gita.

---
### material conceptions, transcendental reciprocation, internal potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=1276
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, when the Lord displays anger, it is transcendental anger. Out of his affection for his devotees."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the need to give up material conceptions when understanding the personality of Godhead. He explains that the anger displayed by the Lord, such as in the form of Narasimha, is not a result of material modes but a transcendental reciprocation with His devotees.

And, those type of qualities or appearance or activities or transcendental form or transcendental name, all these are described in the confidential portions of the Vedic literature. So, when we hear about the personality of God from the scriptures, it is clearly stated in the scriptures that we should give up all our material conceptions of form, person, qualities or activities. We have to give up all those conceptions. To help us give up those conceptions, the Vedanta philosophy says the absolute truth is nirguna, nirakara, nirmishesha, nirmikalpa. So, all the negation is negation of material qualities, material form, material personality, material attributes. And, at the same time, there are transcendental attributes, satchidananda-vikraha, janma-karma-chame-diljam, sambhavami-atma-mayaya. All these are transcendental attributes, spiritual qualities, spiritual names, spiritual forms, spiritual qualities. So, these are entirely different from material qualities. Now, in the Bhagavad Gita, we can clearly understand from the Bhagavad Gita that if a person becomes angry, kama-esha, krodha-esha, rajo-guna-samut-bhava, it is a product of rajo-guna. Whereas, please put your mobiles on silent mode, it disturbs the class. Whereas, the material qualities a person displays, like kama, krodha, are a product of rajo-guna. Krishna's qualities are not a product of any of the gunas. He is gunatika. He is above the material modes. So, when the Lord appears, Absolute Truth appears as Narsimha, He displays anger that is not a result of rajo-guna, because He is completely transcendental to the modes. Then, what is that anger? That anger is actually one of the transcendental reciprocation of the Lord with His devotees.

### Prahlad, Hiranyakashipu, transcendental anger
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=1462
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Why did you torture my son, my devotee?"
**Summary:** The speaker illustrates the concept of transcendental anger through the story of Hiranyakashipu and Prahlad. He explains how Hiranyakashipu's torture of Prahlad, a pure devotee, caused the Lord to become angry, demonstrating the Lord's affection for His devotees.

Just like Hiranyakashipu, he was giving so much trouble to Pralad, even though Pralad was his own son. And the only fault of this boy was that he was chanting the holy names of the Supreme Lord, Vishnu, Narayana. And Hiranyakashipu, with some misunderstanding, thought that Vishnu or Narayana is his enemy. So, Hiranyakashipu was telling his son, Why are you glorifying or chanting the name of my enemy? So, Pralad tried to explain very gently, very patiently, very nicely, Vishnu is not your enemy. Vishnu is nobody's enemy. Vishnu is nobody's anything. Vishnu is everybody's best friend, topmost well-wisher, Surudam Sarvabhutanam. But Hiranyakashipu would not try to understand, would not agree. He only became more and more angry and more and more inimical, to Pralad. So, out of his anger, inimical attitude, he began to give trouble to Pralad, so much so that the boy was tortured. He was tortured mercilessly. And that made the Lord very angry against Hiranyakashipu. Why did you torture my son, my devotee? So, when the Lord displays anger, it is transcendental anger.

### transcendental anger, internal potency, gunas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=1636
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this display of anger is called transcendental anger. Why? Because such anger is not a result of Prajopuna. Why? Because the Lord is unaffected by the Gunas. He is completely transcendently situated above the Gunas."
**Summary:** The speaker explains why the Lord's anger is called transcendental anger, emphasizing that it is not a result of material modes (gunas) because the Lord is the controller and source of all gunas. He clarifies that the Lord employs His internal potency for the sake of His devotees.

And in that anger, he actually killed Hiranyakashipu and all his supporters also, who came to attack Lord Narasimha. So, this display of anger is called transcendental anger. Why? Why is it called transcendental anger? It appears like, you know, anger which is there in this material world, which is displayed by any ordinary person. No, it is not, because such anger is not a result of Prajopuna. Why? Because the Lord is unaffected by the Gunas. He is completely transcendently situated above the Gunas. How is that he is above the Gunas? Because he is the controller, the master, the source of all the Gunas. All the Gunas are being controlled by the Lord and they are employed for a different purpose by the Lord. So, when he displays anger, it is his internal potency, his Atmamayam or Yogamayam. Lord has got different energies. One of his energies called his internal potency is employed by the Lord for the sake of his devotees. So, for the sake of Prahlad, the Lord employed his internal potency and using his internal potency he displayed anger. He displayed affection for Prahlad. He blessed the demigods. The demigods were so much harassed by his indecency. So, the demigods prayed to the Supreme Lord that you have to do something to stop this terrorist activity, the terrorist activities of Hiranyakashipu. He had terrorized the whole universe. So, the demigods prayed to the Lord. So, the Lord in response to the demigods prayers, he came and finished off this terrorist Hiranyakashipu. So, the Lord employs his internal potency for the sake of his activities, for the sake of displaying transcendental qualities, for the sake of protecting his devotees. And that is completely free from the interactions of the kundas. The internal potency is completely a different potency, a different energy. It is completely spiritual.

---
### liberation, karma, devotees
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=1792
**Verse References:** BG 9.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, from that we can understand the Lord's killing is as good as his protection. Both are meant for the ultimate welfare of the particular person being killed or protected."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that when the Lord kills someone, it is an act of liberation due to the Lord's internal potency. He contrasts this with death under the influence of material modes, which leads to rebirth and karmic reactions. He also addresses the apparent partiality of the Lord towards devotees, clarifying that the Lord is equal to all but favors those who are devoted to Him.

And the effect of the internal potency is that when the Lord kills anybody because the Lord employs his internal potency, the person who is killed is actually liberated. Generally, any person in this world who dies under the influence of the kundas, such a person has to take birth again in this material world and has to undergo the reactions of his karma, past karma. Whereas, when the Lord kills somebody, such a person killed by the Lord is very fortunate that he becomes purified of all karma, all sinful reactions. He becomes completely purified by the touch of the Lord. And also, the person gets liberation. That liberation is not possible if a person dies under the influence of the kundas. So, from that we can understand the Lord's killing is as good as his protection. Both are meant for the ultimate welfare of the particular person being killed or protected. So, when the Lord protects his devotees or the Lord kills the demons, both of them are benefited by the Lord transcendentally. Both of them are getting spiritual benefit from the Lord. It appears like the Lord is partial to the lay devotees. The Lord is partial to the devotees. But actually, the Lord says in the Bhagavad Gita, samoham sarva bhuteshu na me deshu astina priyaha. Nobody is the object of envy of the Lord. Nobody is the object of special favor of the Lord. Then, we see that the Lord is sometimes favoring his devotees. Whatever is his favor? How do we understand? That's explained here with Prabhupada, that the Lord lifted a golden hill when he was only seven years old and protected his pure devotees at Vrndavana from the wrath of Indra.

---
### Govardhan Hill, Indra, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=1919
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, therefore, he came and begged forgiveness and he offered wonderful prayers to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts the story of Krishna lifting Govardhan Hill to protect the Vrajavasis from Indra's wrath. He explains how Krishna stopped the Indra Yajna and instructed the Vrajavasis to worship Govardhan, leading to Indra's anger and subsequent torrential rains. Krishna then lifted Govardhan Hill to provide shelter, causing Indra to realize his mistake and offer prayers to Krishna.

The Vrajavasis once they were preparing for the Indra Yajna because they were wanting to offer their gratitude to Indra who supplies rain. The Vrajavasis, they are agriculturists. They depend on the land and cows. So, they require water. So, that water being supplied by Indra as the controller of rains, annually they would worship Indra by performing one Indra Yajna. So, Krishna, when he was seven years old in Vrndavana, when the Vrajavasis were getting ready for one Indra Yajna, he stopped them from doing Indra Yajna. And he told, instead of worshipping Indra, you worship Govardhana. So, the Vrajavasis, because they are very attached to Krishna, very affectionate to Krishna, they agreed to follow Krishna's direction to not worship Indra, but to worship Govardhana. So, when they worshipped Govardhana, Indra became angry. Why are these Vrajavasis not worshipping me? So, I will teach them a lesson. So, he became angry against the Vrajavasis and he sent forth torrential rains, devastating rains. And when the Vrajavasis were harassed in this way, they approached Krishna and told him to protect them against this torrential rains. So, what did Krishna do? Krishna simply lifted the door of the hill and protected the Vrajavasis. Then, Indra realized his mistake. Indra realized his mistake. And Indra came, he stopped the rainfall and came and apologized to Krishna, offered what he prays to Krishna. He understood who is Krishna, not an ordinary covered void, not simply some powerful yogi, because Krishna lifted the door and protected the inhabitants of Vrindavanam. So, somebody may think Krishna is some powerful yogi. No. Krishna is not a powerful yogi. Not just another covered void. Not simply some attractive child who is the center of attraction of the Vrajavasis. No. He is actually the Supreme Lord who is the master of all the devas, all the devas. So, Indra understood that Krishna is my master. He is my well-wisher. So, therefore, he came and begged forgiveness and he offered wonderful prayers to Krishna.

---
### devatas, demons, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=2083
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, the only way to understand this aspect of the Absolute Truth, the personality aspect, whether it is personal qualities, or personal form, or personal name, or the absolute truth of the personality of Godhead, transcendental personality, we cannot understand without the truth."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the understanding of devatas, who are devotees and can correct their mistaken understanding of Krishna through His grace, with that of demons, who cannot understand the personality of Krishna due to their non-devotional nature. He emphasizes that devotional service is the only way to understand the personality of Godhead.

So, the devatas, because they are devotees, even if sometimes they mistake Krishna to be an ordinary person, by Krishna's grace, their mistaken understanding of Krishna is corrected by Krishna. Their mistaken understanding is corrected by Krishna. And they actually offer wonderful prayers to Krishna. They actually beg forgiveness from Krishna. They actually seek Krishna's protection and blessings. Whereas the demons, because they are non-devotees, therefore they cannot understand the personality of Krishna. They cannot understand the incarnations of the Lord. They cannot understand the personality of Vishnu. They categorize Vishnu as a person of this world. They categorize Vishnu as one of the devatas. They categorize Vishnu to be just another powerful devata. But they cannot understand Vishnu is not just another powerful devata. He is not a person of this world. He is completely a transcendental personality. He is the master of the gunas. He is the master of the devatas. He is the source of everything that they cannot understand. So, non-devotees, whether they be demons, whether they be impersonalists, whether they be yogis, whether they be jnanis, whether they be tapasvis, whether they be pious actors, they can never understand the personality of Godhead, Vishnu or Krishna or Narasimha or Vamana or Narayana, they cannot understand as long as they don't take the devotional service. So, the only way to understand this aspect of the Absolute Truth, the personality aspect, whether it is personal qualities, or personal form, or personal name, or the absolute truth of the personality of Godhead, transcendental personality, we cannot understand without the truth.

### bhakti, Absolute Truth, Supreme Person
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=2223
**Verse References:** BG 18.55
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, only by bhakti it is possible to understand the Absolute Truth as a person."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Bhagavad Gita to emphasize that only through bhakti (devotional service) can one truly understand the Absolute Truth as a person. He concludes that Krishna is the Supreme Person, the source of everything, and that Brahma is receiving this realization through the mercy of Vishnu.

So, Bhagavad Gita, it is said, bhakti-yamā abhijānāti yamā yaccha-asmi tattva-tahā. In truth, if you want to understand what is the nature of the personality of the Absolute Truth, then you have to take the devotional service. bhakti-yamā abhijānāti yamā yaccha-asmi tattva-tahā. So, only by bhakti it is possible to understand the Absolute Truth as a person. And that person is the supreme ultimate manifestation of the Absolute Truth. The supreme, the source of even Brahman and Paramatma, the source of the impersonal Brahman, the source of all energies, the source of everything and everyone. So, that source is ultimately the Supreme Person, Krishna. And that is what the Bhagavad Gita says. So, Brahman is now given the realization of the Absolute Truth as the Supreme Person. That is what Brahman is getting the realization through the mercy of Vishnu. And that is being described here by the Supreme Bhagavad Gita.

### location, material space, mind
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/MVo8wPtN4eA?t=2300
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Mind is where your thoughts are. That is where the mind is."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to answer a question about location, distinguishing between material space and the location of the mind. He explains that the mind is not a physical object and cannot be located in physical space, but rather is located where one's thoughts are.

Can the staff have any questions? Now, when we talk of location, we are talking of material space. Just like I am sitting here or all of us are sitting in this hall. So, we are not in some other place. We may have a residence or we may have a place of residence. So, if you are here, you are not in your home. If you are near home, you are not in the temple, right? So, we say a person is in a particular place based on where his body is, correct? After all, the body is matter. Matter or material object can have a material location or place. Even when it comes to subtle matter, this is gross matter. For example, a person is sitting in this hall, but mentally he can be somewhere else. He can be in his home, right? He is sitting here, but he is thinking of his own. Maybe he has left his child and come. Oh, child! He is afraid. Where is my father? Where has he gone? Child has been waiting, anxiously waiting for me to go back home. So, I have to return immediately, right? So, mentally he may be not here. Mentally, he can be somewhere else. So, where is the mind? If I ask you, where is the mind? Is it here? Or is it there? Or is it in America? So, what is the answer? Where is the mind? Mind is where your thoughts are. That is where the mind is. Generally, our thoughts are centered around our body. Therefore, we say the mind is inside here. But actually, the mind cannot be located in physical space because mind is not a physical object.

---

## Srimad Bhagavatam 3.16.10 by HH Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### duty, protection, sin
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those who are helpless must be taken care of by their respective guardians. Otherwise, the guardians will be subjected to the punishment of Yamaraj who is appointed by the lord to supervise the activities of sinful living creatures."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on the translation and purport of a verse emphasizing the importance of protecting brahmanas, cows, and defenseless creatures, and the consequences of neglecting this duty.

Translation: The brahmanas, the cows, and the defenseless creatures are my own body. Those whose faculty of judgment has been impaired by their own sin look upon these as distinct from me. They are just like furious serpents and they are angrily torn apart by the bills of the vulture-like messengers of Yamaraj, the superintendent of sinful persons. The defenseless creatures according to Brahma Samhita are the cows, brahmanas, women, children, and old men. Of these five, the brahmanas and cows are especially mentioned in this verse because the Lord is always anxious about the brahmanas. The Lord's blessings are the benefit of the brahmanas and the cows and he is prayed to in this way. The Lord especially instructs, therefore, that no one should be envious of these five, especially the cows and brahmanas. In some of the Bhagavatam readings, the word duhitruh is used instead of duhatihi but in either case the meaning is the same. Duhatihi means cow. Tuhithruhu can also be used to mean cow because the cow is supposed to be the daughter of the sun god. Just as children are taken care of by the parents, women as a class should be taken care of by the father, husband or grown-up sons. Those who are helpless must be taken care of by their respective guardians. Otherwise, the guardians will be subjected to the punishment of Yamaraj who is appointed by the lord to supervise the activities of sinful living creatures. The assistants or messengers of Yamaraj are likened here to vultures. And those who do not execute their respective duties in protecting their wards are compared to serpents. Vultures deal very seriously with serpents. And similarly, the messengers will deal very seriously with neglectful guardians. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport.

---
### brahmanas, cows, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=233
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The whole of Krishna Consciousness is dependent on Brahmanical culture, protection of Brahmanical culture."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of protecting brahmanas and cows, stating that Krishna is the protector of these groups and that the welfare of the world depends on their protection.

So, this is a verse spoken by the Supreme Lord Narayana. He is speaking to the Kumaras. He is particularly mentioning, not only in this verse but the past few verses, the position of the Brahmanas as so very, very, very important. The whole of Krishna Consciousness is dependent on Brahmanical culture, protection of Brahmanical culture. That's why, every day when we offer food to Krishna, we particularly chant this prayer: Namo Brahmanya Devaya. Go Brahman. Go Brahman Hitaya Cha. Jagadhitaya Krishnaya Govindaya Namo Namah. So, Krishna is the protector especially of the Brahmanas and cows. He is actually the protector of everyone. Jagadhitaya. Go Brahman Hitaya. Jagadhitaya. Simply by giving protection to cows and brahmanas and cows, the entire world can benefit. Welfare activity is possible for the entire world by simply protecting brahmanas and cows. So the king or the governmental head has to particularly protect the brahmanas and cows. Actually shastra says five categories of persons are to be particularly protected by the king or the guardians: defenseless creatures, bhutani alabdha sharanani, defenseless creatures. Otherwise if they are not, particularly these five are not protected, they can be exploited. They can be harmed very easily. Who are they? The cows, the brahmanas, the women, the children and old men. They can be harmed very, very, they are defenseless. And among these five categories of defenseless creatures, two are singled out in that special prayer: namo brahmanya devaya go brahmana hitaya jagad hitaya krishnaya govindaya namo namah. But in this verse, the lord is particularly mentioning, lord narayan is mentioning dvijavaran, duhatir, bhutani alabdha sharanani, dvijavaran, dvijavaran, dvijavaran, dvijavaran, dvijavaran, dvijavaran, the brahmanas.

---
### brahmanas, cows, scripture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Cow is also considered to be non different from the supreme lord."
**Summary:** This segment explains the meaning of 'dvijavaran' and 'duhatihi' in the verse, clarifying that 'dvijavaran' refers to the best among the twice-born, the brahmanas, and 'duhatihi' refers to the cow, considered the daughter of the sun god.

Dvija refers to all those who are twice born, brahmana, kshatriya, vaisha, but here dvijavaran is used to indicate among those who are twice born the best are the brahmanas as a class. Brahmana, kshatriya, vaisha, is all are called dvija. Dvija is used to indicate but dvijavara, dvijavara or dvijavaran, in plural, refers to the brahmanas and dvihatihi. Some readings of the bhagavatam, there is dvihitruhu. Dvihitru means literally means daughter, but in this context it is referring to the daughter of the brahmana, the daughter of the sun god. Which daughter? The cow. The cow is considered the daughter of the sun god. Who is the other daughter of the sun god? Who is famous? Yamuna. Mitrasya putri. Mitra means sun god. Mitrasya putri. There is one prayer offered by chetana maha prabhu when he was travelling, he said when he was travelling, he came across the river ganga, he was travelling in bengal, and in his ecstasy of krishna consciousness, he thought the ganga to be yamuna and he offered wonderful prayers, prayer, one prayer he offered to yamuna, there he refers to yamuna as mitrasya putri, and he said the daughter of the sun god, the daughter of the sun god. So here it is also explained by prabhupada in the purport, the cow is supposed to be the daughter of the sun god, dohitruhu. So in either reading the meaning is the same. Cow is also considered to be non different from the supreme lord, but my own body. As far as supreme lord is concerned, there is no difference between his body and his soul. So it is the lord himself. In our case, our body and our soul are different. Whereas in the case of pure devotees, the supreme lord krishna, anyone with a spiritual form, their body and their soul are identical, not different, they are identical. So the lord is telling yeme tanur dvijavaraan, duhatir madhiya, bhutani alabda sharanani. All these defenseless creatures the cows, the brahmanas the women children and old men are my own body, are myself.

Its sinful to maintain hatred, to be envious of brahmanas or cows or children or women or old men is actually sinful and such sinful persons are punishable by yamadutas. And how are they punished? They are torn apart by the messengers of yamaraj.

### hell, punishment, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=961
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment describes the nature of punishment in hell, explaining that the body is torn apart and that the suffering is extreme. It also discusses how souls are prepared for different births through punishment in hell.

See if in the material world somebody's body is torn apart, it is instant death, instant death. They cannot survive for long. Somebody's body is torn apart. Just like narsimha, he tore apart the body of very angrily, he was very much in anger against hiranyakashipu because hiranyakashipu tortured pralad, that's why he was very angry, my devotee, dear most devotee, you have tortured. So he was very angry. So he tore apart with his nails. So the yamadutas when they punish, they tear apart the body of the the sinful people. Now in hell, which is also sometimes called yamaloka. Yamarat doesn't reside in hell, but he has got his superintendence over hell, hellish planets, 26 kinds of hellish planets described in 5th canto of the bhagavatam, last chapter. So that's sometimes called yamaloka. So in yamaloka those who are to be punished, first of all when somebody is taken to yamaraj, they are not taken in the gross body, they are taken in the subtle body and after that subtle body they are taken to yamaraj. If the punishment is that they have to take birth in some particular family in the human form of life, then they are sent to the womb of a particular mother and father, human being species. But if they are to be degraded and taken birth as a hog, then first they have to be punished in hell to accustom them to eating stool and living like a hog, because the mentality has to change. The mind has to get adjusted to enter hogs body.

### hell, punishment, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=1133
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The suffering due to the punishment in hell is not like any other suffering in any other planet in any body. It is totally extreme."
**Summary:** This segment details the 'yatana sharira,' a special body given to those punished in hell, designed to endure extreme suffering repeatedly without being destroyed, emphasizing the unparalleled severity of hellish punishment.

So they are punished in hell and that punishment is not in the subtle body. The subtle body is covered by one yatana sharira, one special body and that body is meant for just inflicting punishment, that's all. Yatana means suffering due to punishment. Yatana means suffering due to punishment. So there is a special punishment body that is awarded to them, just like prisoners are given one prison dress to identify prisoners, like that this sinful people who are to be punished in hell, they are given one yatana sharira. That yatana sharira, what is the speciality? Yatana sharira, just like this gross body if it is torn apart, it will not remain, but supposing they are to be punished by their body being torn apart hundred times, that yatana sharira doesn't get destroyed like this gross body. It is like the subtle body, like the subtle body, but it is not subtle body. So they can be torn apart hundred times and each time they will be like suffering suffering crying, screaming, extreme pain, hundred times they have to undergo. If they have to be beaten with one whip ten thousand times, one lakh times, material world somebody is beaten so many times they will not even survive they will be finished. I don't know how many times they can withstand the beating, may be hundred times, few hundred times I don't know, certainly nobody can withstand ten thousand times beating, it will be like beating a dead person after few times, but whereas yatana sharira, ten thousand times, that yatana sharira will not become destroyed, it will not get destroyed, it will not become deformed and it will keep on taking that beating and pain and suffering, suffering suffering suffering. This is punishment in hell. So the suffering due to the punishment in hell is not like any other suffering in any other planet in any body. It is totally extreme. There can't be more suffering, experience of suffering than the experience of suffering due to punishment in hell.

### sin, repentance, forgiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=1841
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Degrees of sin are there, accordingly the remedy for becoming free from such sins is also correspondingly."
**Summary:** This segment explains how to rectify sinful activities committed in the mind, through words, or physically, emphasizing the importance of realizing the mistake and being repentant, and that the degree of repentance should correspond to the severity of the sin.

Maintaining enviousness, all the offences that we commit in the mind, not offences, sinful activities that we may perform in the mind can be rectified by realizing the mistake and being repentant. Being repentant at the mind level, mentally we can ask forgiveness. But at the level of words if somebody offends, then they have to ask for pardon using the tongue, actually go and ask pardon or actually beg forgiveness, not mentally verbal mentally. And if physically somebody has been we committed a sin, then physically we have to beg forgiveness by bowing down using our body. You see, so mentally if somebody commits sins, then then realizing within one's own mind and being repentant is enough because the degree of sin in the mind is not as severe as the degree of sin committed by words and the degree of sin committed through physical violence or physical abuse or physical action is not as sorry, the physical one is very more serious than even verbal sin. So degrees of sin are there, accordingly the the remedy for becoming free from such sins is also correspondingly.

### offenses, Vaishnavas, forgiveness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=2005
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** This segment contrasts the process of seeking forgiveness for material sins with offenses against Vaishnavas, stating that offenses against Vaishnavas require physical, verbal, and mental repentance, including bowing down and verbally asking for forgiveness.

Mentally verbally or physically we have to beg forgiveness. But in the case of offenses against Vaishnavas, offense against Vaishnavas, even if mentally we have offended a Vaishnava, we cannot mentally beg forgiveness and expect that we will be absolved of the offense no, it doesn't work like that. What did lord Narayana say, lord Vishnu say to advise to Durvasambali when he offended when Durvasambali offended Amrish Maharaj, said go to Amrish Maharaj and fall at his feet. So it is Vaishnava prad, you have to bow down, you have to actually ask for forgiveness and in the mind also you should be very repentant and we should mindfully ask forgiveness, all the three levels we have to. That's why when we pay obeisances to Vaishnavas every morning Vaan Chha Kalpat Rubh Yasha, we have to physically bow down, we have to also vibrate that prayer using our tongue, not mentally chant, we have to verbally chant and we also have to our mind should be there in the act of offering obeisances and chanting the prayer. So Vaishnava prad is very serious whereas at the level of on the material platform sinful activities, there is consideration of levels of sin, mind words or body. Ok I will stop here any questions.

### punishment, state law, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=2149
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "If the person is not punished, definitely he is going to be punished by Yamaraj."
**Summary:** This segment addresses the difference between punishment given by state laws and Yamaraj, stating that if state punishment is commensurate with the sin, there is no further punishment by Yamaraj, using the example of murder and capital punishment.

Now you can loudly ask the question. Punishment given by state laws is different from punishment given by Yamaraj. Yes it is different, but if the if the punishment is commensurate with the particular mistake or the sin that has been done, then there is no punishment by Yamaraj. Just like somebody has committed a murder, I will give an example, it is a very gross example, but that is the example very easily understandable, somebody has committed a murder. So if the person is not punished, definitely he is going to be punished by Yamaraj. If the person is punished by the state, the only punishment which can absolve the person is capital punishment. That means he also has to be killed as a punishment, he has to be killed. Now supposing the state law pardons him, nowadays they are after capital punishment to be abolished, it is very very inhuman, it is considered inhuman. So if they don't give capital punishment for a murderer, then he is going to be punished by Yamaraj. He is going to be punished by Yamaraj like that.
