### protection, varnasankara, spiritual practice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=2439
**Verse References:** SB 1.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Old age is the chance to completely focus and dedicate themselves for spiritual practice."
**Summary:** This segment explains why brahmanas, cows, women, children, and old men should be protected, stating that women should be protected to generate Krishna conscious progeny, children should be protected to instill good values, and old men should be protected to focus on spiritual practice.

Maharaj, in purport it is explained these five kind of people to be protected like brahmana, cows, women, children and old person. So like brahmanas they are protected because they are carrying or giving this brahmanical culture to the society. So they should be protected. Then cows like they are maintaining. So like old person or children could you please elaborate. Why they can be protected other three. That is explained in the first canto 13th chapter. Women should be protected because the women are meant to generate Krishna conscious progeny. Krishna conscious. Human form of life is very precious and the women are properly protected. Then only there will be good population, satsantan. First chapter prabhupada explains no. Why women should be protected. Unprotected women. That's what Arjuna is telling. If women are unprotected then there will be varnasankara population. Criminals will be born. So women should be protected so that there is good population. Children should be protected because in the child stage when they are taught good values, good habits, good Krishna consciousness imparted. Then they grow up to be very nice citizens, very nice adults. They are given proper protection when they are children. Then they grow up to be varnasankara population. Children should be protected. Old men should be protected because old age is the chance to completely focus and dedicate themselves for spiritual practice. To prepare themselves for a better next life. That's why Shastras say, compulsorily after 50 years one should retire from household life and become engaged in spiritual activities exclusively. Panchashordham vanam vrajet, jet means must go compulsorily, that's a Shastric injunction.

### duty, responsibility, sin
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=2613
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everybody has to perform their particular duty or responsibility irrespective of whether they are involved in a particular activity or not."
**Summary:** This segment addresses the question of witnessing sin, stating that witnessing sin is not sinful, but being a mute spectator and failing to fulfill one's duty or responsibility is sinful, using the example of a father seeing his son ill-treating his mother.

Like if someone witnesses some sin, what is, how it, will it affect the person who witnesses someone, someone's sin? Witnessing the sin is not sinful. But if somebody is a mute spectator, just like I am seeing something is happening and I am not doing something which I am supposed to do. Just like to give a simple example, a father is seeing the son is ill-treated. Why? Ill-treating the mother. Now the father as a husband has got responsibility for his wife. So he can't be just standing and or watching telling, I am not harming her, no. So then he has to act. So everybody has to perform their particular duty or responsibility irrespective of whether they are involved in a particular activity or not. Just like the king, he may say, I am not killing cow, somebody else is killing cow. No. So he stands there and he is trying to act. And he tries to prevent that cow killing. Because he is responsible. If he takes no action for preventing cow killing, then the reaction of the cow killing, the king will also get.

### animal killing, responsibility, karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mP4llS4aXh4?t=2753
**Verse References:** SB 1.3.4-5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "All of them are actually like a gang of conspirators to kill a person."
**Summary:** This segment explains that animal killing is considered murder according to God's law, and that not just the person who physically kills the animal, but also those who rear, transport, sell, cook, and eat the meat are responsible and will be punished.

Prabhupad mentioned this in 1st Cantau. In 1st Parpot. 3. 4. 5. It is considered murder according to the laws of God. So, who are all responsible for the murder? Not just the person who killed, physically actually did the killing. It is the person who reared the animal, person who transported that animal. You see? The person who was responsible for selling the meat, person who is cooking the meat and person who is eating the meat. All of them are actually like a gang of conspirators to kill a person. This is state law also. If there is a gang involved in a murder, all the members of the gang are held responsible for the murder. They are all punished by state law. This is state law actually. Similarly, there is a gang of criminals according to God's law in animal killing

### Lakshmi, Tulasi, Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zibveUdVoPA?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The goddess of fortune Lakshmi, the dust of whose feet is worn on the head by others, waits upon you as appointed, for she is anxious to secure a place in the abode of the king of bees who hovers on the fresh wreath of tulasi leaves offered at your feet by some blessed devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker recites and repeats the translation of a verse describing Lakshmi's eagerness to serve the Lord's lotus feet, where the king of bees hovers over Tulasi leaves.

Is anxious to secure translation and purport by his divine grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Pavupada. Translation: The goddess of fortune Lakshmi, the dust of whose feet is worn on the head by others, waits upon you as appointed, for she is anxious to secure a place in the abode of the king of bees who hovers on the fresh wreath of tulasi leaves offered at your feet by some blessed devotee. Please repeat this translation: The goddess of fortune Lakshmi, the dust of whose feet is worn on the head is worn on the head by others, waits upon you as appointed for she is anxious to secure a place in the abode of the king of bees who hovers on the fresh wreath of tulasi leaves offered at your feet by some blessed devotee.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The conclusion is that no one can be the benefactor of the Lord, everyone is actually the servant of the servant of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker reads Srila Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing that Tulasi's qualities are due to her placement at the Lord's feet, and that everyone, including Lakshmi, serves the Lord, highlighting the Lord's independence.

Purport by Srila Prabhupada: As previously described, tulasi has attained all superior qualities due to being placed at the lotus feet of the Lord. The comparison made here is very nice, as the king of bees hovers over the tulasi leaves offered to the lotus feet of the Lord, so Lakshmi, the goddess who is sought by the demigods, brahmanas, Vaishnavas and everyone else, always engages in rendering service to the lotus feet of the Lord. The conclusion is that no one can be the benefactor of the Lord, everyone is actually the servant of the servant of the Lord. Rajottam Das ends the Bhaktivedanta purport.

### Chanting, Efficiency, Devotional Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zibveUdVoPA?t=1775
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All such other activities we have to efficiently do in minimum possible time."
**Summary:** The speaker advises that at least 16 rounds should be chanted attentively, and this chanting and hearing should be increased, filling all available time. He suggests becoming efficient in other activities to minimize time spent on them, saving time for devotional service.

16 rounds at least we should nicely chant and hear attentively. Then after that we have to increase this chanting and hearing attentively. We have to increase and fill all available time with such chanting and hearing attentively. All available time. So for which we have to become efficient in doing other activities which are not necessarily direct devotional service. All such other activities we have to efficiently do in minimum possible time. Generally, people think they should do important activities or some activities efficiently means maximum time they should spend on that. But here it is a reverse. Bodily maintenance. Minimum time. It doesn't mean we become dirty and we don't care for cleansing the body or feeding the body. No. We have to. We cannot neglect that. But efficiently we should do in such a way that minimum time is spent. So that we save time for increasing chanting, hearing, remembering the Lord's holy name, qualities, pastimes, etc. That is the idea.

## Srimad Bhagavatam Lecture 2.9.32 by HG Stoka Krishna Prabhu
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### personal vs impersonal, qualities of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JkTiablKaLk?t=384
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So one of the attributes of the personal feature is the transcendental qualities of the Lord."
**Summary:** The lecture begins by discussing the personal feature of the Absolute Truth and its transcendental qualities, contrasting it with the impersonalists' view.

So these are words spoken by Vishnu, Prabodha Kishayi Vishnu. They have spoken to Brahma and the Lord is telling Brahma that Brahma can get fractured. This is the realization of the personal feature of the Absolute Truth, the Personal Attribute of God. So one of the attributes of the personal feature is the transcendental qualities of the Lord. The impersonalists, when they hear about the word quality, bhuna, they immediately think or consider God is nirguna.

---
### nirguna, material qualities, personal qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JkTiablKaLk?t=443
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So these are all personal qualities, which are not to be found in any material object, in any dead thing, material thing."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the meaning of 'nirguna' as 'without material qualities' and differentiates between material and personal qualities, highlighting the impermanence of material qualities.

So nirguna literally means without any qualities. But the devotees understand that nirguna means without any material qualities. Our experience in this world is that there are so many different material qualities. Every object, material object has got quality. Every person has quality. Now the objects have qualities based on the material they are made of. This table, if it is a wooden table, it has got qualities of wood. If there is a metal table, it will have qualities of metal. So that we can understand, these are all material qualities based on the material of which the object is made. Now what about a person of this world? What are his qualities like? So we can understand there are qualities of a person, which are different than the qualities of different material objects, when we consider a person. So person means he has feelings. He has got affection. He has got anger. He has got some compassion. So these are all personal qualities, which are not to be found in any material object, in any dead thing, material thing. So these are qualities or attributes of a person.

---
### impermanence, acquired nature, material qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JkTiablKaLk?t=571
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, the qualities a person displays, or appears to have, are not spiritual qualities. They are all material qualities."
**Summary:** The discussion continues on the impermanence of personal qualities and how they are acquired due to association with material nature, emphasizing that these are not spiritual qualities.

But our experience in this world also is that, the personal qualities are not permanent qualities. A person's qualities keep changing. Just like a child may display some playful behaviour, or playful qualities. The same child grows up, and becomes a mature person. Then the playful qualities are not there. So we see these qualities of a person can change. So even though we understand, person's qualities are different than the qualities of this material objects. Still, because the qualities are changing, we do not attribute those qualities to the soul, to the eternal soul within the body, to the real person. And Bhagavad Gita also describes, every person who takes birth in this world, has got what is called as an acquired nature. At the beginning of creation, every person has got, a certain acquired quality, due to association, with the gunas, with material nature. Everybody takes birth, according to the association with the gunas, and accordingly they have different qualities. So, the qualities a person displays, or appears to have, are not spiritual qualities. They are all material qualities. Even though they are not like the qualities of matter, still, they are material qualities. Which means to say, that if the absolute truth is without material qualities, nirpuna, then the absolute truth will not have any of the qualities that we have, or we display.

---
### anger, material qualities, impersonalism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JkTiablKaLk?t=739
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Sometimes, even God becomes angry, when He comes in the, material form."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of anger as a material quality and how impersonalists view the incarnations of God, like Narasimha, as accepting material forms and qualities.

So if we display anger, then the scriptures also say, you should control anger. Anger is not good for you. Anger may lead to so many, dangerous things. Just like, somebody becomes very angry, then, they lose their discrimination. They lose their intelligence. One becomes bewildered, and one's intelligence is lost, and memory is completely bewildered. So, anger should be controlled. Anger is not a good quality. One should conquer anger. So naturally, the conclusion will be, this anger is actually, a material quality, which is not a desirable quality. And certainly we don't expect, that the absolute truth will have anger. Because the absolute truth is nirpuna, without any material qualities. So then, how do we understand, that the Supreme Lord, appears in His form as Narasimha, and He displays anger? So, the impersonalists will say, oh, that, incarnation of Narasimha, is a material incarnation. A material form. Material form, naturally, there will be, anger. And that anger has to be controlled. And that is why, the impersonalists will say, oh, Bhagavatam is described, the Lord was very angry, Narasimha was very angry, then Prahlad Maharaj went and pacified the Lord, and then the Lord became, free from anger, and then very lovingly, He placed His lotus hand on Prahlad, and blessed him. Sometimes, even God becomes angry, when He comes in the, material form. And then, His anger has to, come down, just like, a human being of this world, he becomes angry, then he has to pacify himself, or somebody has to pacify him, he has to give up his anger. Like that, God also.

---
### impersonalism, compassion, transcendental qualities
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/JkTiablKaLk?t=882
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But, the Bhagavatam describes, that absolute truth, is a transcendental person, and absolute truth has got, transcendental qualities."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain the impersonalists' view that the absolute truth is formless and without qualities, contrasting it with the Bhagavatam's description of the absolute truth as a transcendental person with transcendental qualities.

So, they say, that absolute truth, actually doesn't have any form. But when the absolute truth, incarnates, then, they may, the absolute truth may accept some, material form. And that material form, may display sometimes anger, sometimes display compassion, even compassion. Compassion also, the impersonalists think, if a person has compassion, that compassion sometimes we see, is misplaced. Just like a mother is very, very compassionate with her child. But we also know, when the mother, does not, strictly, correct the child, and the child grows up, with some, pampering by the mother, then everybody will say, mother has spoiled the child. So, she, should not have shown compassion, to the child, when it was, not, properly, placed, improperly, placed compassion, unto compassion. So, even this compassion, this materialistic, this impersonalist will say, that's also a material quality. That quality also, should be absent in the law, or in the absolute truth, it should not be there. This is material quality. So, impersonalists will argue, that all these qualities, we see in a person, of this world, are all material qualities. So, there is no question of, absolute truth having compassion, absolute truth having, anger, absolute truth having any personal qualities. So, it goes on to, prove their point, apparently, that absolute truth is impersonal, does not have personality. Because, person means, having some material qualities. But, the Bhagavatam describes, that absolute truth, is a transcendental person, and absolute truth has got, transcendental qualities.

That which you see now is also I. Brahma, it is I, the personality of Godhead. And after annihilation, what remains will also be I, the personality of Godhead. ### impersonalism, creation, Vedic literature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=72
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the difference between the 'I' of Brahma and the 'I' of the Supreme God, refuting the impersonalist's view of oneness and referencing the Kadopanishad.

So every one time we will discuss that in the afternoon and speak of it in the afternoon. Thank you very much. Parvoh, we should note very carefully that the personality of Godhead is addressing Lord Brahma and specifying with great emphasis himself, pointing out that it is he, the personality of Godhead, who existed before the creation. It is he only who maintains the creation and it is he only who remains after annihilation of the creation. Brahma is also a creation. Brahma is the creation of the Supreme God. The impersonalist puts forth a theory of oneness in the sense that Brahma, also being the same principle of I, because he is an emanation from the I, the Absolute Truth, is identical with the Lord, the principle of I. And that is, does nothing more than the principle of I as explained in these words. Accepting the argument of the impersonalist, it is to be admitted that the Lord is the Creator I and that the Brahma is the Created I. Therefore, there is a difference between the two Is, namely the Predominator I and the Predominated I. Therefore, there are still two Is. Even accepting the argument of the impersonalist, the impersonalist, but we must note carefully that these two Is are accepted in the Vedic literature, the Kadopanishad, in the sense of quality. The Kadopanishad says, nithyo nityanam cetanas cetavanam ego vahunam yog vidhati karmanam. I will stop reading the book at this point and we will discuss something else.

### questions, answers, Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=254
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So the answers will make a lot of sense if you know the questions."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the four questions Brahma asked Lord Vishnu and explains that these questions are systematically answered in the four verses of the Yatushloki Bhagavatam.

Lord Vishnu is answering Brahma. Brahma has asked four questions. What are the four questions Brahma has answered? Anathapuram Chitência, King of Pandavas and King of Pandavas. Anathapuram Chitância, King of Pandavas and King of Pandavas and King of Pandavas. The first question we are asked is, what are the forms of the Lord in matter and in transience? Rupa. Form. What are the forms of the Lord in matter and in transience? Second question is, how are the different energies of the Lord working? So remember these four questions because that will give a lot of clarity as to what is it that Vishnu is trying to answer. He is trying to answer specific questions asked by Brahma. So the answers will make a lot of sense if you know the questions. So the first question is, what are the forms of the Lord in matter and in transience? Second is, how are the energies of the Lord working? Third is, how does the Lord play with these different energies? You see in the interaction of the energies, there is a certain control exercised by somebody or something above and beyond these energies. So Brahma's question is, how does the Lord play with these energies? And fourth question of Brahma is, how may Brahma be instructed? Discharge the duty and respect between the two. How should be the duty given? A certain duty that is given to Brahma. And how should it be discharged? So these four questions will be systematically answered in the four verses called the Yatushloki Bhagavatam.

---
### Bhagavatam, realization, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Simply if you hear Bhagavatam, you realize the Absolute Truth."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the entire Bhagavatam can be summarized in four verses, and that Lord Vishnu spoke the essence of the Bhagavatam to Brahma in these verses, emphasizing the importance of hearing for realization.

According to the Acharyas, the entire Bhagavatam can be summarized in four verses. In four verses. Now those four verses are the verses beginning with this present verse. Second canto, ninth chapter, thirty-third, thirty-fourth, thirty-fifth and thirty-sixth verses. Four verses, four Yatushloki Bhagavatam. And it is said by the Acharyas that Lord Vishnu spoke the Bhagavatam to Brahma in these four verses. The essence of the Bhagavatam was spoken by Vishnu to Brahma in these four verses. Specifically answered in Brahma's four verses. So the answer begins with Lord Vishnu telling Brahma that all of me, my transcendental existence, my form, my colors, my qualities, my activities, everything let it be awakened in you by factual realization of my causeless mercy. That was the thirty-second verse. And in the thirty-third verse that realization is being given by the Lord's grace. This is the philosophy of the Bhagavatam. Simply if you hear Bhagavatam, you realize the Absolute Truth. Especially Bhagavatam's presence, the Absolute Truth as the personality of God. So you realize the highest aspect of the Absolute Truth simply by hearing. Just like Brahma realized simply by hearing from Vishnu.

### hearing, source, false ego, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=492
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our false ego is the filter that is actually coming in the way of our proper hearing."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of hearing properly from the right source for realization, highlighting the role of false ego as a filter and the need for purification through Sadhana Bhakti.

The important thing is that there is a certain way of hearing and also the hearer has to select the proper source. So two things for realization. One is you hear properly. And second is you hear from the right source. So Brahma had prepared himself to hear by performing Tapasya. That way Brahma prepared himself. Secondly Brahma was hearing from, directly from the personality of God, Vishnu himself. So we should also prepare ourselves to hear the Bhagavatam by undergoing the process of Sadhana Bhakti. What does Sadhana Bhakti do? It purifies our consciousness of all the misconceptions we may have. When we hear, we hear from a filter. What is that filter? What is the filter that comes between our hearing and what is actually being discovered in our heart? Huh? False ego. Our false ego is the filter that is actually coming in the way of our proper hearing. False ego is a set of misconceptions that we have about the Self, about God, about this world, about anything. So these misconceptions in the form of false ego have to be cleared. So when you practice Sadhana Bhakti and practice Yoga Yuktena, then your false ego is cleared.

### hearing, purification, action, reaction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=629
**Verse References:** SB 1.2.6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All doubts about who you are, who is God, who is your relationship, what is this world, everything is cleared."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the process of purification through hearing the Bhagavatam, which cuts the knot of material existence, terminates the chain of action and reaction, and clears all doubts.

In the beginning of the Bhagavatam this process is described. The process begins with hearing. And this hearing process, if it is properly applied, it results in purification of the consciousness to the extent that this knot of material existence, ahankara, is cut to pieces. All doubts about who you are, who is God, who is your relationship, what is this world, everything is cleared. Chid yate sarvasam shayah. Chi yate jah si karvani. Then the chain of rooting action is completely terminated. We are caught up in this chain of action and reaction. We have performed some actions in the past of which we are experiencing reactions. These reactions prompt us to do further actions. And that gives rise to further reactions. So we have got up in this network of actions and reactions. So to get us out of this network, Bhagavatam says, srimad-am svaratham krsnama. Hear about Krishna from the Bhagavatam. So this sadhana bhakti process is the preparation for hearing for the sake of and the souls, what are the souls? The Lord or the Lord's bona fide representation. So we have to select carefully the souls from which we hear. So in this case we are hearing from Prabhupada who is coming in parampara. From Krishna. So when we hear from Prabhupada then we can actually realize. So here Krishna is explaining to Brahman.

### creation, Godhead, material nature

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He exists beyond cause and effect."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Vishnu existed before creation, even before material nature, which is the cause of creation, emphasizing that He exists beyond cause and effect.

He is telling one fundamental truth that it is the person in Godhead, Vishnu himself who was existing before the creation when there was nothing but himself. So before the creation there was no material world. There were no created beings. Only the Lord existed. The personality of Krishna. He existed. Nor was there the material nature the cause of this creation. The material nature was nothing. The material nature was not there. Sad asat param. Sad in this case refers to the effect. Effect is what we see around us. The material world. It is an effect of the cause. Cause is asat. What is that? Material nature. This is the difficulty in understanding this world. That the cause and effect both are material nature itself. So how can one understand that the cause and effect are the same? Very difficult to understand. Actually even though the cause of this material creation is material nature still material nature is not independently the cause for this creation. That we explain in detail. Anyway. So here Vishnu is telling Sad asat param. He existed before the creation. He also existed when there was no cause of the creation. Even material nature which is the cause was not existing. Sad asat param. Cause and effect beyond cause and effect. He exists beyond cause and effect.

### creation, eternality, impersonalism

That which you see now is also I the personality of God. Now the creation is already come into existence and Vishnu is very much there. He was existing before the creation. What happens to him during the existence of the creation? He remains. He does not disappear. He does not transform. He does not become affected. He does not change. So he remains even after the creation has taken place. And after annihilation the whole creation will disappear. After that what remains will also be I the personality of God. Vishnu only will remain after the creation of this world. So in particular Prabhupada is explaining one point in this first paragraph that we understand that what he is saying carefully. That in person they say with respect to these words they are arguing this that Brahma is an emanation is coming out of Vishnu. Right? Brahma is gone from Vishnu and again into the creation. So that Brahma is the same principle of I, Aham. Here the word used is Aham evaasam evaadhe paschal aham naanyat yatsarasat param paschal aham so vashi shreka so asmi aham. So this word Aham is emphasized I Vishnu is speaking and he is saying I I exist in this world I am present now I will remain after the destruction So the mayavadi argument is that the I is the same I which is the absolute truth which exists before the creation which remains during the creation and which remains after the whole creation and destroyed Brahma is also that same identical I the same I.

### creation, creator, Vedas, Kathopanishad

**Summary:** The speaker explains that even if the mayavadi argument is accepted, Brahma is a created 'I' while Vishnu is the creator, emphasizing the difference between the predominator and the predominated, referencing the Kathopanishad to illustrate the similarity and difference between Brahma and Vishnu.

So our Acharyas explain Yes, even if he accepts the mayavadi argument Brahma is also the same I because an emanation from that original I original Vishnu Brahma is emanation Brahma is coming from Vishnu So even assuming that the mayavadi argument that Brahma is an emanation therefore the same I we have to note that Brahma is a created I a created entity and Vishnu is the creator So there are two distinct I's here the creator and the creator itself the creator and the created So that is explained here as the difference between the two I's is the predominator and the predominated Brahma is subordinate Vishnu is the master Brahma is the subordinate created Vishnu is the creator Vishnu is the controller Brahma is the controlled This difference exists and therefore we cannot accept that both are identical in all respects 100%. No Brahma and Vishnu are not identical in all respects So in the Vedas it is said in the Kathopanishad the similarity between the creation and the creator is described as Nithyo Nithyana Between Brahma and Vishnu the similarity is Nithyo Nithyana Both are Nithya Both are Chetanas Chetanas Chetanana But the difference is there are two categories of Nithya and two categories of Chetana Nithya means eternal Chetana means conscious Two categories of eternals and two categories of conscious beings What is the difference? The next line says Eko bahunam yodhya dhyanti kamaan The one singular eternal conscious being is the maintainer of the many Conscious eternal means Eko bahunam Eka the one Nithya Chetana Distinction is made very clearly One and many Singular and plural Nithyo Nithyana Chetanas Chetanana Eko bahunam Very clear Singular and plural So plural are the many living entities and singular is Vishnu Singular is Vishnu So Vishnu is the maintainer of all the created beings the many living entities So Vishnu is the maintainer All others are maintained Even though both are eternal and conscious beings So the difference is clearly brought out in the domination.

### renunciation, simplicity, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=1425
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He is totally disinterested in any sense gratification."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the extreme simplicity and renunciation of Jagannath Das Babaji Maharaj, highlighting his disinterest in sense gratification and his simple way of life.

Namok Daurati Shodaya Sakshat Vairagya Murtaye. He displayed renunciation of the highest order. He is personification of renunciation. Vairagya Murti. That means if you want to know, What is Vairagya? Then you can see in the personality of Daurati Shodaya Sakshat Vairagya Murti. He is totally disinterested in any sense gratification. He used to live a very very simple life. It is said that for maintaining his body he would beg some grains from the dham vases. And he would simply offer it by holding it in his hand. Very very simple process of offering. And for covering his cloth and for cooking his grains he would use some dry wood pieces lying here and there in the forest or on the road. And he would take pots which were discarded. Urban pots which were discarded after being used for some function or ceremony with them pulling legs. Clean it up with river water and cook those little grains and take them. For covering his body he would go to the cremation place. Burminaat. And the cloth that was used to cover the dead body. When they bring the dead body for burning they cover it with some white cloth. And before burning they remove the white cloth and discard it. They throw it away. So that cloth he would take and cover himself. So basically for maintaining his body. Body necessities.

### devotion, bhajana, knowledge, simplicity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/N4em7bSs-OE?t=1570
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Still all the conclusions of the Shastras, of the Krishna Consciousness philosophy, was always illuminated in his heart."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing Jagannath Das Babaji Maharaj, emphasizing his absorption in hari bhajana, his carrying of books by Harathamdas Thakur, and how, despite being illiterate, he possessed deep philosophical understanding.

He would do it without any difficulty. Without any elaborate arrangements. Very very very simple way. He was always absorbed in hari bhajana. He is called bhajananandi. Bhajananandi means. Somebody who can completely absorb themselves 24 hours a day in meditation on the Supreme Lord Krishna. Through performing bhajana. Through performing kirtan or japa. Or through hearing about the glories. Or through singing the glories of the Lord. In this way. Always absorbed in that. Now Gaurav Vishwanath Babaji Maharaj was ill-trained. He did not know how to read and write. That means he did not undergo formal education. But he would always carry with him two books. Written by Harathamdas Thakur. Prema Bhakti Chandrika and Prarthana. These are two books written by Harathamdas Thakur. It is a collection of songs written by Harathamdas Thakur. Now in these two books. The entire philosophy of Krishna Consciousness is very nicely explained. Harathamdas Thakur has written a simple bengali. He has written a lot of songs. And in these songs he explains the philosophy of Krishna Consciousness. Which is there in Sanskrit. In so many Vedas, Upanishads, Bhagavatam, Bhagavad Gita. All that philosophy. He has explained in very simple bengali language through these songs. So Gaurav Vishwanath Babaji Maharaj would always be carried with him these two books. Apart from these two books. He had on his body one Nandi Mala made of Tulsi. And another Japa Mala made of Tulsi beads. And the cloth he was using to wrap his body. That's all he was carrying with him. So he was very very renowned. Even though he was not literate. Still all the conclusions of the Shastras, of the Krishna Consciousness philosophy, was always illuminated in his heart. When somebody would approach him. And ask some philosophical doubt. He would give the answer with such clarity. All the conclusions he made. So he used to guide those who used to approach him. Asking for philosophical clarity. On the Gauria Vaishnava. On the Vaishnava Siddhanta.

**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He would not so much be interested in giving such benediction for material advancement."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how Gaurav Vishwanath Babaji Maharaj avoided materialistic people seeking blessings and how Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur sought diksha from God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj.

And sometimes when some materialistic people would try to get his blessings for some material benefit. He would not so much be interested. In giving such benediction for material advancement. So he would go away from clean places. And he would sit next to a toilet. Or a drink. That drink means specifically where somebody passes through. It could be very smelly. It could be dirty. It could be bad. So he would sit next to that place and chant. So that nobody would disturb him. So he did not so much say. So he did not so much like. People coming to him for some material benefit. And he never liked to accept service from other devotees. Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur. When he wanted to take Diksha. His father came to know Thakur. He approached his father and said I want Diksha. So his father directed him to God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj. He said go to God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj and take Diksha. So accordingly Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur came to God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj and requested. Please give me Diksha. So God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj. In the beginning. He would not respond to this request. So but repeatedly. Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur approached God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj. Then one day. God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj said. I will ask my Lord. And. Depending on. His reply. I shall tell you. Whether I can initiate you or not. So after telling this. Again. Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur was waiting. For a reply. So one day he asked. What did your Lord say? So then. God Kishodad Babaji Maharaj said. That. He would not be interested. In giving Diksha. He would not accept service. From anyone. So if he has to give Diksha. He has to accept service from the disciple. So since he did not want to accept service from anybody. He wanted to be only engaged in serving. Not in accepting service. Therefore he did not want to give Diksha.

And Shrila Bhaktisiddhanta Saraswati Thakur describes. That after some years in Vrindavan. Lord Kishodad Babaji Maharaj came to Navadvipa. In Navadvipa also he travelled to all the places. Where Chaitanya Mahaprabhu performed the pastimes. And he would reside under different trees. Realising the non-difference between. Navadvipa Dham and Vrindavan Dham. Vrindavan Dham is the place where Krishna performed pastimes. And a replica of Vrindavan Dham is Navadvipa Dham. Where Chaitanya Mahaprabhu performed the pastimes. So he visited different places in Navadvipa Dham. Lord Kishodad Babaji Maharaj. And he would stay under different trees. Realising the non-difference between. Navadvipa Dham and Vrindavan Dham. He would also come. To hear.

The discourses on Bhagavatam. Which was being given by Bhaktivinoda Thakur. In a place called Svananda Sukhara Purja. Bhaktivinoda Thakur built this place. In one sacred spot. In Navadvipa Dham. And he would regularly give discourses on Bhagavatam. And. Many great devotees would come and hear. Among the many great devotees would come and hear. Bhaktivinoda Thakur's discourses on Bhagavatam. One of them was Lord Kishodad Babaji Maharaj. He would sit and hear. The discourses of Bhaktivinoda Thakur. And. He would. Also. Not be. So much. Careful about. Regularly. Taking prasadam. Or regularly. Taking rest. Most of the time. He would be awake. He would be either chanting. Or singing. Or he would be. Studying. This. Two books that he would always carry. Pratma and Prema. The collection of songs. So. People used to wonder. That he actually. Is illiterate. So how does he. Read. How does he sing. These songs. How does he know the philosophy. It is because of his. So this is particularly. Applicable for. Guru Ekshitarayo Bhajan. Krishna. Who. Seated as. Paratma. In the heart. Of the devotee. He enlightens. The devotee. From within. If the devotee. Is engaged. devotional service to the Lord. He is constantly engaged with love and devotion in serving the Lord. Then the Lord enlightens the devotee from within. There is no need for the devotee to study externally anything. So, Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj was enlightened from within by the Lord. So, that was his position. He was fully enlightened. And he was fully renounced. So, it is said in the Bhagavatam that these two factors are very important for self-realization. What is that? Jnana and Vairantya. Jnana is enlightenment and Vairantya is renunciation. Both of them were seen in the personality of Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj to the highest degree. And therefore, many, many great saintly persons actually took shelter of Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj by actually enquiring from Gaurav Krishna. By getting clarification and so many doubts. Also, Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj could easily recognize an imposter devotee. Sometimes people pose as if they are great devotees. Though within their heart, they are not purified of material meditation. They pose externally as if they are renounced. But actually in their heart, they have so open desire for enjoyment daily. So, when such imposters would approach Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj, he would immediately expose them. So, it was well known in the holy dham that any imposter will get exposed if he goes to Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj. So, nobody could cheat him. Nobody could display false renunciation in front of Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj. So, he was very, very, very advanced Paramahansa. But he led a very, very simple life. He was renunciation personified. And today is the disappearance day, anniversary of his divine birth. So, we pray to him that by his mercy, we may develop renunciation and we may get enlightenment in Krishna consciousness.

It's called Vipralambara Sampode. Krishna is served by those who are advanced devotees, who have developed their relationship with Krishna. They serve Krishna in two ways. One is in association with Krishna they serve Krishna. And other one is in separation from Krishna they serve Krishna. Just like Krishna when he appeared in Vrindavan. So, when he was, as long as he was in Vrindavan, the Prajavasis were serving him in association. And when he left Vrindavan and went to Mathura and Dwarka, he was away from Vrindavan. All the Prajavasis were serving Krishna in separation from Krishna. So, these are two ways of serving the Lord. So, Gaurav Krishna and Bhavani Maharaj was always serving Krishna. In separation from him. Vipralambara Sampode. So, there is a certain mellow taste to be experienced by a devotee in such service. Vipralambara Sampode. So, he is the expert Acharya who served Krishna in Vipralambara Sampode. ## Srimad bhagavatam 2.9.33 Lecture-2 by HG Stoka Krishna Prabhu
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Bhagavatam essence, Chatur Shloki, Philosophy of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=475
**Verse References:** SB 2.9.33

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the importance of the four verses (Chatur Shloki) from the 2nd Canto, 9th chapter, verses 33-36, stating they contain the essence of the Bhagavatam's philosophy of God and will be the focus of upcoming classes.

The 18,000 verses, Bhagavatam has 18,000 verses. Padhunetta aayira, correct? In Tamil you say that. Like naala aayira devya prabandham, padhunetta aayira Bhagavatam. That has been summarized in 4 verses. Just 4 verses. So these 4 verses are very, very, very, very, very important. You can understand the whole of Bhagavatam just by studying these 4 verses. So for these verses, Srila Prabhupada has written very, very elaborate purports. And these 4 verses bring out the essence of the philosophy of the Bhagavatam. Bhagavatam is presenting the philosophy of God. So that philosophy has been very nicely explained in these 4 verses. So which are those 4 verses? They are the verses beginning with the 2nd canto 9th chapter 33rd verse up to 36th verse. 33, 34, 35 and 36. So these 4 verses will spend more time than on the other verses. We have already begun this verse in the last class. So I will continue with this same verse, this class. And probably it will go on for few more classes.

### Creator, Vedas, Philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=949
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The predominator I is the feeder of the predominated I's and thus there is a vast difference between the two principles of I."
**Summary:** The speaker reads a paragraph from Srila Prabhupada's purport, explaining the difference between the creator and the created, emphasizing the vast difference between the predominator 'I' and the predominated 'I's as described in the Vedas.

Are both accepted in the Vedas as qualitatively one because both of them are Nityas and Chetanas but the singular I is the creator I and the created I's are of plural number because there are many I's like Brahma and those generated by Brahma it is the simple truth the father creates or begets a son and the son also creates many other sons and all of them may be one as human beings but at the same time from the father the son and the grandsons are all different the son cannot take the place of the father nor can the grandson simultaneously the father the son and the grandson are one and different also as human beings they are one but as relativities they are different therefore the relativities of the creator and the created or the predominator and the predominated have been differentiated in the Vedas by saying that the predominator I is the feeder of the predominated I's and thus there is a vast difference between the two principles of I. So this is just one paragraph among many paragraphs in this purport. I'll stop here reading the purport though the purport is not over.

### Vedic Literature, Vyasa Deva, Oral Tradition
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1113
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Understanding, learning, understanding and realizing through hearing."
**Summary:** The speaker provides background information on the Srimad Bhagavatam as the last work of Vyasa Deva, who compiled the Vedic literature, explaining that before Vyasa Deva, the Vedas existed only in oral tradition (Shruti), where disciples memorized and understood the teachings through hearing from the Guru.

Now a certain background for this verse is that the Srimad Bhagavatam is the last work of Vyasa Deva. Vyasa Deva compiled the Vedic literature as we have it today. Because before Vyasa Deva compiled the Vedic literature, what was available was only in the form of Shruti, the Vedas were available as that which was heard and passed on through oral tradition. Oral tradition means the Guru will speak, the disciple will hear and as soon as the disciple hears, he will commit to memory. Just he hears once and he commits to memory and he is able to remember the rest of the verse. And after hearing from the Guru and committing to memory, the disciple will try to understand the meaning of what is heard from the Guru and then he will deliberate on the meaning and ask questions from the Guru and try to understand and realize this subject matter of the Vedas. This is called Shrutapantha. Understanding, learning, understanding and realizing through hearing.

---
### Kali Yuga, Vedic Knowledge, Lifespan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1226
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In Kali Yuga, people's duration of life is shortened."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Vyasa Deva appeared at the beginning of Kali Yuga because he foresaw that people's memory and lifespan would be reduced, making it difficult to study the Vedas, noting the decreasing lifespans in each Yuga from Sakya Yuga to Kali Yuga.

Now in the beginning of Kali Yuga, Vyasa Deva appeared for a very specific reason. He could see that in Kali Yuga, people's memory will be very short-lived. Their lifespan also will be reduced. In Kali Yuga, people's duration of life is shortened. So the Bhagavatam explains, In Sakya Yuga, the duration of life was 100,000 years, 1 lakh years. And that duration of life reduced in Treta Yuga, the next Yuga, to 10,000 years. Further reduced in the next Yuga, called Dwapar Yuga, to 1,000 years. And in Kali Yuga, the last of the four Yugas, in the cycle of four Yugas, the last Yuga, Kali Yuga, it is further reduced to 100 years. So the duration of life being reduced, the time available for studying the Vedas is also very less. Not only that, people's memory has also become less. In Kali Yuga, duration of life, memory and so many other such good qualities are reduced.

---
### Vedas, Vyasa Deva, Vedic Division
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1343
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He divided the one Veda into four."
**Summary:** The speaker details Vyasa Deva's actions to make Vedic knowledge accessible in Kali Yuga, including putting the Vedas in writing and dividing the single Veda into four parts: Rig Veda, Yajur Veda, Sama Veda, and Atharva Veda.

So Vyasa Deva foreseeing this condition of human beings in Kali Yuga, he first of all decided to put the Vedas in writing. So Vedas were put into writing at the end of Dwapar Yuga, just before the beginning of Kali Yuga, by Vyasa Deva. The name Vyasa is actually a title. It is a title which indicates that one who records, one who puts down in writing and creates a record, Vyasa is a title. Actually, the Vyasa title is given to the incarnation of the Lord who appeared at the end of Dwapar Yuga, Krishna Dwaipaayana Vyasa. His full name is Krishna Dwaipaayana Vyasa. He is an incarnation of the Supreme Lord who has appeared for a very special purpose. The first of the reasons is that he put down the Vedas in writing. He put down the Vedas in writing. Then also, he saw that the Vedas are very, very vast. Even if, the written Vedas are available for people, still he decided to divide the one Veda into four. What did he do? He divided the one Veda into four. And that is how we have the four divisions of the Vedas called Rig Veda, Yajur Veda, Sama Veda, and Atharva Veda. Why did he do this division?

---
### Upanishads, Vedanta Sutra, Vedic Essence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The summary of the summary. The cream of the cream."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Vyasa Deva's efforts to condense Vedic knowledge, describing how he summarized the four Vedas into Upanishads and further condensed the Upanishads into the Vedanta Sutra, which is described as the 'cream of the cream'.

That he saw that in the Vedas, there are different portions which describe different things. So he logically divided the one Veda into four. Thereby, people can take up only the portion that is relevant for them and learn and study only that portion. So Rig Veda, Yajur Veda, Sama Veda, and Atharva Veda, he divided the one Veda into four. But even that, one fourth of the entire Vedas that he divided was also very, very big. It was not small. So he further summarized each of those four divisions of Vedas into what is called as Upanishads. Vedas contain many different details called Brahmana, Aranyaka, Samhita, and Upanishads. All these different sections of the Vedas, for each of the Vedas, Rig Veda has its own Brahmana, Aranyaka, Samhita, and Upanishads. Yajur Veda has its own Brahmana, Aranyaka, Samhita, and Upanishads. Similarly, Sama Veda and Atharva Veda. So the Upanishad portion of each of the Vedas contains the essence of the philosophy of the Vedic knowledge. The Upanishad portion contains the philosophy. And it is also in the form of the essence of the philosophy. So the Upanishads also are many. There are 108 principal Upanishads. How many? 108 important or principal Upanishads. So Upanishads also are very, very big in number. So even those 108 Upanishads, in Kali Yuga, people will not be able to study. 108 Upanishads. Very big number. So what did Vyasa Deva do? He further summarized all the Upanishads into what is called as Vedanta Sutra. The summary of the summary. The cream of the cream. Just like you boil milk, you get cream. But that cream, if you further condense, cream of the cream, further you condense, you get the real, real, real essence. That cream of the cream is called Vedanta Sutra. Upanishads are also called Vedanta.

---
### Vedanta Sutra, Itihasas, Puranas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1669
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vyasa Deva could also see that this summarization will not be understood properly by majority of the people."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Vedanta, meaning the conclusion or essence of the Vedas, is still too vast, so Vyasa Deva condensed it into the Vedanta Sutra, a collection of short aphorisms. However, foreseeing that even this would be difficult for most people to understand, he compiled the Itihasas and Puranas to explain the Vedic message in an accessible way.

Vedanta means the conclusion of the Vedas, of the essence of the Vedas. Now essence of the Vedas as Upanishads or Vedanta is very big. Even though it is distant and made small, but still that is also not sufficiently summarized. That's what Vyasa Deva could foresee. So he further condensed the Vedanta or Upanishads into Vedanta Sutra. And that Vedanta Sutra is just some thousand short sutras, aphorisms. Sutra means like a formula. These aphorisms are very, very short and very terse. Athako brahma jinyasa is one Vedanta Sutra. Like this there are 1055 short sutras. That's what entire Vedic knowledge is summarized into this Vedanta Sutra. It's one small book. That's all. The whole Vedas, entirety of Vedas in one small book Vyasa Deva has summarized. This is the genius of Vyasa Deva. Very, very, very brave work. Now in summarizing the entire Vedas into this Vedanta Sutra, Vyasa Deva could also see that this summarization will not be understood properly by majority of the people. Most people cannot understand because it's very much condensed, very terse, very difficult to understand. So what he did, he first of all compiled the itihasas and puranas in order to explain the Vedic message in an easily understandable way for the common people.

---
### Itihasas, Historical Narrations, Vedic Message
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1811
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "These itihasas, even though they are presented as stories, they are not fictitious, imaginary, concocted stories. No. They are real stories, real incidents, historical incidents."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Itihasas as historical narrations containing important historical incidents that explain the Vedic message, citing the Ramayana and Mahabharata as examples, and emphasizing that these stories are real historical events, not imaginary tales.

So itihasas and puranas. Itihasas are historical narrations which contain some important historical incidents. But which explain through the historical incidents the Vedic message. For example, the Ramayana, the Mahabharata. These are two important itihasas. Ramayana explains or describes the life of Lord Rama. Mahabharata explains the life of the Kauravas and the Pandavas. So these two itihasas are explaining the Vedic message in the form, but presented in the form of a nice story, historical narration. Now this is story, the story of Lord Rama, the story of the Pandavas. Now these stories are not imaginary, fictitious stories. Just like you have Jataka tales, Aesop's fables, fairy tales. These are not like that. These itihasas, even though they are presented as stories, they are not fictitious, imaginary, concocted stories. No. They are real stories, real incidents, historical incidents.

---
### Puranas, Ancient History, Vedic Philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=1942
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Puranas also contain stories, historical narrations, but these stories are real. They are again not fabricated stories."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates Puranas from Itihasas, explaining that Puranas are very ancient historical narrations that go back to the beginning of creation or even before, containing real historical incidents and discussing definite topics concerning the philosophy of the Vedas.

Now in addition to the itihasas, there are other historical narrations called the Puranas. Now Puranas are very, very, very, very ancient historical narrations. In the itihasas, it's a definite narration of a particular set of incidents in the history. There's a time of Lord Rama, Treta Yuga, Rama appeared. So that life of Lord Rama is described in the Ramayana. Or the Pandavas and Kauravas, they were living in the Dvapara Yuga, before this Kali Yuga began. So that narration is concerning that period of time, Pandavas and Kauravas. But the Puranas are little different than the itihasas. What do the Puranas contain? The Puranas contain very, very, very ancient historical narrations that go back even to the beginning of creation, or even before the creation. And Puranas also contain stories, historical narrations, but these stories are real. They are again not fabricated stories. They are real incidents, historical incidents. But they could be very, very, very, very old. And one more characteristic of the Puranas is that in the Puranas there are definite topics which are described concerning the philosophy of the Vedas.

---
### Bhagavata Purana, Vedic Literature, Welfare
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2055
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Rishi or a Sadhu his life is dedicated for the welfare of the people in general."
**Summary:** The speaker provides examples of topics discussed in the Puranas, such as creation, planetary systems, and the science of God, highlighting the Bhagavata Purana's ten topics and Upapuranas' five topics, and emphasizing that Itihasas and Puranas are accepted as Vedic literature, compiled by Vyasadeva to explain the Vedic message in an easily understandable way. He then transitions to discussing Vyasadeva's dissatisfaction despite his extensive work.

Just like Bhagavata Purana, it has ten topics. It describes the creation, the secondary creation, the planetary systems, the science of God, the maintenance of this material world, the destruction. All these topics are discussed systematically in the Bhagavata Purana. There are other categories of Puranas in which there are only five topics discussed. They are called Upapuranas. So there are Puranas which discuss, which describe ten topics and the Upapuranas describe five topics. So Puranas are in this sense different from the Itihasis. Puranas are different in what sense? That they are very ancient, historical narrations may be there in the Puranas and they contain definite topics, either ten or five. So these Itihasis and Puranas are also accepted as Vedic literature though there are some transcendentalists, some Vedic scholars, don't accept the Itihasis and Puranas as Vedic literature. But actually because they are presenting the same Vedic message in the form of historical narrations they are also accepted as Vedas. So Vyasadeva particularly compiled the Itihasis and Puranas for the sake of explaining the Vedic message in an easily understandable way for the common people. So if you study the life of the Pandavas or study the life of Lord Rama or study the Puranas which describe different historical incidents Vishnu Purana, Garuda Purana, Bhagavata Purana, you will get the Vedic message explained in a very easily understandable way for the common people because so many stories are there, so many historical narrations are there. But in addition to these Itihasis and Puranas Vyasadeva was also not very happy or satisfied that he has done enough welfare activity. A Rishi or a Sadhu his life is dedicated for the welfare of the people in general.

---
### Atmarama, Self-Realization, Compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2232
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "While they are themselves satisfied within themselves and they are not dependent externally on anything or any person for their satisfaction they also out of compassion for the others who are not able to actually be self-satisfied, for the welfare of the others they keep working."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the life of a Rishi or Sadhu, explaining that they are self-satisfied (Atmarama) and not dependent on external things for happiness, yet they work out of compassion for others who are not self-satisfied, dedicating their lives to the welfare of others.

Because these Rishis or Sadhus as far as they are concerned because of their spiritual realization they are satisfied and happy and contented within themselves. They are Atmarama. Atmarama means one who can be self-satisfied does not require anything outside for his satisfaction. It is like all of us we require so many things externally for our satisfaction or for our happiness. Right? We are dependent on so many external things. Whereas a self-realized soul or an Atmarama a person who is Atmarama is satisfied within himself he does not require anything external for his satisfaction or happiness or contentment. So what is the life of such Atmaramas? What would they do if they are self-satisfied? They have nothing to do for the sake of their satisfaction or happiness or contentment. They have nothing to do. So does it mean that they don't do anything? No. While they are themselves satisfied within themselves and they are not dependent externally on anything or any person for their satisfaction they also out of compassion for the others who are not able to actually be self-satisfied. For the welfare of the others they keep working. There are so many activities. There are so many different things they do. So a Sadhu or a Rishi is one who lives for the welfare of the others. So Vyasa Deva is a Rishi is a Sadhu whose life is only dedicated for the welfare of others.

---
### Bhagavatam, Krishna, Narada Muni
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2370
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Narada Muni pointed out to Vyasa Deva that even though you have done so much of work even though you have compiled all the Vedas you have done all that you have done still and that's very big work it's not small work very big work still Vyasa Deva was experiencing dissatisfaction."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Vyasa Deva, despite his extensive welfare activities, felt unsatisfied and incomplete, a feeling that led him to compile the Bhagavata Purana. This thought came from God, and Narada Muni pointed out that Vyasa Deva's dissatisfaction stemmed from not properly glorifying Krishna in his Vedic literature.

So how did he decide to do welfare activity for others? He put down the Vedas in writing. He compiled the Vedas. He did divided the Vedas into four parts. He summarized the Vedas. Further summarized and also wrote down Itihasas and Puranas. But in spite of doing all this he was not satisfied. He thought still is there something more I can do? This thought Vyasa Deva got from God before he wrote this last work or compiled the last book the Bhagavata Purana. Before the Bhagavatam was compiled Vyasa Deva felt in his heart some vacuum some insufficiency some incompleteness. So when he felt that he thought that even though I have done so much of work for the welfare of the people I am still not satisfied that I have done enough. Something is lacking something is missing in what I have done. What is that I have not done? That was pointed out to him by his Guru Narada Muni. Narada Muni pointed out to Vyasa Deva that even though you have done so much of work even though you have compiled all the Vedas you have done all that you have done still and that's very big work it's not small work very big work still Vyasa Deva was experiencing dissatisfaction and Narada Muni noticed that and he told Vyasa Deva you are not satisfied or you are feeling insufficient because you are not actually properly glorified Krishna in all your Vedic literature. In all the literature you have not properly or sufficiently glorified Krishna.

---
### Bhagavatam, Krishna, Paramahamsa
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2515
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagavata is that Vedic literature which exclusively glorifies Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Narada Muni directed Vyasa Deva to write the Bhagavatam, the final work in which Krishna is exclusively glorified, also known as Krishna Samhita and Paramahamsa Samhita, best appreciated by Paramahamsas who have reached perfection in spiritual realization.

So Narada Muni directed Vyasa Deva to write the Bhagavatam this final contribution of Vyasa Deva the final work of Vyasa Deva the last work of Vyasa Deva in which Krishna is exclusively glorified. So Bhagavata is also called Krishna Samhita Samhita generally means Vedic literature. So Bhagavata is that Vedic literature which exclusively glorifies Krishna. Also Bhagavata is called Paramahamsa Samhita. It is the very literature which can be best appreciated by Paramahamsas. Who are Paramahamsas? Paramahamsas are the topmost transcendentalists who have reached the perfection of spiritual realization by being able to distinguish spirit from matter. There are two things in this world. There is matter that we all know and there is something which normally people do not know and that is called spirit. So one should know in addition to what is matter one should also know what is spirit.

---
### Spirit, Matter, Paramahamsa
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2611
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Spirit is important matter is not important."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of distinguishing spirit from matter, stating that spirit is the essence and of value, while matter is not essential. He explains that most people lead material lives centered around matter, including their bodies, which they mistakenly identify as their self.

And one should be able to distinguish or separate spirit from matter. Spirit is important matter is not important. Spirit is the essence which is of value to us and matter is not essential not of value to us. Our life generally is simply centered around matter. We are leading what is called as a material life concerned only with matter. Including our body which we think is our self this body also is made up of matter. And everything around us we see we deal with is made up of matter. So this matter is not the essence is not important is not essential. So spirit is important. And one who is able to distinguish spirit from matter and not only do that but also extract effectively the spiritual essence of everything in this world. Like whatever we see in existence including this body which we think is our self how to extract the spiritual essence from everything material. That is only known to a Paramahamsa. The real topmost transcendentalist. He knows why in this world how to extract the spiritual essence of everything. There is a mixture of matter and spirit. So from this mixture just like Hamsa why they are called Paramahamsa? Another reason is Hamsa swam is a very very special bird.

---
### Hamsa, Paramahamsa, Spiritual Essence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2730
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Similarly a Paramahamsa is a person who can extract the spiritual essence from a mixture of spirit and matter."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of the Hamsa bird, which can separate milk from water, to explain how a Paramahamsa can extract the spiritual essence from a mixture of spirit and matter, emphasizing that they can not only separate spirit from matter but also take the spiritual essence from the mixture.

What is the special quality of the Hamsa? It can separate milk from a mixture of milk and water. Just like generally the milkman when he brings milk to your house he will generally if you take from a person who milks a cow generally they add milk to the water and give you. Water mixed with milk. Now if you present that water mixed with milk to a swam it will definitely drink that milk but it will only drink the milk portion. It will leave behind water. The swam has got this special quality. Means it is able to take the essence of the mixture of the milk and water. What is the essence? The milk. So it is able to take the nectar the amrita and leave behind the water. Similarly a Paramahamsa is a person who can extract the spiritual essence from a mixture of spirit and matter. So not only he can separate spirit from matter but he can also take the spiritual essence from a mixture of spirit and matter. So such a Paramahamsa specially for a Paramahamsa Vyasa Deva has compiled this Sriman Bhagavatam.

### Bhagavatam, Vedanta Sutra, Vedic Literature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2826
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore the Bhagavatam is so important in the literature. It is the ultimate Vedic book book of the Vedas book of the Vedic knowledge because it contains the cream of the cream of the Vedas."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Bhagavatam is also the commentary or explanation of the Vedanta Sutra by the author himself, making it a crucial Vedic text because it contains the essence of the Vedas and is presented as a Purana with historical narrations, unlike the dry and story-less Vedanta Sutra.

So therefore this Bhagavatam is also called Paramahamsa Samhita. It is also the literature which is the commentary or the explanation of the Vedanta Sutra by the author himself. As I said Vedanta Sutra is the cream of the cream. So it is very difficult to understand. For most people this Vedanta Sutra is difficult to understand. And even those who try to study the Vedanta Sutra after studying Sanskrit after studying the other Vedic literature they may misunderstand the meaning of the Sutras. There is a possibility that somebody even if they study in a scholarly way the Vedic literature and try to understand the meaning of the Vedanta Sutra they may misunderstand the meaning. In order to explain correctly the meaning of the Vedanta Sutra the author who has compiled the Vedanta Sutra himself has explained the Vedanta Sutra in the form of Sriman Bhagavatam. Therefore the Bhagavatam is so important in the literature. It is the ultimate Vedic book book of the Vedas book of the Vedic knowledge because it contains the cream of the cream of the Vedas and it also is presented in the form of a Purana Purana means historical narration so there are many Vedic books that contain the cream of the Vedanta Sutra so there are nice historical incidents described which make it very interesting. In the Vedanta Sutra there is no historical narration so it is very dry no stories no historical incidents it is very dry.

### Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, Vedic Knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/DHqk0rATFL4?t=2986
**Verse References:** BG 18.66
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If you study the Bhagavad Gita and you study the Bhagavatam you know the Vedas entirely what they are actually communicating."
**Summary:** The speaker notes that the Bhagavatam is not recommended as the first book to study, suggesting the Bhagavad Gita as a preliminary study. He highlights the Bhagavad Gita's special position as the essence of all Upanishads, presented as a conversation between Krishna and Arjuna, and emphasizes that studying both the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam provides complete Vedic knowledge.

So this Bhagavatam which explains the Vedanta Sutra is presented in a very unique format of a Purana. Also it explains the Vedanta Sutra which may be misunderstood by even some great scholars. So the author himself is explaining the Vedanta Sutra what is the meaning of the sutras in this Bhagavatam. Therefore this Bhagavatam is the most important Vedic literature. But the Bhagavatam is also not recommended to be studied directly or as the first book because it explains the Vedanta Sutra it is not again to be studied as the first book. So preliminary study of the Bhagavatam it is recommended to study the Bhagavad Gita. It is very impressive literature. What is the special position of the Bhagavad Gita? Just like Bhagavatam has got a very special position from all various literatures the Bhagavad Gita also has got a very special position. What is that? The Bhagavad Gita is the essence of all the Upanishads. So just like the Vedanta Sutra is the essence of all the Upanishads the Bhagavad Gita also is an essence of all the Upanishads. And Bhagavad Gita is presented as a conversation or a dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna and it is also the essence of all the Upanishads. The Bhagavad Gita also is a very important Vedic literature. So the study of Bhagavad Gita as the preliminary study of the Bhagavatam and the actual study of the Bhagavatam itself are the Vedas these two books which are the Bhagavad Gita and Bhagavatam. If you study the Bhagavad Gita and you study the Bhagavatam you know the Vedas entirely what they are actually communicating. The entire Vedic message Vedic philosophy is there as a part of the Mahabharata.

### impersonalism, personalism, creator vs. created
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6az3lopn0ho?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For realization of oneself in spiritual psychology, it may be convenient to assume oneself to be the same principle as the absolute truth, but there is always the difference of the predominated and the predominator."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the concept of the difference between the predominated and the predominator, refuting the impersonalists' view and establishing the personal nature of the Absolute Truth.

And that he is a person. The predominated I, Brahma, is also a person, but he is not absolute. For realization of oneself in spiritual psychology, it may be convenient to assume oneself to be the same principle as the absolute truth, but there is always the difference of the predominated and the predominator, as clearly pointed out here in this verse, which is grossly misused by the impersonalists. So that's the third parā-lāha purport.

### Bhagavatam, personality of Godhead, transcendental
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6az3lopn0ho?t=174
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker explains that the Bhagavatam elaborately explains the truth about Krishna as the Supreme Person, particularly in the Tushloki Bhagavata, and emphasizes that the personality of Godhead is not an ordinary person but a completely transcendental one.

So as I was explaining yesterday, the truth about Krishna or the personality of God or Supreme person is being explained in the Bhagavatam very elaborately and particularly in these four verses called the Tushloki Bhagavata, this particular fact is explained that the Absolute Truth is the Supreme Person. Now, Prabhupada is trying to explain in the purport that this personality of Godhead in front of Brahma is not an ordinary person like you and me. He is a completely transcendental personality. And what is the reason to say he is a transcendental person? He is not a person like me. He is a creative person. So the arguments or the explanations are supported in so many ways.
